cases.title: Fever and a Productive Cough
cases.chiefComplaint: Three days of fever, productive cough, and right-sided chest pain that worsens with deep breaths.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents with three days of progressive fever, chills, and a productive cough bringing up thick rust-colored sputum. He reports a sharp right-sided pleuritic chest pain and increasing shortness of breath with exertion. He denies hemoptysis, weight loss, or night sweats. He had a mild upper-respiratory illness the week prior. Past medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily, and metformin 1000 mg twice daily. He has no known drug allergies. He is a former smoker with a 25-pack-year history who quit 10 years ago. He drinks alcohol socially and lives independently with his wife. He received the influenza vaccine this season but is unsure of his pneumococcal vaccination status. Family history is notable for coronary artery disease in his father. He has no recent hospitalizations, no recent antibiotic exposure, and no travel. Review of systems is positive for fatigue and decreased appetite but negative for vomiting, dysuria, or leg swelling.
cases.exam: The patient appears mildly ill and tachypneic but is alert and fully oriented. He answers questions in full sentences. Examination of the right lower posterior chest reveals dullness to percussion, increased tactile fremitus, bronchial breath sounds, and late inspiratory crackles. Egophony is present over the right base. The left lung is clear. Cardiac exam reveals a regular tachycardia without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. There is no jugular venous distention and no peripheral edema. The abdomen is soft and nontender. There is no calf tenderness or asymmetric leg swelling. Skin is warm and well perfused with no rashes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which finding on examination most specifically supports a diagnosis of lobar consolidation rather than another cause of dyspnea?
options: Bilateral diffuse wheezing on expiration
Increased tactile fremitus with egophony and bronchial breath sounds over the right base
Jugular venous distention with an S3 gallop
Hyperresonance to percussion with decreased breath sounds
stages.explanation: Increased tactile fremitus, bronchial breath sounds, dullness to percussion, and egophony together indicate fluid-filled, consolidated lung tissue that transmits sound better than air-filled lung — the classic signature of lobar pneumonia. Bilateral wheezing suggests bronchospasm (asthma/COPD) rather than consolidation. JVD with an S3 points to heart failure and pulmonary edema, which is typically bilateral and not associated with increased fremitus. Hyperresonance with decreased breath sounds indicates trapped air or a pneumothorax, the physiologic opposite of consolidation. In this febrile patient with rust-colored sputum and focal unilateral findings, the consolidation pattern strongly localizes a bacterial lobar pneumonia, classically pneumococcal.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 15.8 ×10³/µL (88% neutrophils)
Hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL
Na 137 mEq/L
BUN 26 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Glucose 188 mg/dL
Lactate 1.6 mmol/L
Chest X-ray: dense right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms; no pleural effusion or pneumothorax

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the CURB-65 score, which single component is unambiguously met by this patient based on his demographics alone?
options: Confusion
Respiratory rate greater than or equal to 30/min
Systolic BP less than 90 mmHg
Age greater than or equal to 65 years
stages.explanation: CURB-65 awards one point each for Confusion, Urea (BUN > 19 mg/dL), Respiratory rate ≥ 30, Blood pressure (SBP < 90 or DBP ≤ 60), and age ≥ 65. This patient is 68, so age scores a point. He is alert and oriented, so confusion does not score. His respiratory rate is 24, below the 30 threshold, so it does NOT score — a common trap because tachypnea is present but does not reach the cutoff. His systolic BP is 128, well above 90, so that does not score. (Note his BUN of 26 also scores separately, but the demographic-only certainty is age ≥ 65.) Age ≥ 65 is the unambiguous demographic criterion met here.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His total CURB-65 score is 2 (age ≥ 65 and BUN 26). What is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Discharge home on oral amoxicillin with next-day follow-up
Admit to a general medical ward for inpatient treatment
Admit directly to the intensive care unit for vasopressors
Observe for 2 hours and discharge if the fever resolves
stages.explanation: A CURB-65 of 2 carries an intermediate 30-day mortality (roughly 9%) and warrants short-stay inpatient admission rather than outpatient management. Discharging home is reserved for scores of 0–1 in reliable patients with adequate oxygenation; this man scores 2 and has an SpO2 of 91%, so discharge is unsafe. ICU admission is driven by scores of 4–5, septic shock, or need for mechanical ventilation/vasopressors — none of which he has (lactate normal, BP stable). A brief observe-and-discharge strategy ignores his hypoxemia and intermediate risk. Ward admission allows IV antibiotics, supplemental oxygen, and monitoring for deterioration.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this admitted patient with no MRSA or Pseudomonas risk factors, which empiric antibiotic regimen is most appropriate?
options: Oral azithromycin monotherapy
IV vancomycin plus cefepime
IV ceftriaxone plus azithromycin (or respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy)
Oral doxycycline alone
stages.explanation: For non-ICU inpatients with community-acquired pneumonia, IDSA/ATS guidelines recommend a beta-lactam (e.g., ceftriaxone) plus a macrolide (azithromycin), or respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin/moxifloxacin) monotherapy. This covers Streptococcus pneumoniae and atypicals. Azithromycin alone is inadequate inpatient therapy given rising pneumococcal macrolide resistance. Vancomycin plus cefepime adds broad MRSA and Pseudomonas coverage that is unwarranted without specific risk factors (recent IV antibiotics, prior resistant isolates, structural lung disease) and promotes resistance. Doxycycline monotherapy is an outpatient option for low-risk patients, not appropriate for an admitted hypoxemic patient. Combination beta-lactam/macrolide therapy is the standard ward regimen here.

cases.diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia (right lower lobe, pneumococcal), CURB-65 score 2
pearls: Rust-colored sputum with focal consolidation findings classically points to Streptococcus pneumoniae.
CURB-65 ≥ 2 generally warrants inpatient admission; 0–1 suggests outpatient candidacy and 4–5 prompts ICU evaluation.
Increased tactile fremitus, egophony, and bronchial breath sounds distinguish consolidation from effusion or pneumothorax.
Standard ward CAP therapy is a beta-lactam plus a macrolide, or respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy.

cases.title: Worsening Breathlessness in a Longtime Smoker
cases.chiefComplaint: Four days of increasing shortness of breath, wheezing, and a change to thick yellow-green sputum.
cases.history: A 64-year-old woman presents with four days of progressively worsening shortness of breath, audible wheezing, and increased cough productive of thick yellow-green sputum. Over the last day she has become breathless walking from one room to another, whereas she normally manages a full flight of stairs. She denies fever, chest pain, hemoptysis, orthopnea, or leg swelling. She has known COPD (GOLD group B) and uses tiotropium daily plus an albuterol inhaler as needed, which she has been using every two hours without relief. Other history includes hypertension treated with hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily. She has no drug allergies. She is an active smoker with a 45-pack-year history. She lives alone, is independent in daily activities, and has had two COPD exacerbations in the past year, neither requiring intubation. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for sick contacts, recent travel, calf pain, or palpitations, and she reports no recent change in her chronic baseline sputum color until this episode.
cases.exam: The patient is sitting upright, using accessory muscles, and speaking in short phrases. She is alert and oriented without confusion or somnolence. The chest demonstrates a prolonged expiratory phase with diffuse expiratory wheezes and diminished air movement bilaterally; there are no focal crackles or areas of dullness. There is mild pursed-lip breathing. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia with no murmurs or gallops; jugular venous pressure is normal. There is no peripheral edema and no calf tenderness. There is no cyanosis, and capillary refill is brisk. The abdomen is soft and nontender. She has no asterixis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first pharmacologic intervention for this acute exacerbation?
options: Inhaled short-acting bronchodilators (albuterol with ipratropium)
Intravenous magnesium sulfate
Empiric intravenous theophylline
Inhaled corticosteroid started immediately
stages.explanation: Inhaled short-acting bronchodilators — a short-acting beta-agonist (albuterol) combined with a short-acting muscarinic antagonist (ipratropium) — are the cornerstone first-line therapy for an acute COPD exacerbation and relieve bronchospasm quickly. IV magnesium has a limited adjunctive role in severe asthma and is not first-line in COPD. Theophylline has a narrow therapeutic index, significant toxicity, and is not recommended as initial therapy. Inhaled corticosteroids are maintenance medications for chronic management and have no role in the acute rescue of an exacerbation; systemic (oral or IV) corticosteroids — not inhaled — are what is added systemically. Rapid bronchodilation addresses her airflow obstruction immediately while other therapies are layered on.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond bronchodilators, which combination of therapies is indicated given her purulent sputum and increased dyspnea?
options: Systemic corticosteroids alone, no antibiotics
Antibiotics alone, no corticosteroids
Both systemic corticosteroids and antibiotics
Neither; reserve both for ICU-level illness
stages.explanation: She meets criteria for both therapies. Systemic corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone 40 mg daily for 5 days) shorten recovery, improve lung function, and reduce relapse in COPD exacerbations and are indicated in essentially all moderate-to-severe exacerbations. Antibiotics are indicated when at least two of the three Anthonisen cardinal symptoms are present (increased dyspnea, increased sputum volume, increased sputum purulence) or when mechanical ventilation is needed. She has increased dyspnea AND new purulence, satisfying the threshold. Giving steroids alone ignores the bacterial trigger her purulent sputum suggests; antibiotics alone forgoes the proven anti-inflammatory benefit of steroids. Withholding both is inappropriate for a moderate-to-severe exacerbation. The correct approach combines both.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ABG (room air): pH 7.31, PaCO2 56 mmHg, PaO2 58 mmHg, HCO3 27 mEq/L
WBC 11.4 ×10³/µL
Chest X-ray: hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms; no consolidation or pneumothorax
ECG: sinus tachycardia, no ischemic changes

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Her ABG shows an acute respiratory acidosis (pH 7.31, PaCO2 56). She remains dyspneic and alert. What is the most appropriate next intervention?
options: Immediate endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation
Noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation (BiPAP)
High-flow 100% oxygen by nonrebreather to target SpO2 100%
IV opioids to reduce her sense of dyspnea
stages.explanation: Noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation (BiPAP) is strongly indicated for COPD exacerbations with acute respiratory acidosis (pH < 7.35 with hypercapnia) in an alert, cooperative patient; it reduces work of breathing, improves gas exchange, and lowers intubation and mortality rates. Immediate intubation is reserved for failure of NIPPV, depressed consciousness, inability to protect the airway, or hemodynamic instability — none present here. Targeting an SpO2 of 100% with high-flow oxygen risks worsening hypercapnia through loss of hypoxic drive and the Haldane effect; the goal is a controlled SpO2 of 88–92%. Opioids would suppress her respiratory drive and worsen hypercapnia. BiPAP is the evidence-based next step for this acidotic but alert patient.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the appropriate target oxygen saturation range while titrating supplemental oxygen in this patient?
options: Greater than 85% with no upper limit
stages.explanation: In patients with COPD at risk for chronic CO2 retention, the recommended target oxygen saturation is 88–92%. This provides adequate tissue oxygenation while avoiding hyperoxia-induced hypercapnia, which can result from blunting of hypoxic respiratory drive, worsening ventilation-perfusion matching, and the Haldane effect. A target of 94–98% is appropriate for most acutely ill patients WITHOUT CO2 retention risk but is too high here. Aiming for 99–100% is unnecessary in any acute patient and is specifically hazardous in COPD. 'Greater than 85% with no upper limit' invites dangerous hyperoxia. Careful titration to 88–92%, ideally guided by repeat ABG, balances oxygenation against the risk of worsening respiratory acidosis.

cases.diagnosis: Acute exacerbation of COPD with hypercapnic respiratory acidosis
pearls: Antibiotics in COPD exacerbation are indicated with 2 of 3 Anthonisen criteria (dyspnea, sputum volume, sputum purulence) or if ventilation is required.
Short systemic steroid courses (prednisone 40 mg x 5 days) speed recovery and reduce relapse.
BiPAP reduces intubation and mortality in COPD exacerbations with pH < 7.35 and hypercapnia in alert patients.
Target SpO2 88–92% in COPD to avoid oxygen-induced hypercapnia.

cases.title: Breathless and Swollen After the Holidays
cases.chiefComplaint: One week of worsening shortness of breath, leg swelling, and difficulty lying flat.
cases.history: A 72-year-old man is admitted with one week of progressive dyspnea on exertion, bilateral leg swelling, and orthopnea now requiring three pillows to sleep. He awakens at night gasping for air. He admits to indulging in heavily salted foods over a recent holiday and missing several doses of his diuretic. He has known ischemic cardiomyopathy with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 30%, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease stage 3. Medications include lisinopril 10 mg daily, carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily, furosemide 40 mg daily, and aspirin 81 mg daily. He has no drug allergies. He is a former smoker who quit 15 years ago and drinks minimal alcohol. He lives with his daughter. He denies chest pain, fever, cough, or palpitations. Family history includes coronary artery disease. He has gained roughly 4 kilograms over the past week. Review of systems is positive for early satiety and mild abdominal fullness but negative for syncope, hemoptysis, or calf pain.
cases.exam: The patient is mildly dyspneic at rest and prefers sitting upright. The jugular venous pressure is elevated at approximately 12 cm of water. Lung auscultation reveals bilateral inspiratory crackles to the mid-lung fields. Cardiac exam discloses a laterally displaced apical impulse, a regular rhythm, and an audible S3 gallop; there is no murmur of acute valvular failure. The abdomen is soft with mild hepatomegaly and a positive hepatojugular reflux. There is 2+ pitting edema to the mid-shins bilaterally. Extremities are warm with intact distal pulses. There is no calf tenderness or cord, and no focal neurologic deficit.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings is most specific for elevated left- and right-sided filling pressures from decompensated heart failure?
options: Fever, productive cough, and focal crackles
Elevated JVP, an S3 gallop, bilateral crackles, and peripheral edema
Unilateral leg swelling with calf tenderness
Hyperresonant lungs with prolonged expiration
stages.explanation: Elevated jugular venous pressure, an S3 gallop, bilateral pulmonary crackles, and peripheral edema together reflect both elevated left-sided pressures (crackles, S3) and right-sided congestion (JVP, edema, hepatomegaly) — the hallmark of decompensated heart failure. An S3 is the single most specific bedside sign of elevated filling pressure and ventricular volume overload. Fever with focal crackles suggests pneumonia. Unilateral leg swelling with calf tenderness points toward deep venous thrombosis, not the bilateral, symmetric congestion seen here. Hyperresonant lungs with prolonged expiration indicate obstructive lung disease. The combined left- and right-sided congestion, weight gain, orthopnea, and dietary/medication noncompliance make decompensated HFrEF the unifying diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
BNP 1480 pg/mL
Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (baseline 1.4)
Na 136 mEq/L
K 4.2 mEq/L
Troponin: negative x2
Chest X-ray: cardiomegaly, pulmonary vascular congestion, small bilateral pleural effusions
ECG: old inferior Q waves, no acute ST changes

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial therapy to relieve his congestion?
options: Initiate a nondihydropyridine calcium channel blocker
Intravenous normal saline bolus
Intravenous loop diuretic (furosemide)
Start intravenous beta-blocker for rate control
stages.explanation: Intravenous loop diuretic therapy (furosemide) is the cornerstone of treating the volume overload of acute decompensated heart failure; it relieves pulmonary and systemic congestion. Patients on chronic oral diuretics usually require an IV dose at least equal to or greater than their home oral dose to overcome diminished gut absorption from bowel-wall edema. IV saline would worsen his volume overload and is contraindicated. Nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem) are negative inotropes and are harmful in reduced-EF heart failure. IV beta-blockade during acute decompensation can precipitate cardiogenic shock and is avoided until the patient is euvolemic and stable. Diuresis with IV furosemide directly addresses his congestion.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After successful diuresis he is near euvolemic with stable renal function. Regarding his guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT), what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Discontinue his beta-blocker permanently because it caused the decompensation
Continue and optimize GDMT, adding an aldosterone antagonist and considering an ARNI and SGLT2 inhibitor
Stop the ACE inhibitor and replace it with a calcium channel blocker
Avoid any new agents until the next outpatient visit in 3 months
stages.explanation: Once euvolemic and stable, the goal is to optimize the four pillars of GDMT for HFrEF: a beta-blocker, a renin-angiotensin inhibitor (ideally transitioning an ACE inhibitor/ARB to an ARNI such as sacubitril-valsartan), a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (spironolactone or eplerenone), and an SGLT2 inhibitor — all of which reduce mortality and rehospitalization. His beta-blocker should be continued, not stopped; the decompensation was driven by dietary salt and diuretic nonadherence, not the beta-blocker, and abrupt withdrawal worsens outcomes. Replacing the ACE inhibitor with a calcium channel blocker removes a mortality-reducing therapy for a non-beneficial one. Deferring all optimization for three months wastes the hospitalization, a key opportunity to titrate evidence-based therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which intervention has the greatest impact on preventing recurrent admissions for this patient at discharge?
options: A prescription for as-needed supplemental oxygen at home
Routine serial echocardiograms every two weeks
Long-term prophylactic oral antibiotics
Patient education on daily weights, dietary sodium restriction, and medication adherence with early follow-up
stages.explanation: Structured discharge education — daily weight monitoring, sodium and fluid restriction, medication adherence, and early (within 7–14 days) follow-up — markedly reduces heart failure readmissions, because most decompensations stem from dietary indiscretion and nonadherence, exactly this patient's triggers. Home oxygen is unnecessary once he is euvolemic with adequate saturations and does not address the cause of his admission. Echocardiograms every two weeks provide no actionable benefit and waste resources; reassessment is periodic, not biweekly. Prophylactic antibiotics have no role in heart failure and promote resistance and adverse effects. Self-management education and close follow-up directly target the modifiable behaviors that precipitated his decompensation.

cases.diagnosis: Acute decompensated heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF)
pearls: An S3 gallop is the most specific bedside sign of elevated ventricular filling pressure in heart failure.
IV loop diuretics at a dose equal to or greater than the home oral dose are first-line for acute congestion.
The four pillars of HFrEF GDMT are beta-blocker, ARNI/ACEi/ARB, MRA, and SGLT2 inhibitor — optimize before discharge.
Daily weights, sodium restriction, adherence counseling, and early follow-up are the highest-yield readmission-prevention tools.

cases.title: A Routine Diabetes Check-Up
cases.chiefComplaint: Routine follow-up for type 2 diabetes with no acute complaints.
cases.history: A 54-year-old woman presents for a routine follow-up of type 2 diabetes diagnosed six years ago. She feels generally well and denies polyuria, polydipsia, blurred vision, numbness, or non-healing wounds. She admits her diet has been inconsistent and she rarely exercises. Her diabetes is currently managed with metformin 1000 mg twice daily, which she takes reliably. Other history includes hypertension treated with lisinopril 20 mg daily and dyslipidemia treated with atorvastatin 40 mg daily. She has no drug allergies. She has never smoked, drinks wine occasionally, and works as an accountant. Her body mass index is 33 kg/m². Family history includes type 2 diabetes in both parents and a father who had a myocardial infarction at age 60. Home glucose logs show fasting values in the 160–190 mg/dL range. Review of systems is otherwise negative. Her most recent laboratory work, drawn one week ago, is reviewed at this visit, and her foot exam and dilated eye exam are up to date.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm without murmurs. Lungs are clear bilaterally. The abdomen is obese, soft, and nontender without organomegaly. Examination of the feet shows intact skin without ulceration or deformity; dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+ bilaterally; monofilament testing demonstrates intact protective sensation, and vibratory sensation is preserved. There is no peripheral edema. The thyroid is not enlarged. The remainder of the examination, including a focused neurologic screen, is unremarkable.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin A1c 9.2%
Fasting glucose 178 mg/dL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m²
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio 45 mg/g
LDL cholesterol 88 mg/dL
Potassium 4.1 mEq/L

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her A1c is 9.2% on maximally tolerated metformin. What is the most appropriate next step in glycemic management?
options: Continue metformin alone and recheck the A1c in 6 months
Discontinue metformin and rely on lifestyle modification alone
Intensify therapy by adding a second agent now
Add a sulfonylurea as the preferred second agent for cardiorenal protection
stages.explanation: Her A1c of 9.2% is far above her individualized target (generally < 7% for most nonpregnant adults), so therapy must be intensified now by adding a second agent rather than waiting. Continuing metformin alone for another six months leaves her substantially uncontrolled and accelerates microvascular and macrovascular risk. Stopping metformin in favor of lifestyle alone abandons effective first-line therapy and is inappropriate at this degree of hyperglycemia. A sulfonylurea is an inexpensive add-on but carries hypoglycemia and weight-gain risk and confers no cardiovascular or renal benefit, so it is not the preferred choice for a patient with albuminuria and high cardiovascular risk. The correct step is timely intensification with an agent chosen for her comorbidities.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given her albuminuria (ACR 45 mg/g), hypertension, and elevated cardiovascular risk, which add-on agent is most appropriate?
options: An SGLT2 inhibitor
Basal insulin glargine
A dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitor
A thiazolidinedione (pioglitazone)
stages.explanation: An SGLT2 inhibitor is the preferred add-on for a patient with type 2 diabetes plus albuminuric chronic kidney disease and high cardiovascular risk: this class slows CKD progression, reduces albuminuria, lowers heart failure hospitalizations, and reduces cardiovascular and all-cause mortality, independent of glucose lowering. (A GLP-1 receptor agonist would be a comparable evidence-based choice, especially with established atherosclerotic disease or for greater weight loss.) Basal insulin lowers glucose effectively but offers no cardiorenal protection and carries hypoglycemia and weight gain. DPP-4 inhibitors are weight-neutral and glucose-lowering but cardiorenal-neutral. Pioglitazone can worsen fluid retention and is relatively contraindicated with heart failure risk. The SGLT2 inhibitor uniquely matches her comorbidity profile.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She is started on an SGLT2 inhibitor. Which counseling point is most important to provide?
options: Expect significant weight gain and increased appetite
Hold the drug during acute illness with poor oral intake (sick-day rules) and watch for genital yeast infections
Check fingerstick glucose hourly because of severe hypoglycemia risk
The medication must be taken only at bedtime with a fatty meal
stages.explanation: Key counseling for SGLT2 inhibitors centers on the sick-day rule: hold the drug during acute illness, dehydration, or markedly reduced oral intake because of the risk of euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis. Patients should also be warned about genital mycotic infections and maintain good perineal hygiene, plus the risk of volume depletion. SGLT2 inhibitors promote modest weight LOSS, not gain, through urinary glucose excretion, so the first option is wrong. As monotherapy add-ons they carry low intrinsic hypoglycemia risk, so hourly fingersticks are unnecessary and the third option is incorrect. There is no requirement to dose at bedtime with a fatty meal. Sick-day guidance and infection awareness are the highest-yield safety counseling points.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Her blood pressure today is 134/82 and her LDL is 88 mg/dL on atorvastatin. What additional cardiovascular risk-reduction step is most appropriate?
options: Add a second antihypertensive to target a BP below 110/70
Reinforce her current ACE inhibitor and statin, confirming adherence, with continued lifestyle counseling
Discontinue the statin since her LDL is under 100
Start aspirin for primary prevention in all patients with diabetes regardless of risk
stages.explanation: Her ACE inhibitor is well chosen given albuminuria and hypertension, and her statin is appropriate for a diabetic patient with high cardiovascular risk; the best step is to confirm adherence and reinforce both alongside lifestyle measures. A blood pressure target below 110/70 is overly aggressive and risks hypotension; most guidelines target roughly < 130/80. Stopping the statin because the LDL is under 100 is incorrect — statin therapy in diabetes is driven by overall risk, not a single threshold, and she benefits from continued moderate-to-high-intensity therapy. Routine aspirin for primary prevention is no longer recommended for all diabetics because bleeding risk often offsets benefit; it is individualized. Reinforcing guideline-concordant ACE inhibitor and statin therapy is the highest-value action.

cases.diagnosis: Uncontrolled type 2 diabetes mellitus with albuminuric chronic kidney disease
pearls: An A1c well above target on metformin warrants prompt addition of a second agent, not delayed reassessment.
SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists are preferred add-ons in diabetes with CKD, ASCVD, or heart failure for their cardiorenal benefits.
Counsel SGLT2 inhibitor users on sick-day rules (risk of euglycemic DKA) and genital mycotic infections.
Statin and ACE-inhibitor therapy in diabetic patients are driven by overall cardiovascular and renal risk, not single lab thresholds.

cases.title: Vomiting and Rapid Breathing in a Young Man
cases.chiefComplaint: One day of nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and rapid deep breathing.
cases.history: A 23-year-old man with type 1 diabetes mellitus presents with one day of nausea, repeated vomiting, diffuse abdominal pain, and increasing fatigue. He reports that he ran out of his insulin three days ago and has not taken any since. Over the past day he has noticed increased thirst, frequent urination, and now rapid breathing. He denies fever, cough, dysuria, or diarrhea, though he had nasal congestion earlier in the week. His only medication is a basal-bolus insulin regimen, which he has not been taking. He has no drug allergies. He is a college student, drinks alcohol occasionally on weekends, and denies recreational drug use. Family history includes autoimmune thyroid disease in his mother. He has had one prior episode of diabetic ketoacidosis at the time of his diagnosis four years ago. Review of systems is positive for blurry vision and generalized weakness but negative for chest pain, focal neurologic symptoms, or recent trauma.
cases.exam: The patient appears ill, fatigued, and mildly drowsy but is arousable and oriented. His mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is decreased, indicating dehydration. Respirations are deep and labored (Kussmaul pattern), and there is a fruity odor to his breath. Cardiac exam reveals a regular tachycardia without murmurs. The abdomen is diffusely tender without rebound or guarding, and bowel sounds are present. There is no focal neurologic deficit, neck stiffness, or photophobia. Capillary refill is mildly delayed at approximately 3 seconds. There are no rashes or sites of obvious infection.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Glucose 542 mg/dL
Na 132 mEq/L
K 5.3 mEq/L
Cl 99 mEq/L
HCO3 9 mEq/L
BUN 28 mg/dL, Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
Arterial pH 7.18, PaCO2 22 mmHg
Serum beta-hydroxybutyrate 6.2 mmol/L
Urinalysis: glucose 4+, ketones large, no nitrites or leukocyte esterase

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the labs, what is the calculated anion gap and what does it confirm?
options: Gap of 8, confirming a normal-anion-gap acidosis
Gap of 24, confirming a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis consistent with DKA
Gap of 12, indicating a purely respiratory process
Gap of 6, indicating laboratory error
stages.explanation: The anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3) = 132 − (99 + 9) = 24, which is markedly elevated (normal ≈ 8–12). Combined with the arterial pH of 7.18, bicarbonate of 9, glucose of 542, and serum beta-hydroxybutyrate of 6.2 mmol/L, this confirms a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis from diabetic ketoacidosis. The accumulating ketoacids (beta-hydroxybutyrate, acetoacetate) are the unmeasured anions widening the gap. A gap of 8 (normal) would not explain his acidosis. A gap of 12 attributed to a 'purely respiratory' process is wrong — his low PaCO2 is appropriate respiratory compensation (Kussmaul breathing) for a primary metabolic acidosis, not the primary problem. A gap of 6 suggesting lab error ignores the internally consistent ketoacidosis picture.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step in management of his DKA?
options: Rapid intravenous isotonic fluid resuscitation (normal saline)
Immediate IV bolus of regular insulin before any fluids
Administer IV sodium bicarbonate to correct the pH
Give an oral potassium supplement and observe
stages.explanation: The first priority in DKA is aggressive intravenous isotonic fluid resuscitation (e.g., 0.9% normal saline), because patients have a profound osmotic-diuresis-driven fluid deficit (often 5–10 liters). Fluids restore intravascular volume, improve perfusion and renal clearance of glucose and ketones, and begin lowering glucose even before insulin. Starting insulin before fluids and before checking/repleting potassium is dangerous: insulin drives potassium intracellularly and can precipitate life-threatening hypokalemia, and giving insulin to a hypovolemic patient can worsen hypotension. Routine bicarbonate is not recommended unless pH is below approximately 6.9, as it can cause paradoxical CNS acidosis and hypokalemia. Oral potassium with mere observation ignores his critical fluid deficit and acidosis. Fluids first is the cornerstone.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His initial potassium is 5.3 mEq/L. How should potassium be managed as insulin therapy begins?
options: Withhold insulin entirely until potassium falls below 3.5 mEq/L
Start insulin now; begin adding potassium to fluids once K falls below about 5.2 mEq/L and urine output is adequate
Give 40 mEq of IV potassium push immediately because the patient is total-body depleted
Insulin and potassium are unrelated, so no monitoring of potassium is needed
stages.explanation: Although his measured potassium is high-normal at 5.3, total-body potassium is depleted in DKA; the serum level is falsely elevated because acidosis and insulin deficiency shift potassium out of cells. Insulin can begin now since potassium is above ~3.3, but as insulin drives potassium intracellularly the level will fall, so potassium should be added to fluids once it drops below roughly 5.2 mEq/L provided the patient is making urine. Withholding insulin until potassium < 3.5 is wrong — insulin is only held if K is below ~3.3 to first replete. An immediate 40 mEq IV push is dangerous and never given as a rapid bolus. Ignoring potassium entirely risks fatal hypokalemia and arrhythmia. Vigilant potassium monitoring and timely repletion are essential.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Several hours into treatment his glucose falls to 210 mg/dL but his anion gap remains elevated at 18 and beta-hydroxybutyrate is still positive. What is the correct adjustment?
options: Stop the insulin infusion because the glucose has normalized
Switch immediately to subcutaneous long-acting insulin only
Continue the insulin infusion and add dextrose-containing IV fluids to keep glucose around 150–200 until the gap closes
Double the insulin rate to drive glucose below 100 rapidly
stages.explanation: DKA resolves when the anion gap closes and ketogenesis stops, not when glucose normalizes — glucose corrects faster than the underlying ketoacidosis. Because his gap is still 18 and ketones persist, the insulin infusion must continue to suppress ketogenesis; to avoid hypoglycemia as glucose falls, dextrose is added to the IV fluids to keep glucose around 150–200 mg/dL while insulin runs. Stopping insulin once glucose falls would allow rebound ketogenesis and worsening acidosis. Switching to subcutaneous long-acting insulin alone before the gap closes (and without the recommended overlap period) risks recurrence of DKA. Doubling the insulin to push glucose below 100 causes hypoglycemia without hastening gap closure. Continue insulin with dextrose support until ketoacidosis resolves.

cases.diagnosis: Diabetic ketoacidosis precipitated by insulin omission
pearls: DKA is defined by hyperglycemia, high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis, and ketonemia/ketonuria; beta-hydroxybutyrate is the dominant ketone.
Fluids come first — correct the large volume deficit before or alongside starting insulin.
Serum potassium is often normal or high at presentation despite total-body depletion; insulin will drop it, so monitor and replete (hold insulin if K < 3.3).
Treat until the anion gap closes, not until glucose normalizes; add dextrose to fluids to keep the insulin running safely.

cases.title: Crushing Chest Pain and Sweating
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden onset of severe crushing substernal chest pressure radiating to the jaw with profuse sweating.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man presents with 45 minutes of sudden, severe, crushing substernal chest pressure that began while he was mowing the lawn. The pain radiates to his jaw and left arm and is associated with profuse diaphoresis, nausea, and a feeling of impending doom. He denies prior episodes of similar pain. Past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a 30-pack-year smoking history. Medications are amlodipine 10 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg daily. He has no drug allergies. He works as a construction foreman, drinks alcohol on weekends, and has a family history of early myocardial infarction in his older brother at age 52. He took one of his wife's nitroglycerin tablets without relief before arrival. Review of systems is positive for lightheadedness when he stands but negative for fever, cough, recent illness, leg swelling, or calf pain. He has had no recent surgery, trauma, or immobilization.
cases.exam: The patient is diaphoretic, pale, and visibly uncomfortable, clutching his chest. He is alert and oriented. Cardiac exam reveals bradycardia with a regular rhythm and no murmurs or rubs; heart sounds are distant. The lungs are clear bilaterally with no crackles. The jugular venous pressure appears elevated, but there is no peripheral edema. Extremities are cool with delayed capillary refill. There is no chest-wall tenderness to palpation and no asymmetry of breath sounds. Peripheral pulses are symmetric in both arms and legs without radial-femoral delay. The abdomen is soft and nontender, and the neurologic exam is grossly intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the single most important immediate diagnostic test to obtain within 10 minutes of arrival?
options: 12-lead electrocardiogram
Chest X-ray
CT pulmonary angiogram
Bedside transthoracic echocardiogram
stages.explanation: A 12-lead ECG must be obtained and interpreted within 10 minutes of arrival for any patient with possible acute coronary syndrome; it is the pivotal test that distinguishes STEMI (requiring emergent reperfusion) from NSTEMI/unstable angina and identifies arrhythmias. A chest X-ray is useful to exclude other causes (e.g., aortic dissection, pneumothorax) but does not diagnose myocardial infarction and should not delay the ECG. CT pulmonary angiography evaluates pulmonary embolism, which is lower on the differential given his exertional crushing pain, jaw radiation, and risk factors. A bedside echo can show wall-motion abnormalities and is helpful adjunctively, but it is not the first-line, time-critical test. The ECG drives the entire reperfusion pathway and is the immediate priority.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ECG: ST-segment elevation of 3 mm in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL
Right-sided lead V4R: ST elevation present
Troponin I: pending
Chest X-ray: normal cardiac silhouette, clear lungs, no widened mediastinum
Glucose 142 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The ECG shows inferior ST elevations with ST elevation in V4R. What does this indicate?
options: Anterior STEMI from a left anterior descending occlusion
Inferior STEMI with right ventricular involvement
Acute pericarditis
Left bundle branch block of uncertain age
stages.explanation: ST elevation in the inferior leads (II, III, aVF) indicates an inferior STEMI, most often from right coronary artery occlusion. ST elevation in the right-sided lead V4R is the key marker of right ventricular infarction, which complicates up to 40% of inferior MIs and explains his hypotension, bradycardia, and elevated JVP with clear lungs (right-sided failure without pulmonary congestion). An anterior STEMI would show ST elevation in the precordial leads V1–V4 from LAD occlusion, not the inferior leads. Acute pericarditis classically causes diffuse, non-territorial ST elevation with PR depression and no reciprocal changes; here reciprocal depression in I and aVL is present, favoring infarction. There is no mention of a left bundle branch pattern. This is an inferior STEMI with RV involvement.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the right ventricular involvement and hypotension, which intervention is potentially harmful and should be avoided?
options: Aspirin 325 mg chewed
A cautious intravenous normal saline bolus
Nitroglycerin
Urgent activation of the cardiac catheterization lab
stages.explanation: Nitroglycerin is potentially dangerous in right ventricular infarction. The failing right ventricle is critically preload-dependent, and nitrates cause venodilation that reduces preload, which can precipitate profound hypotension and cardiovascular collapse — exactly the risk in this hypotensive patient (note his nitroglycerin trial worsened his lightheadedness). Aspirin 325 mg chewed is a cornerstone antiplatelet therapy that should be given immediately. A cautious IV fluid bolus is the correct supportive measure for RV infarction to maintain preload and blood pressure. Emergent catheterization-lab activation for primary PCI is the definitive reperfusion strategy and must occur without delay. Avoiding nitrates (and other preload reducers) while supporting preload is the central teaching point of RV infarction.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He is 90 minutes from a PCI-capable center and onset was 1 hour ago. What is the preferred reperfusion strategy?
options: Administer fibrinolytics and admit to the floor
Treat medically and obtain a stress test in 48 hours
Start a heparin drip and observe for spontaneous resolution
Transfer for primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI)
stages.explanation: Primary PCI is the preferred reperfusion strategy for STEMI when it can be performed promptly, ideally with a first-medical-contact-to-device time under 120 minutes. With anticipated transfer and intervention achievable within that window, transfer for primary PCI is superior to fibrinolysis, offering better vessel patency and lower rates of reinfarction, stroke, and death. Fibrinolytics are reserved for when timely PCI is not available (transfer delays beyond ~120 minutes); they are second-line here. Medical management with a delayed stress test is unacceptable for an active STEMI, which mandates emergent reperfusion. Heparin alone while awaiting spontaneous resolution abandons the patient to ongoing myocardial necrosis. Transfer for primary PCI is the standard of care in this scenario.

cases.diagnosis: Inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction with right ventricular involvement
pearls: Obtain and interpret a 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes for any suspected acute coronary syndrome.
Inferior STEMI (II, III, aVF) warrants right-sided leads — ST elevation in V4R signals RV infarction.
Avoid nitrates and other preload reducers in RV infarction; the RV is preload-dependent — give cautious fluids instead.
Primary PCI is preferred when achievable within ~120 minutes of first medical contact; otherwise use fibrinolysis.

cases.title: Jaw and Arm Discomfort with Nausea
cases.chiefComplaint: Several hours of intermittent jaw and left arm aching with nausea and fatigue.
cases.history: A 66-year-old woman presents with several hours of intermittent aching in her jaw and left arm accompanied by nausea, fatigue, and mild shortness of breath. She describes no classic chest pain but feels generally unwell and uncharacteristically weak. The discomfort has waxed and waned and partially eased with rest. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and dyslipidemia. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily, losartan 50 mg daily, and rosuvastatin 10 mg daily. She has no drug allergies. She is a never-smoker who walks for exercise and lives with her husband. Family history includes coronary artery disease in her mother. She initially attributed her symptoms to a dental problem. Review of systems is positive for diaphoresis earlier in the day and exertional fatigue over the past week but negative for fever, cough, calf pain, recent travel, or syncope. She reports no recent illness, trauma, or surgery and has never had symptoms like this before.
cases.exam: The patient is mildly anxious but in no acute distress and is fully oriented. Cardiac auscultation reveals a regular rate and rhythm with a soft S4 gallop and no murmurs or rubs. The lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally without crackles or wheezes. The jugular venous pressure is normal. There is no chest-wall tenderness reproducible on palpation. Extremities are warm and well perfused with symmetric pulses and no edema. The abdomen is soft and nontender. There is no calf swelling or tenderness, and the neurologic examination is intact. Skin is normal in color without pallor or cyanosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond the immediate ECG, which initial laboratory test is most important for evaluating possible myocardial injury?
options: A single creatine kinase-MB level
B-type natriuretic peptide
Serial high-sensitivity cardiac troponin measurements
D-dimer
stages.explanation: Serial high-sensitivity cardiac troponin measurements are the most important laboratory test for detecting myocardial injury and diagnosing NSTEMI. A rising and/or falling troponin pattern (a dynamic change) on serial draws, in the right clinical context, defines acute myocardial infarction. CK-MB is far less sensitive and specific than troponin and a single value cannot establish a dynamic pattern. BNP reflects ventricular wall stress and aids heart-failure assessment but does not diagnose acute coronary syndrome. D-dimer is used to risk-stratify for venous thromboembolism, not myocardial injury, and would be a distractor here. Her atypical anginal-equivalent symptoms in a diabetic woman make serial troponins essential, since diabetics and women frequently present without classic chest pain.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ECG: 1 mm horizontal ST depression in leads V4–V6 with T-wave inversions; no ST elevation
High-sensitivity troponin: initial 48 ng/L, repeat at 3 hours 180 ng/L (upper reference limit 14 ng/L)
Hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Glucose 162 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the ECG and troponin trend, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI)
Non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI)
Stable angina
Acute pericarditis
stages.explanation: The combination of ischemic symptoms, ST depression and T-wave inversions WITHOUT ST elevation, and a clearly dynamic troponin (rising from 48 to 180 ng/L, well above the reference limit) defines a non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction. STEMI requires diagnostic ST-segment elevation, which is absent. Stable angina is exertional, predictable, relieved by rest, and does NOT cause troponin elevation — her significant troponin rise excludes it. Acute pericarditis produces diffuse ST elevation with PR depression and usually pleuritic, positional pain, not the territorial ST depression and rising troponin seen here. The dynamic troponin plus ischemic ECG changes in the absence of ST elevation is the textbook definition of NSTEMI, frequently presenting atypically in older diabetic women.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which initial medical therapy is appropriate for her NSTEMI while planning further care?
options: Fibrinolytic therapy immediately
Antibiotics and supportive care only
Immediate emergent thrombectomy without antiplatelet therapy
Dual antiplatelet therapy and anticoagulation with antianginal/anti-ischemic measures
stages.explanation: Initial NSTEMI management combines antiplatelet therapy (aspirin plus a P2Y12 inhibitor), anticoagulation (e.g., heparin or enoxaparin), and anti-ischemic measures (beta-blocker if not contraindicated, nitrates for ongoing pain, statin), along with risk stratification to time angiography. Fibrinolytics are contraindicated in NSTEMI — they have no proven benefit and increase bleeding risk without ST elevation. Antibiotics have no role in acute coronary syndrome. Thrombectomy or PCI is performed during catheterization, but never without antiplatelet and anticoagulant therapy, which are foundational. The correct initial approach is guideline-based antithrombotic and anti-ischemic medical therapy while determining the timing of invasive evaluation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She has ongoing dynamic troponin elevation and ischemic ECG changes but is hemodynamically stable. What is the appropriate timing of coronary angiography?
options: Immediate (within 2 hours) emergent angiography as for a STEMI
Early invasive angiography, generally within 24 hours
Routine outpatient angiography in 2–3 weeks
No angiography; manage medically and discharge today
stages.explanation: For a hemodynamically stable NSTEMI patient who is at high risk (dynamic troponin and ischemic ECG changes, elevated GRACE score), an early invasive strategy with angiography generally within 24 hours is recommended and improves outcomes. Immediate (within 2 hours) angiography is reserved for very-high-risk features such as hemodynamic instability, refractory ischemia, life-threatening arrhythmias, or mechanical complications — none present here, so treating her exactly like a STEMI is not required. Delaying to routine outpatient angiography in weeks is unsafe given her ongoing biomarker rise and ECG changes. Forgoing angiography and discharging her ignores a confirmed myocardial infarction with high-risk features. Early invasive evaluation within 24 hours is the guideline-concordant choice.

cases.diagnosis: Non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI)
pearls: Women and diabetics often present with atypical anginal equivalents (jaw/arm pain, nausea, fatigue) rather than classic chest pain.
A dynamic high-sensitivity troponin (rise and/or fall) with ischemic ECG changes and no ST elevation defines NSTEMI.
NSTEMI management is antiplatelet + anticoagulation + anti-ischemic therapy; fibrinolytics are contraindicated.
High-risk stable NSTEMI warrants early invasive angiography within 24 hours; immediate cath is reserved for instability or refractory ischemia.

cases.title: Pleuritic Chest Pain After a Long Flight
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden sharp chest pain that worsens with breathing and shortness of breath one day after a long flight.
cases.history: A 45-year-old woman presents with sudden onset of sharp, right-sided pleuritic chest pain and shortness of breath that began this morning. She returned yesterday from an 11-hour international flight. She also notes mild right calf discomfort over the past two days. She denies fever, productive cough, or hemoptysis, though she felt briefly lightheaded when standing. Past medical history is notable for migraines and she takes a combined oral contraceptive pill. She has no other medications and no drug allergies. She is a never-smoker, drinks alcohol rarely, and works as an attorney. Family history includes a maternal aunt with a 'blood clot' after surgery. She has had no recent surgery, trauma, or immobilization other than the flight, and she has had no prior episodes of clots. Review of systems is positive for mild anxiety and palpitations but negative for orthopnea, leg swelling on the left, recent illness, or chest trauma. She reports her symptoms have not improved with rest.
cases.exam: The patient is mildly tachypneic and anxious but alert and oriented. Cardiac exam reveals a regular tachycardia with a loud pulmonic component of the second heart sound and no murmurs. The lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally without crackles, wheezes, or focal dullness. The jugular venous pressure is mildly elevated. The right calf is mildly tender with minimal swelling compared to the left; there is no palpable cord. There is no chest-wall tenderness reproducible on palpation. Extremities are otherwise warm and well perfused. The abdomen is soft and nontender, and the neurologic examination is intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Considering her presentation, which feature contributes the most points to her Wells score for pulmonary embolism?
options: Clinical signs and symptoms of deep vein thrombosis
Her age over 40
Her use of oral contraceptives
Her anxiety and palpitations
stages.explanation: In the Wells criteria for PE, clinical signs and symptoms of DVT (leg swelling and pain on palpation of the deep veins) contribute 3 points — the highest single category, tied with 'PE is the most likely diagnosis.' Her tender, mildly swollen right calf qualifies. Age over 40 is NOT a Wells criterion (it appears in some other scores, like the simplified pulmonary score, not classic Wells). Oral contraceptive use is an important clinical risk factor that raises pretest suspicion but is not itself a scored Wells item. Anxiety and palpitations are nonspecific and not scored. Recognizing DVT signs as the heaviest weighted Wells element — alongside tachycardia (1.5), recent immobilization/travel (1.5), and prior VTE — is the key to correct risk stratification.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Wells score: 6 (DVT signs 3, tachycardia 1.5, PE most likely 1, immobilization/travel-related risk noted)
D-dimer: not ordered given high pretest probability
CT pulmonary angiogram: filling defects in the right main and segmental pulmonary arteries; right ventricular-to-left ventricular diameter ratio > 1
Troponin: mildly elevated at 35 ng/L
ECG: sinus tachycardia with an S1Q3T3 pattern
Bedside echo: right ventricular dilation and hypokinesis; normal blood pressure

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her Wells score is 6 (high probability). Why was proceeding directly to CT pulmonary angiography rather than D-dimer appropriate?
options: D-dimer is more expensive than CT angiography
D-dimer is only useful in patients over 70
With a high pretest probability, a normal D-dimer cannot reliably exclude PE, so definitive imaging is preferred
CT angiography is risk-free and should always be the first test
stages.explanation: With a high pretest probability (Wells > 4 or 'PE likely'), a negative D-dimer does NOT reliably exclude pulmonary embolism because the post-test probability after a negative result remains unacceptably high; therefore definitive imaging with CT pulmonary angiography is the appropriate next step. D-dimer's value lies in its high negative predictive value in LOW or intermediate pretest probability patients, where a normal result safely rules out PE without imaging. Cost is not the rationale. D-dimer is not restricted to patients over 70 (age-adjusted cutoffs simply refine interpretation in older patients). CT angiography is not risk-free — it involves contrast and radiation — and is not indiscriminately first-line; it is selected here precisely because pretest probability is high.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The CT confirms PE with RV strain and elevated troponin, but she is normotensive (submassive/intermediate-risk PE). What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Systemic thrombolysis with full-dose tPA immediately
Therapeutic anticoagulation with close hemodynamic monitoring
Inferior vena cava filter placement alone
Observation without anticoagulation pending repeat imaging
stages.explanation: For intermediate-risk (submassive) PE — confirmed PE with RV strain and/or biomarker elevation but preserved blood pressure — the recommended initial treatment is therapeutic anticoagulation with close hemodynamic monitoring for deterioration. Systemic full-dose thrombolysis is reserved for massive (high-risk) PE with hemodynamic instability/hypotension or for submassive patients who decompensate, because routine thrombolysis in stable submassive PE increases major bleeding (including intracranial hemorrhage) without a clear mortality benefit. An IVC filter alone does not treat the existing clot and is reserved for patients with absolute contraindications to anticoagulation. Withholding anticoagulation and merely observing a confirmed PE is dangerous. Prompt anticoagulation with vigilant monitoring (and rescue thrombolysis if she deteriorates) is the correct strategy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which provoking factor most directly contributed to her venous thromboembolism and should be addressed going forward?
options: Her history of migraines
Combined oral contraceptive use plus recent prolonged travel/immobilization
Her occupation as an attorney
Occasional alcohol use
stages.explanation: Her VTE was provoked by a combination of estrogen-containing oral contraceptive use (a prothrombotic state) and recent prolonged immobilization during an 11-hour flight, both well-established, modifiable risk factors. Going forward, discontinuing the estrogen-containing contraceptive in favor of a non-estrogen method and counseling on travel prophylaxis (mobility, hydration) directly reduce recurrence risk. Migraines are relevant mainly because migraine with aura further argues against estrogen contraceptives, but the migraines themselves did not cause the clot. Her occupation and occasional alcohol use are not meaningful thrombotic risk factors. Identifying and removing the estrogen exposure plus addressing immobilization is the key secondary-prevention step and also informs anticoagulation duration for a provoked event.

cases.diagnosis: Acute submassive (intermediate-risk) pulmonary embolism provoked by estrogen and travel
pearls: Signs/symptoms of DVT and 'PE most likely' each score 3 points — the heaviest items in the Wells criteria.
In high pretest-probability PE, skip D-dimer and proceed to CT pulmonary angiography; D-dimer rules out only in low/intermediate probability.
Submassive PE (RV strain or troponin elevation but normotensive) is treated with anticoagulation and close monitoring, not routine thrombolysis.
Estrogen-containing contraceptives plus immobilization are classic provoking factors — address them for secondary prevention.

cases.title: Severe Headache with Very High Blood Pressure
cases.chiefComplaint: Pounding headache and blurry vision found to have markedly elevated blood pressure.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents with a pounding bilateral headache, blurry vision, and mild nausea that began this morning. He has not taken his blood pressure medications for the past two weeks because he ran out and could not refill them. He denies chest pain, focal weakness, slurred speech, or seizures. Past medical history includes long-standing poorly controlled hypertension and chronic kidney disease stage 3. His prescribed medications, which he has not been taking, are amlodipine 10 mg daily and lisinopril 40 mg daily. He has no drug allergies. He is a former smoker, drinks alcohol occasionally, and works as a warehouse manager. Family history includes hypertension and stroke in his father. Review of systems is positive for the headache and visual blurring but negative for chest pain, dyspnea at rest, focal neurologic deficits, abdominal/back pain, or decreased urine output that he has noticed. He reports no recent cocaine or stimulant use. He has no history of prior hypertensive crises or end-organ damage.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable from headache but alert, oriented, and without focal neurologic deficits; speech is fluent and strength is symmetric. Fundoscopic examination reveals bilateral retinal flame hemorrhages and papilledema. Cardiac exam shows a regular rate with a forceful, sustained apical impulse and an S4 gallop; no murmurs are heard. The lungs are clear without crackles. There is no jugular venous distention or peripheral edema. Peripheral pulses are symmetric without radial-femoral delay, and there is no abdominal bruit appreciated. The abdomen is soft and nontender. There is no asterixis, and the remainder of the neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What distinguishes a hypertensive emergency from severe asymptomatic hypertension (hypertensive urgency)?
options: A systolic blood pressure above 180 mmHg by itself
The patient reporting a headache only
A diastolic blood pressure above 110 mmHg by itself
The presence of acute, ongoing end-organ damage
stages.explanation: A hypertensive emergency is defined by severely elevated blood pressure accompanied by acute, ongoing target-organ damage (e.g., hypertensive encephalopathy, retinal hemorrhages/papilledema, acute kidney injury, acute heart failure/pulmonary edema, myocardial ischemia, aortic dissection, or stroke). The absolute blood pressure number alone — whether a systolic above 180 or a diastolic above 110 — does not define an emergency; many patients tolerate very high pressures without acute injury (that is hypertensive urgency). A headache alone is nonspecific. This patient's papilledema, retinal flame hemorrhages, and (as labs will show) acute kidney injury establish acute end-organ damage, making this a true hypertensive emergency requiring controlled intravenous blood-pressure reduction.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (baseline 1.4)
BUN 38 mg/dL
Potassium 4.6 mEq/L
Urinalysis: 2+ protein, few red cells, no casts
Troponin: negative
ECG: left ventricular hypertrophy with strain pattern, no acute ischemia
Non-contrast CT head: no hemorrhage or acute infarct

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding confirms acute end-organ damage and the diagnosis of hypertensive emergency in this patient?
options: Left ventricular hypertrophy on ECG, which is chronic
Acute kidney injury (creatinine rise from 1.4 to 2.4) plus retinal hemorrhages and papilledema
A normal head CT
His two-week lapse in medication adherence
stages.explanation: The acute rise in creatinine from a baseline of 1.4 to 2.4 mg/dL signifies acute kidney injury, and the retinal flame hemorrhages with papilledema indicate acute hypertensive retinopathy/grade IV changes — together these confirm acute target-organ damage and establish a hypertensive emergency. Left ventricular hypertrophy with strain reflects chronic, long-standing hypertension and is not evidence of acute injury. A normal head CT is reassuring (it excludes hemorrhage/infarct) but its normality does not by itself confirm or refute end-organ damage. Medication nonadherence is the precipitant, not a marker of organ injury. The acute kidney injury plus acute retinal changes are the defining acute end-organ findings here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: How rapidly should his blood pressure be lowered in this hypertensive emergency (without aortic dissection or acute stroke)?
options: Lower the BP to completely normal (120/80) within the first hour
Lower the systolic to 90 mmHg as fast as possible
Reduce mean arterial pressure by no more than about 10–20% in the first hour, then gradually further over the next 23 hours
Avoid lowering the BP at all to preserve organ perfusion
stages.explanation: In most hypertensive emergencies, blood pressure should be lowered in a controlled fashion — reducing the mean arterial pressure by no more than roughly 10–20% in the first hour, then a further gradual reduction over the next 23 hours, using titratable IV agents (e.g., nicardipine, labetalol, clevidipine). Abruptly normalizing the pressure to 120/80 within an hour or driving the systolic to 90 risks watershed ischemia (cerebral, coronary, renal) because chronically hypertensive patients have rightward-shifted autoregulation. Refusing to lower the pressure ignores ongoing organ damage. (Notable exceptions requiring faster, lower targets are aortic dissection and certain acute strokes, which this patient does not have.) Gradual, monitored reduction protects organ perfusion while halting injury.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which class of intravenous agent is most appropriate for the controlled, titratable blood-pressure reduction he needs?
options: An oral immediate-release short-acting nifedipine capsule
A titratable IV infusion such as nicardipine or labetalol
A large intravenous bolus of hydralazine repeated frequently
Sublingual captopril given as needed
stages.explanation: A continuous, titratable IV infusion such as nicardipine (a calcium channel blocker) or labetalol (a combined alpha/beta-blocker) allows smooth, controllable blood-pressure reduction with rapid adjustment, which is exactly what a hypertensive emergency requires. Immediate-release oral/sublingual nifedipine is contraindicated because it causes precipitous, unpredictable drops in pressure and reflex tachycardia, risking ischemic complications. Repeated large hydralazine boluses produce an erratic, unpredictable response and prolonged unpredictable hypotension, making titration difficult. Sublingual captopril offers neither the predictability nor the fine titratability needed, and ACE inhibitors are also less ideal acutely in a patient with acute kidney injury. Titratable IV infusions are the standard tools for managing hypertensive emergencies.

cases.diagnosis: Hypertensive emergency with acute kidney injury and hypertensive retinopathy
pearls: Hypertensive emergency = severe hypertension PLUS acute end-organ damage; the BP number alone does not define it.
Acute kidney injury, papilledema/retinal hemorrhages, encephalopathy, pulmonary edema, and ischemia are key end-organ findings.
Lower MAP by only ~10–20% in the first hour (exceptions: aortic dissection and certain strokes need different targets).
Use titratable IV agents (nicardipine, labetalol, clevidipine); avoid short-acting sublingual/oral nifedipine.

cases.title: Fever and Flank Pain in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of fever, right-sided flank pain, and burning with urination.
cases.history: A 34-year-old woman presents with two days of fever, chills, right-sided flank pain, and burning with urination. She also reports increased urinary frequency and urgency and one episode of nausea this morning. Her symptoms began as simple dysuria three days ago and have progressed to include the flank pain and fever. She denies vaginal discharge, abdominal pain elsewhere, or hematuria. Past medical history is unremarkable. Her only medication is a combined oral contraceptive, and she has no drug allergies. She is sexually active with one partner, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol socially. She has had two uncomplicated lower urinary tract infections in the past but never pyelonephritis. She is not pregnant; a urine pregnancy test today is negative. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for fever, chills, flank pain, and nausea but negative for vomiting that prevents oral intake, diarrhea, rash, or recent antibiotic use. She has been able to keep down fluids and oral medications so far.
cases.exam: The patient appears uncomfortable but not toxic and is alert and oriented. There is marked right costovertebral angle tenderness on percussion; the left flank is nontender. The abdomen is soft with mild right-sided suprapubic tenderness but no rebound, guarding, or rigidity, and bowel sounds are normal. Cardiac exam reveals a regular tachycardia without murmurs, and the lungs are clear. There is no pelvic or vaginal discharge noted on limited external exam. Extremities are warm and well perfused without edema. Mucous membranes are moist, and capillary refill is brisk. The neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which clinical feature most strongly differentiates pyelonephritis (upper tract) from a simple lower urinary tract infection (cystitis)?
options: The presence of dysuria
Increased urinary frequency
Suprapubic discomfort
Fever with costovertebral angle tenderness and systemic symptoms
stages.explanation: Fever, flank pain with costovertebral angle tenderness, and systemic symptoms (chills, nausea) signal upper-tract involvement — pyelonephritis — rather than simple cystitis. Dysuria, urinary frequency, and suprapubic discomfort are features of lower urinary tract infection and are commonly present in BOTH cystitis and pyelonephritis, so they do not discriminate between them. The hallmark that elevates the diagnosis from cystitis to pyelonephritis is the combination of systemic illness (fever) and localizing renal signs (CVA tenderness). This patient began with isolated dysuria (cystitis) that has ascended, now manifesting fever and right CVA tenderness, indicating progression to acute pyelonephritis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: positive leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, 50–100 WBC/hpf, many bacteria, no casts
Urine pregnancy test: negative
WBC 13.6 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL
Urine culture: sent, pending
Vital trend: tolerating oral fluids, no vomiting

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely causative organism in this otherwise healthy woman with acute pyelonephritis?
options: Staphylococcus aureus
Escherichia coli
Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Candida albicans
stages.explanation: Escherichia coli is by far the most common cause of uncomplicated urinary tract infections and pyelonephritis in healthy women, responsible for roughly 80–90% of community-acquired cases; the positive nitrites support a gram-negative Enterobacterales organism (nitrites reflect bacterial reduction of urinary nitrate, characteristic of E. coli and similar bacteria). Staphylococcus aureus is an uncommon urinary pathogen and, when found in urine, raises concern for hematogenous seeding rather than ascending infection. Pseudomonas aeruginosa typically causes complicated or healthcare-associated infections (catheters, instrumentation, recent antibiotics), not uncomplicated community pyelonephritis. Candida is seen with indwelling catheters, diabetes, or immunosuppression, not in a healthy young woman. Empiric therapy should therefore target E. coli and related gram-negative uropathogens.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She is hemodynamically stable, not pregnant, not vomiting, and able to tolerate oral intake. What is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Admit for intravenous antibiotics and continuous monitoring
Admit to the ICU for presumed urosepsis
Outpatient management with oral antibiotics and close follow-up
Discharge with no antibiotics and symptomatic care only
stages.explanation: A healthy, hemodynamically stable, non-pregnant woman with uncomplicated acute pyelonephritis who can tolerate oral intake and has reliable follow-up can be managed as an outpatient with oral antibiotics — a safe, guideline-supported approach. Admission for IV antibiotics is reserved for patients who are septic/hemodynamically unstable, pregnant, persistently vomiting (unable to tolerate oral therapy), immunocompromised, or have complicating features such as obstruction or failure of outpatient therapy — none of which she has. Discharging without any antibiotics is inappropriate because untreated pyelonephritis can progress to sepsis and renal abscess. ICU admission is unwarranted in the absence of septic shock or organ dysfunction. Outpatient oral therapy with close follow-up is the correct, resource-appropriate disposition.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which empiric oral antibiotic is most appropriate for outpatient treatment of her acute pyelonephritis?
options: Nitrofurantoin
A fluoroquinolone such as ciprofloxacin (where local resistance is low), or an alternative guided by local resistance
Oral vancomycin
Azithromycin
stages.explanation: For outpatient acute pyelonephritis, a fluoroquinolone such as ciprofloxacin is a recommended first-line oral option where local E. coli fluoroquinolone resistance is low (generally < 10%); when resistance is higher or fluoroquinolones are unsuitable, an alternative such as trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (if susceptible) or an initial long-acting parenteral dose (e.g., ceftriaxone) followed by oral therapy is used. Nitrofurantoin is INAPPROPRIATE for pyelonephritis because it does not achieve adequate renal tissue/parenchymal concentrations — it is limited to lower-tract cystitis. Oral vancomycin is not absorbed systemically and treats only intestinal C. difficile, not urinary infection. Azithromycin lacks reliable activity against gram-negative uropathogens and is not appropriate for pyelonephritis. The fluoroquinolone (with attention to local resistance) is the best listed choice.

cases.diagnosis: Acute uncomplicated pyelonephritis
pearls: Fever plus costovertebral angle tenderness and systemic symptoms distinguish pyelonephritis from simple cystitis.
E. coli causes the large majority of uncomplicated UTIs and pyelonephritis; positive nitrites suggest a gram-negative organism.
Stable, non-pregnant, non-vomiting patients with uncomplicated pyelonephritis can be treated as outpatients with oral antibiotics.
Avoid nitrofurantoin for pyelonephritis — it doesn't reach therapeutic renal tissue levels; use a fluoroquinolone or resistance-guided alternative.

cases.title: Loud Snoring and Falling Asleep at His Desk
cases.chiefComplaint: I'm exhausted all day and my wife says I stop breathing at night.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man presents for evaluation of daytime sleepiness present for over a year and worsening. He reports loud, habitual snoring nightly, and his wife has witnessed several episodes where he stops breathing and then gasps awake. He nods off at his desk, during meetings, and feels dangerously drowsy driving home. He sleeps 7-8 hours but wakes unrefreshed with morning headaches and a dry mouth. He has gained 30 lbs over 3 years. PMH: hypertension and prediabetes. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: works as an accountant, drinks 2-3 beers most evenings, denies tobacco, no recreational drugs. He drinks coffee throughout the day to stay awake. Family history: father had hypertension and 'heart trouble.' Review of systems notable for decreased libido and nocturia twice nightly. He denies cataplexy, sleep paralysis, or hypnagogic hallucinations. His Epworth Sleepiness Scale score is 16, consistent with excessive daytime sleepiness.
cases.exam: Obese middle-aged man in no acute distress. BMI 38 kg/m². Neck circumference 44 cm (17.5 in). Oropharynx crowded with a low-lying soft palate and Mallampati class IV airway; tonsils are not enlarged. Nasal passages patent. Cardiac exam: regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, no JVD, no S3. Lungs clear bilaterally. No lower-extremity edema. Neurologic exam nonfocal with normal strength, reflexes, and gait. No thyromegaly. No cyanosis or clubbing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which validated screening tool best stratifies this patient's pretest probability for obstructive sleep apnea?
options: CHA₂DS₂-VASc score
STOP-BANG questionnaire
CURB-65 score
Wells criteria
stages.explanation: The STOP-BANG questionnaire (Snoring, Tiredness, Observed apnea, Pressure/hypertension, BMI >35, Age >50, Neck >40 cm, male Gender) is the validated bedside screen for OSA risk. This patient scores 7-8 (snoring, tired, observed apneas, hypertension, BMI 38, age 52, neck 44 cm, male), indicating high risk and warranting sleep testing. CHA₂DS₂-VASc estimates stroke risk in atrial fibrillation, not sleep apnea. CURB-65 grades pneumonia severity and disposition. Wells criteria estimate pretest probability of pulmonary embolism. Only STOP-BANG addresses the clinical question of sleep-disordered breathing and helps decide whether to pursue polysomnography versus home sleep apnea testing.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate diagnostic test to confirm obstructive sleep apnea in this patient?
options: Overnight pulse oximetry alone at home
Multiple sleep latency test (MSLT)
Polysomnography (in-laboratory sleep study)
Daytime arterial blood gas
stages.explanation: Attended in-laboratory polysomnography is the gold standard for diagnosing OSA and quantifies the apnea-hypopnea index (AHI), oxygen desaturations, and sleep staging. In an uncomplicated high-risk patient without significant cardiopulmonary disease, a home sleep apnea test is an acceptable alternative, but isolated overnight oximetry lacks sensitivity and specificity and cannot distinguish apnea types or hypopneas. MSLT evaluates pathologic daytime sleepiness/narcolepsy and is performed after ruling out OSA, not as the primary test. A daytime ABG screens for chronic hypoventilation (e.g., obesity hypoventilation) but does not diagnose OSA or measure respiratory events during sleep.

stages.label: POLYSOMNOGRAPHY
stages.content:
Total sleep time: 6.2 h
Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI): 42 events/hour (predominantly obstructive)
Lowest SpO2: 81%
Arousal index: 38/hour
Events worse in supine position and REM sleep
No periodic limb movements; no evidence of central apneas
Interpretation: Severe obstructive sleep apnea

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given severe OSA (AHI 42), what is the first-line treatment?
options: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP)
Mandibular advancement oral appliance
Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP)
Modafinil monotherapy
stages.explanation: CPAP is first-line therapy for moderate-to-severe OSA and provides the greatest, most reliable reduction in AHI, daytime sleepiness, and blood pressure. It pneumatically splints the upper airway open. Oral mandibular advancement devices are reserved for mild-to-moderate OSA or CPAP-intolerant patients, with lower efficacy in severe disease. UPPP is surgical, has variable success, and is not first-line for severe OSA. Modafinil treats residual sleepiness despite adequate CPAP adherence but does not treat the underlying airway obstruction or its cardiovascular consequences, so it cannot be used as monotherapy. Weight loss and avoiding alcohol/sedatives are important adjuncts but inadequate alone in severe disease.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Beyond starting CPAP, which counseling point is most important to address at this visit?
options: Reassure him that snoring is benign and requires no follow-up
Advise that untreated severe OSA increases risk while driving drowsy and recommend he avoid driving when sleepy
Recommend he stop his antihypertensives now that CPAP will normalize blood pressure
Tell him alcohol at night improves sleep quality
stages.explanation: Untreated severe OSA markedly increases the risk of motor-vehicle and occupational accidents from microsleeps; counseling on drowsy-driving safety is essential and sometimes legally reportable depending on jurisdiction. Snoring with witnessed apneas is not benign—it signals significant pathology requiring treatment and follow-up. Antihypertensives should not be stopped; CPAP may modestly lower BP but his hypertension still requires therapy, and stopping medication risks uncontrolled BP. Alcohol is a respiratory depressant that worsens upper-airway collapsibility and apnea severity, so patients should be advised to reduce or avoid evening alcohol, not encouraged to drink.

cases.diagnosis: Severe obstructive sleep apnea (OSA)
