pearls: STOP-BANG ≥3 flags elevated OSA risk; ≥5 suggests high probability of moderate-to-severe disease.
Attended polysomnography is the gold standard; AHI ≥30 defines severe OSA.
CPAP is first-line for moderate-to-severe OSA; oral appliances suit mild disease or CPAP intolerance.
Untreated OSA drives hypertension, atrial fibrillation, pulmonary hypertension, and drowsy-driving crashes—counsel on driving safety.

cases.title: A Retired Carpenter With Two Years of Worsening Breathlessness
cases.chiefComplaint: I get winded just walking to the mailbox, and it's been getting worse.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents with insidiously progressive exertional dyspnea over 2 years and a persistent nonproductive dry cough. Initially he tired only on stairs; now he is breathless walking on level ground. He denies orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, chest pain, wheezing, fevers, or weight loss. No joint pains, rashes, Raynaud phenomenon, dysphagia, or sicca symptoms. PMH: GERD and hyperlipidemia. Medications: omeprazole 20 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: 20 pack-year smoking history, quit 15 years ago; worked as a carpenter, no birds or down bedding, no hot tub, no mold-exposed work. Family history: a brother with 'lung scarring.' He has not had occupational dust protection consistently. Review of systems negative for hemoptysis, edema, and recurrent infections. He has tried inhaled albuterol without benefit. He is frustrated that prior 'bronchitis' treatments did nothing. Functional status: limited to one flight of stairs with frequent rests. No recent travel or sick contacts.
cases.exam: Thin elderly man, mildly tachypneic at rest. Fine, dry, end-inspiratory 'Velcro' crackles at both lung bases, not clearing with cough. No wheezes or rhonchi. Digital clubbing of fingers present. Cardiac: regular rhythm, no murmurs; a slightly accentuated P2 noted. No JVD, no peripheral edema, no rashes, no synovitis or sclerodactyly. Resting oxygen saturation 91% on room air with exertional desaturation to 86%. No cyanosis at rest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of findings most strongly suggests a fibrotic interstitial lung disease rather than COPD or heart failure?
options: Expiratory wheezing with hyperinflation and prolonged expiration
Bilateral basilar fine inspiratory 'Velcro' crackles with digital clubbing and exertional desaturation
Elevated JVP, S3 gallop, and bilateral pitting edema
Unilateral focal crackles with fever and purulent sputum
stages.explanation: Fine, dry, late-inspiratory 'Velcro' crackles at the bases, digital clubbing, and exertional hypoxemia are classic for fibrotic ILD such as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF); the crackles arise from the popping open of fibrosed small airways and do not clear with cough. Expiratory wheezing, hyperinflation, and prolonged expiration instead point to obstructive disease (COPD or asthma). Elevated JVP, an S3 gallop, and bilateral pitting edema indicate heart failure—a key mimic of chronic dyspnea but not pulmonary fibrosis. Unilateral focal crackles with fever and purulent sputum suggest pneumonia. The chronic, progressive, bibasilar pattern with clubbing and desaturation directs the workup toward interstitial fibrosis and high-resolution CT rather than empiric bronchodilator or diuretic trials, which would only delay diagnosis.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic study?
options: Standard chest radiograph alone
High-resolution CT (HRCT) of the chest
Empiric high-dose prednisone trial
Methacholine challenge test
stages.explanation: HRCT of the chest is the pivotal noninvasive test for suspected ILD; it characterizes the distribution and pattern of fibrosis and can establish a usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) pattern, often obviating biopsy. A plain chest radiograph is insensitive and nonspecific for early fibrosis and cannot reliably define the pattern. Empiric high-dose steroids are inappropriate before a diagnosis—steroids can harm IPF and even increase mortality (per the PANTHER-IPF trial). A methacholine challenge evaluates airway hyperreactivity in suspected asthma, which is not the clinical picture here. Pulmonary function testing should also be obtained to quantify restriction and diffusion impairment.

stages.label: IMAGING & PFTs
stages.content:
HRCT chest: bibasilar, subpleural reticulation with honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis; minimal ground-glass; no significant mediastinal adenopathy — pattern consistent with usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP)
PFTs: FVC 62% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.84 (no obstruction), TLC 64% predicted (restrictive), DLCO 41% predicted
ABG (room air): pH 7.43, PaO2 66 mmHg, PaCO2 38 mmHg
Serologies (ANA, RF, anti-CCP, myositis panel): negative
BNP 70 pg/mL; echocardiogram: normal LV function, estimated PASP 38 mmHg

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a definite UIP pattern on HRCT, negative autoimmune serologies, and no identifiable exposure, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis
Nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP) from connective tissue disease
Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF)
Sarcoidosis
stages.explanation: In an older adult with a definite UIP pattern on HRCT (subpleural, basal-predominant reticulation with honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis), no autoimmune features, and no inciting exposure, idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis is the diagnosis—UIP without a secondary cause is IPF. Chronic hypersensitivity pneumonitis classically shows upper/mid-zone changes, mosaic attenuation, and an exposure history (birds, mold), which he lacks. CTD-related NSIP typically yields ground-glass-predominant disease and positive serologies. Sarcoidosis causes upper-lobe nodularity along bronchovascular bundles with bilateral hilar adenopathy and granulomas, not basal honeycombing. Multidisciplinary discussion confirms IPF; surgical biopsy is unnecessary when HRCT shows definite UIP.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which therapy is evidence-based to slow the decline in lung function in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis?
options: Long-term oral prednisone plus azathioprine and N-acetylcysteine
An antifibrotic agent such as pirfenidone or nintedanib
Inhaled corticosteroid plus long-acting beta-agonist
Chronic broad-spectrum antibiotics
stages.explanation: Antifibrotic therapy with pirfenidone or nintedanib slows the rate of FVC decline in IPF and is standard of care; both should be offered, with referral for lung transplantation evaluation in appropriate candidates. The triple regimen of prednisone, azathioprine, and N-acetylcysteine was shown in PANTHER-IPF to increase hospitalizations and mortality and is contraindicated. ICS/LABA treats obstructive airway disease, which this patient does not have (FEV1/FVC normal, restrictive physiology). Chronic antibiotics have no established role in slowing IPF progression. Supplemental oxygen for exertional/resting hypoxemia, pulmonary rehabilitation, vaccinations, and treatment of comorbid GERD are important supportive measures.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which complication should prompt the highest concern for acute clinical deterioration in IPF?
options: Acute exacerbation of IPF with rapidly worsening hypoxemia and new ground-glass opacities
Slowly resolving simple pneumothorax
Transient benign sinus tachycardia
Mild reversible bronchospasm
stages.explanation: Acute exacerbations of IPF—rapid (under 1 month) worsening of dyspnea and hypoxemia with new bilateral ground-glass opacities superimposed on UIP, after excluding heart failure and infection/PE—carry very high mortality and are the most feared acute complication. They require hospitalization, supportive care, and exclusion of mimics. A slowly resolving simple pneumothorax is comparatively low-acuity, though air leaks can occur in fibrotic lungs. Benign sinus tachycardia and mild reversible bronchospasm are not life-threatening and are not characteristic drivers of IPF deterioration. Recognizing acute exacerbation drives prognosis, transplant urgency, and goals-of-care discussions.

cases.diagnosis: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (UIP pattern)
pearls: Bibasilar 'Velcro' crackles + clubbing + restrictive PFTs with low DLCO should trigger HRCT for ILD.
A definite UIP pattern (subpleural/basal reticulation, honeycombing) plus no secondary cause = IPF; biopsy often unneeded.
Antifibrotics (pirfenidone, nintedanib) slow FVC decline; steroid-based triple therapy increases mortality (PANTHER-IPF).
Acute exacerbations with new ground-glass opacities carry high mortality—exclude infection, PE, and heart failure first.

cases.title: Tender Red Shin Nodules and a Persistent Cough
cases.chiefComplaint: I have painful red bumps on my shins, a dry cough, and I just feel run down.
cases.history: A 35-year-old woman presents with 3 weeks of tender, raised, erythematous nodules over both anterior shins, accompanied by a 2-month dry cough, low-grade fevers, fatigue, and mild migratory ankle arthralgias. She reports a 5-lb weight loss and intermittent blurred vision with eye redness. She denies hemoptysis, drenching night sweats, sick contacts, or recent travel to TB-endemic regions. No oral or genital ulcers, no abdominal pain. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: none other than occasional ibuprofen. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, works as a schoolteacher, no occupational dust or bird exposure, no recent antibiotics or new drugs. Family history: noncontributory; no autoimmune disease. Review of systems notable for the eye symptoms and the leg lesions; she has no dyspnea at rest. She has felt thirsty and is urinating more than usual over the past few weeks. She has not had a recent streptococcal infection and is up to date on vaccinations.
cases.exam: Well-appearing young woman in no distress. Bilateral anterior shins with tender, warm, erythematous subcutaneous nodules consistent with erythema nodosum, without ulceration. Eyes: mild conjunctival injection with photophobia; anterior chamber appears inflamed on penlight—anterior uveitis suspected. Lungs clear to auscultation. No palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy. Cardiac exam normal. Mild ankle warmth without effusion. No hepatosplenomegaly. Skin otherwise without plaques or scaling. Neurologic exam normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single imaging study is most likely to reveal the unifying diagnosis behind erythema nodosum, cough, and uveitis in this young woman?
options: Chest radiograph
Bilateral lower-extremity venous duplex
Abdominal ultrasound
Brain MRI
stages.explanation: A chest radiograph is the highest-yield first test: the constellation of erythema nodosum, cough, arthralgias, and uveitis (Löfgren-type presentation) points to sarcoidosis, which classically shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Venous duplex evaluates for DVT, which does not explain the systemic and ocular findings. Abdominal ultrasound is not indicated by this presentation. Brain MRI would be reserved for focal neurologic deficits suggesting neurosarcoidosis, which she does not have. The chest film both supports the leading diagnosis and screens for the most common organ involvement, guiding the subsequent workup including labs and possible biopsy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Chest X-ray: bilateral symmetric hilar lymphadenopathy, clear lung fields (radiographic stage I)
Ca 11.6 mg/dL (elevated)
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
ACE level: elevated
1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D: elevated; 25-OH vitamin D low-normal
CBC: mild lymphopenia; ESR mildly elevated
Interferon-gamma release assay (TB): negative
Urinalysis: no casts; 24-h urine calcium elevated

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the mechanism of this patient's hypercalcemia?
options: PTH-mediated bone resorption from a parathyroid adenoma
Granuloma macrophage 1α-hydroxylase converting 25-OH vitamin D to active 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D
PTHrP secretion from an occult malignancy
Excess dietary calcium intake
stages.explanation: In sarcoidosis, activated macrophages within granulomas express 1α-hydroxylase, converting 25-hydroxyvitamin D to active 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D independent of PTH. This increases intestinal calcium absorption and causes hypercalcemia/hypercalciuria—reflected here by elevated 1,25-D with a suppressed PTH expected. A parathyroid adenoma causes PTH-mediated hypercalcemia with elevated PTH, not the granulomatous pattern. PTHrP from malignancy (e.g., squamous cell carcinoma) is a humoral mechanism but does not fit a young woman with classic Löfgren syndrome and elevated 1,25-D. Dietary calcium alone rarely causes hypercalcemia with normal renal function. Management includes sun avoidance and limiting vitamin D/calcium intake.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Apart from biopsy, which histopathologic finding defines sarcoidosis and what must be excluded?
options: Caseating granulomas; exclude fungal infection only
Non-caseating (non-necrotizing) granulomas; exclude infection (e.g., TB, fungi) and other causes
Neutrophilic microabscesses; exclude vasculitis
Eosinophilic granulomas with Langerhans cells; exclude drug reaction
stages.explanation: Sarcoidosis is defined histologically by non-caseating (non-necrotizing) epithelioid granulomas, and it is a diagnosis of exclusion—infections such as tuberculosis and fungal disease (which cause caseating/necrotizing granulomas) and other granulomatous conditions must be excluded, hence the negative IGRA here. Caseating granulomas suggest TB, not sarcoidosis. Neutrophilic microabscesses point toward infection or certain vasculitides. Eosinophil-rich granulomas with Langerhans cells (CD1a+, S100+) characterize Langerhans cell histiocytosis. In a textbook Löfgren syndrome (bilateral hilar adenopathy, erythema nodosum, fever, arthralgias), the diagnosis can sometimes be made clinically without biopsy given its very high specificity.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given symptomatic hypercalcemia and anterior uveitis, what is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Observation alone, since most sarcoidosis resolves spontaneously
Systemic corticosteroids (prednisone)
Methotrexate as first-line monotherapy
Bisphosphonate infusion only, with no other therapy
stages.explanation: Although many patients with sarcoidosis—especially Löfgren syndrome—remit spontaneously and can be observed, this patient has organ-threatening/symptomatic disease (hypercalcemia and uveitis), which is an indication for systemic corticosteroids (prednisone). Steroids reduce granulomatous activity, lower 1,25-D production, and treat ocular inflammation. Pure observation is inappropriate when hypercalcemia and eye inflammation are present. Methotrexate is a steroid-sparing second-line agent for chronic or refractory disease, not initial monotherapy here. A bisphosphonate may transiently lower calcium but does not treat the underlying granulomatous process or the uveitis; the definitive therapy is corticosteroids plus calcium/vitamin D restriction and ophthalmology referral.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature in this presentation predicts the most favorable prognosis?
options: Presence of erythema nodosum with acute Löfgren syndrome
Lupus pernio
Stage IV pulmonary fibrosis
Cardiac and neurologic involvement
stages.explanation: Löfgren syndrome (erythema nodosum, bilateral hilar adenopathy, fever, and arthralgias) carries an excellent prognosis, with the majority of patients achieving spontaneous remission within 1-2 years. Lupus pernio (violaceous indurated facial/nasal plaques) signals chronic, often refractory disease with a worse prognosis. Stage IV pulmonary disease indicates established fibrosis with irreversible architectural distortion and worse outcomes. Cardiac (conduction disease, cardiomyopathy) and neurologic involvement portend serious morbidity and mortality and require aggressive immunosuppression. Recognizing the benign Löfgren phenotype helps counsel the patient and avoid overtreatment while still managing her symptomatic hypercalcemia and uveitis.

cases.diagnosis: Sarcoidosis (Löfgren syndrome) with hypercalcemia and uveitis
pearls: Löfgren syndrome = erythema nodosum + bilateral hilar adenopathy + fever/arthralgias; excellent prognosis, often self-limited.
Sarcoid hypercalcemia is from granuloma 1α-hydroxylase making active vitamin D—treat with steroids and limit calcium/vitamin D.
Sarcoidosis shows non-caseating granulomas and is a diagnosis of exclusion—rule out TB and fungal infection.
Indications to treat include hypercalcemia, uveitis, cardiac, neurologic, or progressive pulmonary disease; steroids are first-line.

cases.title: Progressive Breathlessness and Lightheadedness on Exertion
cases.chiefComplaint: I get severely short of breath and almost pass out when I climb stairs.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman reports 8 months of progressive exertional dyspnea, now occurring with minimal activity, accompanied by exertional lightheadedness, two episodes of near-syncope, and fatigue. She notes early satiety and lower-extremity swelling over the past month. She denies orthopnea improving with sitting, chest pain at rest, fevers, or hemoptysis. No skin tightening, but she reports cold, color-changing fingers in winter (possible Raynaud). PMH: hypothyroidism, treated. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Allergies: none. Social: never smoker, no anorexigen/stimulant use, no IV drug use, social alcohol; not on oral contraceptives. Family history: no known pulmonary hypertension or clotting disorders. Review of systems: no dysphagia, no telangiectasias noted by her, no recent prolonged immobilization or surgery. She had a normal stress test for atypical symptoms last year. She is increasingly limited and worried she has 'a heart problem.' No snoring or witnessed apneas. No liver disease history.
cases.exam: Middle-aged woman, comfortable at rest but dyspneic after walking. Elevated JVP with prominent a-wave. Cardiac: a loud, palpable P2; a left parasternal (right ventricular) heave; a soft holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border increasing with inspiration (tricuspid regurgitation). Lungs clear without crackles or wheeze. Abdomen: mild hepatomegaly with pulsatile liver edge. Extremities: 1+ pitting pedal edema. Fingers cool with mild color change; no sclerodactyly or digital ulcers. No clubbing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which exam findings best localize the problem to elevated pulmonary pressures and right heart strain?
options: Bibasilar crackles, S3 gallop, and displaced apical impulse
Loud P2, right ventricular (parasternal) heave, elevated JVP, and inspiratory-augmented tricuspid regurgitation murmur
Diastolic decrescendo murmur at the right upper sternal border
Pulsus paradoxus with muffled heart sounds and hypotension
stages.explanation: A loud/palpable P2 (elevated pulmonary artery pressure closing the pulmonic valve forcefully), an RV heave, elevated JVP, and a tricuspid regurgitation murmur that augments with inspiration (Carvallo sign) together localize disease to the pulmonary circulation and right heart—the signature of pulmonary hypertension with RV pressure overload. Bibasilar crackles, S3, and a displaced apex suggest left-sided systolic heart failure. A diastolic decrescendo murmur at the right upper sternal border indicates aortic regurgitation. Pulsus paradoxus with muffled sounds and hypotension suggests cardiac tamponade. The constellation here directs the workup toward echocardiography to estimate pulmonary pressures and assess RV function.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial noninvasive screening test for suspected pulmonary hypertension?
options: Transthoracic echocardiography
Right heart catheterization
Coronary angiography
Cardiac stress MRI
stages.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiography is the recommended noninvasive screen: it estimates pulmonary artery systolic pressure from the tricuspid regurgitation jet, assesses RV size/function, septal flattening, and screens for left heart disease and valvular causes. Right heart catheterization is the gold standard for confirming and hemodynamically classifying pulmonary hypertension but is invasive and follows an abnormal echo. Coronary angiography evaluates epicardial coronary disease, not pulmonary pressures, and her prior stress test was normal. Cardiac stress MRI is not the first-line screen for pulmonary hypertension. The diagnostic pathway is echo to screen, then RHC to confirm and characterize before assigning a WHO group and starting targeted therapy.

stages.label: ECHO & LABS
stages.content:
Echocardiogram: estimated PASP 62 mmHg; dilated, hypertrophied right ventricle with reduced RV systolic function; flattened interventricular septum (D-shaped LV in systole); normal LV ejection fraction 60%; no significant left-sided valve disease; moderate tricuspid regurgitation
NT-proBNP 1850 pg/mL
V/Q scan: no segmental perfusion defects (low probability for chronic thromboembolic disease)
ANA positive 1:320; pulmonary function: mildly reduced DLCO; HIV negative; LFTs normal
ECG: right axis deviation, right ventricular hypertrophy with strain

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis and define hemodynamics before therapy?
options: Begin empiric calcium channel blocker without further testing
Right heart catheterization with vasoreactivity testing
Start a loop diuretic and reassess in 6 months
Repeat echocardiography in 1 year
stages.explanation: Right heart catheterization is required to confirm pulmonary hypertension (mean pulmonary artery pressure >20 mmHg) and to distinguish precapillary disease (PAH: pulmonary capillary wedge pressure ≤15 mmHg with elevated pulmonary vascular resistance) from postcapillary (left heart) disease. Acute vasoreactivity testing identifies the small subset who may respond to calcium channel blockers. Starting a calcium channel blocker empirically is dangerous—nonresponders can decompensate, and only confirmed vasoreactive patients benefit. A loop diuretic treats volume overload symptomatically but does not establish the diagnosis or address the cause. Deferring with repeat echo delays diagnosis in a symptomatic patient with near-syncope. The positive ANA and Raynaud raise concern for connective-tissue-disease-associated PAH (WHO Group 1).

stages.label: RIGHT HEART CATHETERIZATION
stages.content:
Mean pulmonary artery pressure: 41 mmHg
Pulmonary capillary wedge pressure: 10 mmHg
Pulmonary vascular resistance: 7 Wood units
Cardiac index: 2.0 L/min/m²
Acute vasoreactivity testing: negative
Interpretation: precapillary pulmonary hypertension (pulmonary arterial hypertension)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For confirmed Group 1 pulmonary arterial hypertension that is vasoreactivity-negative, which is an appropriate disease-targeted therapy?
options: High-dose calcium channel blocker (e.g., nifedipine)
An endothelin receptor antagonist and/or a PDE-5 inhibitor (e.g., ambrisentan plus tadalafil)
Systemic beta-blockade to slow heart rate
Therapeutic warfarin as the sole therapy
stages.explanation: For vasoreactivity-negative PAH, disease-targeted vasodilator therapy is indicated—commonly initial combination therapy with an endothelin receptor antagonist (ambrisentan) plus a PDE-5 inhibitor (tadalafil); prostacyclin pathway agents are added or used upfront in high-risk patients. High-dose calcium channel blockers are reserved exclusively for vasoreactive responders and can cause hemodynamic collapse in nonresponders. Beta-blockers reduce cardiac output and are generally avoided because RV function depends on heart rate and contractility. Anticoagulation alone is not disease-targeted therapy for PAH and is no longer routinely recommended for idiopathic PAH. She should also be evaluated for the underlying connective tissue disease and referred to a PH center; diuretics manage RV volume overload as an adjunct.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which acute, life-threatening complication of severe pulmonary arterial hypertension must the team anticipate, and how is hypotension in this setting best managed?
options: Right ventricular failure/decompensation; support with careful volume optimization, inotropes/vasopressors, and oxygen rather than aggressive fluid boluses
Aortic dissection; lower blood pressure aggressively with IV nitroprusside
Massive hemoptysis; give high-dose anticoagulation
Hypoglycemic shock; administer a large insulin bolus
stages.explanation: Decompensated right ventricular failure is the feared, life-threatening complication of severe PAH: the pressure-overloaded RV can fail acutely, causing systemic hypotension and a downward spiral of reduced RV coronary perfusion. Management focuses on optimizing (not overloading) volume—the failing RV is sensitive to both under- and over-filling—plus inotropic and vasopressor support (e.g., norepinephrine, dobutamine), supplemental oxygen, treating triggers like arrhythmia or infection, and avoiding systemic hypotension. Aggressive fluid boluses can overdistend the RV and worsen output. Aortic dissection and nitroprusside are unrelated to this RV-failure physiology. Massive hemoptysis treated with anticoagulation would be dangerous and is not the issue here. Hypoglycemic shock and insulin are irrelevant to PAH decompensation.

cases.diagnosis: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (WHO Group 1, likely connective-tissue-disease-associated)
pearls: Loud/palpable P2 + RV heave + elevated JVP + inspiratory TR murmur = right heart pressure overload from pulmonary hypertension.
Echo screens (estimates PASP, RV strain); right heart catheterization confirms (mPAP >20 mmHg) and classifies pre- vs postcapillary.
Vasoreactivity testing matters: only responders get high-dose calcium channel blockers—they are dangerous in nonresponders.
Group 1 PAH treatment uses endothelin antagonists, PDE-5 inhibitors, and prostacyclin agents; screen for CTD, HIV, and thromboembolic causes.

cases.title: Sudden Bright Red Blood in the Toilet Without Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: I passed a lot of bright red blood from my rectum and feel dizzy.
cases.history: A 74-year-old man presents to the ED after three large-volume episodes of painless bright red blood and clots per rectum over 4 hours, with associated lightheadedness when standing. He denies abdominal pain, recent fevers, weight loss, or change in bowel habits, and he has had no vomiting or hematemesis. He had a similar self-limited bleed 2 years ago. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, osteoarthritis, and known colonic diverticulosis on prior colonoscopy. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, and naproxen 500 mg twice daily for knee pain. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker, occasional alcohol, lives with spouse. Family history: no colon cancer or inflammatory bowel disease. Review of systems negative for chest pain or melena; he reports the blood was clearly red, not black or tarry. He took an extra naproxen dose today. He appears mildly anxious and pale. He has no prior GI surgeries and no liver disease.
cases.exam: Pale, diaphoretic elderly man, alert and oriented. Tachycardic with a regular rhythm; no murmurs. Abdomen soft, nontender, nondistended, with normal-to-increased bowel sounds and no rebound or guarding; no palpable masses or organomegaly. Rectal exam reveals bright red blood and clots in the vault without an external mass, fissure, or palpable rectal mass; no melena. Extremities cool with delayed capillary refill. No stigmata of chronic liver disease (no spider angiomata, no ascites, no jaundice). Orthostatic vital sign changes present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most important immediate management step for this hypotensive, tachycardic patient?
options: Obtain an urgent CT colonography
Establish two large-bore IVs and begin volume resuscitation while sending type and crossmatch
Give oral activated charcoal
Start a proton pump inhibitor infusion and discharge with outpatient follow-up
stages.explanation: The first priority in any significant GI bleed with hemodynamic instability is resuscitation: two large-bore IVs, crystalloid/blood as needed, and sending labs including CBC, coagulation studies, and type and crossmatch. Stabilize before diagnostics. CT colonography is not used in active brisk bleeding and does not address shock. Activated charcoal is for certain ingestions, not GI hemorrhage. A PPI infusion targets upper GI (peptic) bleeding and would not be the priority for a presumed lower GI source; discharging an unstable patient is unsafe. Resuscitation and transfusion thresholds (typically transfuse to maintain hemoglobin ≥7 g/dL in stable patients) guide care while the source is localized.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL (baseline 13.8 g/dL 6 months ago)
Platelets 240 ×10³/µL
INR 1.1
BUN 22 mg/dL, Creatinine 1.2 mg/dL
BUN:Cr ratio ~18
Lactate 2.1 mmol/L
Nasogastric lavage (performed): clear, bile-stained, no blood
Type and crossmatch: 2 units PRBC available

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: The relatively normal BUN:Cr ratio and clear bile-stained NG lavage best support which conclusion?
options: An upper GI source is most likely
A lower GI source is most likely; hematochezia is consistent with diverticular bleeding
The bleeding has definitively stopped and no further workup is needed
This represents a small-bowel arteriovenous malformation exclusively
stages.explanation: A clear, bile-stained nasogastric lavage argues against an active upper GI source (bile suggests the pylorus was sampled), and a markedly elevated BUN:Cr ratio (>30) typically signals an upper bleed from digested blood—its relative absence here supports a lower GI source. Painless hematochezia in an elderly patient with known diverticulosis is most consistent with a diverticular bleed, the most common cause of major lower GI bleeding. The bleed has not been proven to stop—he remains tachycardic with a hemoglobin drop—so workup must continue. While angiodysplasia is a differential, the data do not point exclusively to a small-bowel AVM; colonoscopy after resuscitation/preparation is the key diagnostic and potentially therapeutic step.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: After adequate resuscitation, what is the preferred next diagnostic and potentially therapeutic procedure for a hemodynamically stabilized patient with presumed diverticular bleeding?
options: Colonoscopy after bowel preparation
Immediate exploratory laparotomy
Capsule endoscopy
Barium enema
stages.explanation: Colonoscopy after bowel preparation is the preferred next step in a stabilized patient: it can localize the bleeding source and provide therapy (clips, epinephrine injection, thermal coagulation, band ligation). Exploratory laparotomy/surgery is reserved for uncontrollable, massive bleeding after failed endoscopic and angiographic measures. Capsule endoscopy evaluates obscure small-bowel bleeding once upper and lower sources are excluded and is not used in acute brisk hematochezia. Barium enema obscures the mucosa, precludes endoscopic therapy, and has no role in acute GI bleeding. If bleeding is too brisk to permit colonoscopy or it is nondiagnostic, CT angiography and catheter angiography with embolization (or a tagged RBC scan) are the alternatives for localization and treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: If the patient continues to bleed briskly and is too unstable for adequate colonoscopy prep, which is the most appropriate localizing/therapeutic option?
options: Repeat the same naproxen dosing to assess rebleed
CT angiography followed by catheter angiography with selective embolization
Discharge with iron supplementation
Empiric octreotide infusion as definitive therapy
stages.explanation: In ongoing brisk lower GI bleeding when colonoscopy is not feasible, CT angiography can detect active extravasation (requires roughly ≥0.3-0.5 mL/min bleeding), and catheter angiography allows selective embolization to stop the bleed. NSAIDs and antiplatelet agents should be stopped, not continued—naproxen and aspirin increase bleeding risk and the offending naproxen should be discontinued. Discharging an actively bleeding, unstable patient is unsafe. Octreotide reduces splanchnic blood flow and is used adjunctively in variceal/upper GI bleeding, not as definitive therapy for diverticular hemorrhage. A tagged red blood cell scan is another sensitive localizer for slower, intermittent bleeding. Surgical resection is the last resort for refractory, localized disease.

cases.diagnosis: Lower GI bleed from colonic diverticulosis (diverticular hemorrhage)
pearls: Painless hematochezia in an elderly patient with diverticulosis = diverticular bleed until proven otherwise; resuscitate first.
A high BUN:Cr ratio and melena suggest an upper source; clear bile-stained NG lavage and bright red blood favor a lower source.
Colonoscopy after prep both localizes and treats; CT/catheter angiography with embolization is for brisk bleeding unsuitable for scope.
Stop NSAIDs/antiplatelets when feasible; transfuse to keep hemoglobin ≥7 g/dL in stable patients (higher if cardiac ischemia).

cases.title: Years of Bloating, Loose Stools, and Stubborn Anemia
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had bloating and loose stools for over a year, and my iron just won't come up.
cases.history: A 32-year-old woman presents with more than a year of intermittent diarrhea, abdominal bloating, excessive flatulence, and crampy discomfort, along with fatigue. She has lost about 8 lbs unintentionally. She was found to be iron-deficient on routine labs and has not responded well to oral iron. She notes symptoms seem worse after meals with bread and pasta. She denies bloody stools, nocturnal diarrhea, fevers, or recent travel/antibiotic use. PMH: hypothyroidism and iron-deficiency anemia. Medications: levothyroxine 88 mcg daily, ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily (poor response). Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, works as a nurse. Family history: a sister with 'gluten intolerance' and a mother with autoimmune thyroid disease. Review of systems notable for an intermittent itchy, blistering rash on the elbows and knees, and easy bruising. Menses are regular but heavy. She has not been pregnant. She reports occasional mouth ulcers. She has not knowingly tried a gluten-free diet.
cases.exam: Thin young woman, pale conjunctivae consistent with anemia. Abdomen soft, mildly distended with diffuse tympany and mild generalized tenderness without rebound or guarding; no hepatosplenomegaly or masses; normal bowel sounds. Skin: grouped, excoriated vesicular/papular lesions over the extensor elbows and knees (consistent with dermatitis herpetiformis). Angular cheilosis at the mouth corners and a few aphthous ulcers. No lymphadenopathy. No peripheral edema. Neurologic exam intact without peripheral neuropathy on screening. No jaundice.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given chronic diarrhea, iron deficiency refractory to oral iron, and an extensor-surface vesicular rash, what is the most appropriate first-line serologic test?
options: Anti-mitochondrial antibody
Tissue transglutaminase IgA (tTG-IgA) with a total IgA level
ASCA and p-ANCA panel
Hydrogen breath test for lactose intolerance
stages.explanation: The clinical picture—chronic diarrhea, malabsorptive iron deficiency unresponsive to oral iron, and dermatitis herpetiformis—strongly suggests celiac disease. Tissue transglutaminase IgA (tTG-IgA) is the recommended first-line serologic test for sensitivity and specificity, and a total IgA level is drawn concurrently because selective IgA deficiency (more common in celiac) can cause false-negative IgA-based tests. Anti-mitochondrial antibody screens for primary biliary cholangitis. ASCA/p-ANCA assist in inflammatory bowel disease workup, which is less consistent with this rash and refractory iron deficiency. A lactose breath test evaluates lactose intolerance, which causes bloating/diarrhea but not iron deficiency, dermatitis herpetiformis, or the autoimmune associations seen here. Testing must occur while the patient is still consuming gluten.

stages.label: SEROLOGIES & LABS
stages.content:
tTG-IgA: strongly positive (>10x upper limit of normal)
Total IgA: normal
Endomysial antibody (EMA): positive
Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, MCV 76 fL (microcytic)
Ferritin 6 ng/mL (low)
Folate low-normal; vitamin D low
TSH normal on levothyroxine

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With strongly positive tTG-IgA and EMA, what is the recommended next step to confirm the diagnosis in this adult?
options: Begin a gluten-free diet immediately without further testing
Duodenal (small-bowel) biopsy via upper endoscopy while still consuming gluten
Colonoscopy with terminal ileum biopsy
Repeat serology in one year
stages.explanation: In adults, the diagnosis of celiac disease is confirmed by duodenal biopsy obtained during upper endoscopy while the patient continues to eat gluten; characteristic findings include villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and intraepithelial lymphocytosis. Starting a gluten-free diet before biopsy can normalize histology and serology, obscuring the diagnosis—so testing must precede dietary change. Colonoscopy with ileal biopsy evaluates the colon and terminal ileum for IBD, not celiac disease, which affects the proximal small bowel. Repeating serology in a year delays diagnosis and treatment of a symptomatic patient with malabsorption. (In children, very high tTG titers with positive EMA may allow a no-biopsy diagnosis, but adults generally require confirmatory biopsy.)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms villous atrophy. What is the cornerstone of treatment for celiac disease?
options: Lifelong strict gluten-free diet
Long-term systemic corticosteroids
Pancreatic enzyme replacement
Empiric antibiotics for bacterial overgrowth
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of celiac disease management is a lifelong, strict gluten-free diet (eliminating wheat, barley, and rye), which allows mucosal healing, resolves symptoms, corrects malabsorption, and reduces long-term complications. Referral to a dietitian and repletion of deficiencies (iron, folate, vitamin D, calcium) are important adjuncts. Systemic corticosteroids are reserved for refractory celiac disease or celiac crisis, not routine therapy. Pancreatic enzyme replacement treats exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, a different cause of malabsorption. Empiric antibiotics for small intestinal bacterial overgrowth address a different entity; while SIBO can coexist, it is not the primary treatment for celiac disease. Adherence to the gluten-free diet typically also resolves dermatitis herpetiformis (dapsone can provide rapid skin relief).

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If untreated, patients with long-standing celiac disease are at increased risk for which malignancy?
options: Enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (and small-bowel adenocarcinoma)
Hepatocellular carcinoma
Renal cell carcinoma
Prostate adenocarcinoma
stages.explanation: Long-standing, untreated celiac disease increases the risk of enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL) and small-bowel adenocarcinoma; strict adherence to a gluten-free diet reduces this risk, which is part of why diagnosis and lifelong treatment matter. Hepatocellular carcinoma is linked to cirrhosis from chronic hepatitis B/C, alcohol, and metabolic liver disease—not celiac disease. Renal cell carcinoma associates with smoking, obesity, and von Hippel-Lindau disease. Prostate adenocarcinoma is driven by age and androgen exposure, unrelated to celiac disease. New or worsening symptoms despite strict dietary adherence (refractory celiac disease) should prompt evaluation for these complications and for dietary noncompliance, the most common reason for persistent symptoms.

cases.diagnosis: Celiac disease
pearls: tTG-IgA with a total IgA level is first-line serology; check IgA because selective IgA deficiency causes false negatives.
Confirm with duodenal biopsy (villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, intraepithelial lymphocytosis) while the patient still eats gluten.
Dermatitis herpetiformis (itchy extensor vesicles) and iron deficiency refractory to oral iron are classic clues.
Treatment is a lifelong strict gluten-free diet; untreated disease raises the risk of enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma.

cases.title: A Confused Man Brought In Unsteady and Seeing Double
cases.chiefComplaint: Brought in by EMS confused, unsteady on his feet, with his eyes not moving right.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man is brought to the ED by friends after several days of worsening confusion, unsteady gait, and complaints of double vision. They report he has eaten poorly for weeks and has been drinking heavily—roughly a pint of vodka daily for years—after losing his job. He vomited multiple times over the past week and has barely eaten. He has had no head trauma, fever, or seizures per collateral. PMH: alcohol use disorder, prior alcohol withdrawal, hypertension. Medications: none taken reliably; reportedly prescribed thiamine in the past but not taking it. Allergies: none known. Social: heavy daily alcohol use, 1 pack-per-day smoker, lives alone, poor nutrition. Family history: noncontributory. On arrival he is inattentive, disoriented to time and place, and cannot give a coherent history. Friends note he has been increasingly forgetful and 'making things up.' He has not had recent benzodiazepine use that they know of. No focal weakness was noted by EMS. He appears cachectic and malnourished.
cases.exam: Cachectic, disheveled man, inattentive and disoriented to time and place. Eyes: bilateral horizontal gaze-evoked nystagmus with a partial abducens (lateral rectus) palsy producing diplopia—ophthalmoplegia present. Pupils equal and reactive. Gait: wide-based and markedly ataxic, unable to walk unassisted; truncal instability. No focal limb weakness; reflexes symmetric. Cranial nerves otherwise intact. No meningismus. No asterixis or jaundice. Cardiac and pulmonary exams unremarkable aside from sinus tachycardia. No obvious head trauma.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A point-of-care glucose returns 58 mg/dL. In this malnourished alcohol-dependent patient, what must be done before or simultaneously with administering glucose?
options: Give IV dextrose immediately and defer all other treatment
Administer IV thiamine before (or together with) glucose
Administer IV phosphate first
Give oral glucose tablets and recheck in one hour
stages.explanation: In a thiamine-deficient patient, giving glucose without thiamine can precipitate or worsen Wernicke encephalopathy because thiamine is a required cofactor for glucose metabolism (pyruvate dehydrogenase, transketolase, alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase); a glucose load consumes the last thiamine reserves. Therefore IV thiamine should be given before or concurrently with glucose. Giving dextrose alone first is the classic error that can trigger irreversible neurologic injury. Phosphate repletion matters in refeeding but is not the immediate priority over thiamine here. Oral glucose with delayed recheck is inadequate for symptomatic hypoglycemia and ignores the thiamine imperative. Empiric high-dose IV thiamine is safe and should not be delayed for confirmatory testing.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The triad of confusion (encephalopathy), ophthalmoplegia/nystagmus, and ataxia in this patient is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Hepatic encephalopathy
Wernicke encephalopathy (thiamine deficiency)
Cerebellar hemorrhage
Alcohol withdrawal delirium (delirium tremens)
stages.explanation: The classic triad of encephalopathy, oculomotor dysfunction (nystagmus, lateral rectus palsy, conjugate gaze palsies), and gait ataxia in a malnourished alcohol-dependent patient defines Wernicke encephalopathy from thiamine (B1) deficiency—though the full triad is present in a minority, so a low threshold for treatment is essential. Hepatic encephalopathy causes confusion and asterixis, typically without ophthalmoplegia, and is suggested by liver disease/hyperammonemia. Cerebellar hemorrhage causes acute ataxia but usually with headache and focal signs on imaging, not this subacute triad with poor nutrition. Delirium tremens features autonomic hyperactivity, tremor, agitation, and hallucinations 48-96 hours after the last drink, without the characteristic ophthalmoplegia.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Glucose 58 mg/dL (corrected)
Mg 1.4 mg/dL (low)
Na 137 mEq/L, K 3.6 mEq/L
Phosphate 2.2 mg/dL (low-normal)
AST 90 U/L, ALT 45 U/L (AST:ALT >2)
Ammonia: mildly elevated
MRI brain: symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the mammillary bodies and medial thalami
CT head: no hemorrhage or mass

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate treatment regimen for confirmed Wernicke encephalopathy?
options: Low-dose oral thiamine 100 mg daily for one week
High-dose IV thiamine (e.g., 500 mg IV three times daily) plus magnesium repletion
Lactulose to lower ammonia
Benzodiazepine taper alone
stages.explanation: Treatment is high-dose parenteral thiamine—commonly 500 mg IV three times daily for several days, then tapering—because oral absorption is unreliable and the deficit is profound. Magnesium is a cofactor for thiamine-dependent enzymes, so concurrent hypomagnesemia must be corrected or thiamine repletion may fail. Low-dose oral thiamine is inadequate for acute Wernicke encephalopathy and risks progression to irreversible Korsakoff syndrome. Lactulose treats hepatic encephalopathy, not thiamine deficiency, and would not address this triad. A benzodiazepine taper manages alcohol withdrawal but does not treat the underlying thiamine deficiency causing his neurologic findings. Prompt high-dose thiamine can reverse ophthalmoplegia within hours and prevent permanent memory impairment.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If treatment is delayed or inadequate, this patient is at greatest risk of progressing to which chronic, often irreversible condition?
options: Korsakoff syndrome (anterograde amnesia with confabulation)
Multi-infarct dementia
Normal pressure hydrocephalus
Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease
stages.explanation: Untreated or undertreated Wernicke encephalopathy progresses to Korsakoff syndrome, a chronic amnestic disorder marked by severe anterograde (and some retrograde) amnesia with confabulation, reflecting permanent damage to the mammillary bodies and diencephalon. This is why empiric thiamine must be given urgently—the early friends' report of him 'making things up' already hints at memory involvement. Multi-infarct (vascular) dementia results from cumulative strokes and stepwise decline, unrelated to thiamine. Normal pressure hydrocephalus presents with the triad of gait apraxia, urinary incontinence, and dementia with ventriculomegaly. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease is a rapidly progressive prion dementia with myoclonus and characteristic MRI/EEG findings, not a complication of thiamine deficiency.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After thiamine is started and he is stabilized, the team begins refeeding the malnourished patient. Which complication should be anticipated and monitored?
options: Refeeding syndrome with hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, and hypomagnesemia
Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state
Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome
Contrast-induced nephropathy
stages.explanation: Reintroducing nutrition to a chronically malnourished patient triggers an insulin surge that drives phosphate, potassium, and magnesium intracellularly, producing refeeding syndrome—hypophosphatemia is the hallmark and can cause cardiac arrhythmias, respiratory failure, and rhabdomyolysis. He is high-risk (cachexia, alcohol use, poor intake), so electrolytes must be monitored and repleted with cautious, gradual caloric advancement and continued thiamine. Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state is a complication of uncontrolled diabetes, not refeeding a thiamine-deficient alcoholic. Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome occurs after starting antiretroviral therapy in HIV, irrelevant here. Contrast-induced nephropathy follows iodinated contrast administration and is unrelated to nutritional repletion. Anticipating refeeding syndrome and pre-emptively correcting electrolytes prevents life-threatening complications.

cases.diagnosis: Wernicke encephalopathy (thiamine deficiency)
pearls: Wernicke triad: encephalopathy + ophthalmoplegia/nystagmus + ataxia; the full triad is present in a minority—treat empirically.
Give thiamine before or with glucose in at-risk patients; a glucose load can precipitate or worsen Wernicke encephalopathy.
Treat with high-dose IV thiamine and correct hypomagnesemia (a required cofactor) for thiamine to work.
Untreated Wernicke can progress to irreversible Korsakoff amnesia with confabulation—mammillary body damage on MRI.

cases.title: Agitation, Sweating, and Twitchy Legs After a Medication Change
cases.chiefComplaint: I'm agitated, sweating, and my legs keep jerking on their own.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman is brought to the ED with abrupt onset of agitation, restlessness, profuse sweating, and involuntary leg jerking that began several hours ago. She has a history of major depressive disorder on a stable dose of sertraline. Three days ago her psychiatrist added an additional medication; today she also took an over-the-counter cough preparation containing dextromethorphan and one tramadol tablet borrowed from a friend for a headache. She denies recreational stimulant use, fevers preceding the episode, neck stiffness, or recent infection. PMH: depression, migraines. Medications: sertraline 100 mg daily; recently added agent (per record, linezolid prescribed by an outside clinician for a skin infection). Allergies: none. Social: occasional alcohol, no illicit drugs, works as a graphic designer. Family history: noncontributory. She reports nausea, shivering, and a racing heart. She is anxious and intermittently confused but arousable. No recent head trauma. She has not started any antipsychotics. Her symptoms are worsening over the past hour.
cases.exam: Anxious, diaphoretic young woman, restless and tremulous. Mydriasis (dilated pupils) present. Neurologic exam notable for inducible and spontaneous clonus, most prominent in the lower extremities; ocular clonus noted; hyperreflexia (3+) with symmetric reflexes; mild generalized rigidity that is greater in the lower extremities than upper. No nuchal rigidity. Bowel sounds hyperactive. Skin warm and moist. Mucous membranes moist. No focal weakness. She is oriented but distractible and intermittently agitated. No rash.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which clinical feature is most specific for serotonin syndrome and helps distinguish it from neuroleptic malignant syndrome?
options: Lead-pipe rigidity with bradyreflexia developing over days
Neuromuscular hyperactivity with clonus and hyperreflexia, especially lower-extremity predominant
Diffuse urticarial rash with mucosal involvement
Profound hyporeflexia with flaccid paralysis
stages.explanation: Serotonin syndrome is characterized by neuromuscular hyperactivity—clonus (spontaneous, inducible, or ocular), hyperreflexia, tremor, and rigidity that is typically lower-extremity predominant—developing rapidly (within 24 hours) after a serotonergic change. Clonus and hyperreflexia are the most specific findings and form the basis of the Hunter Criteria. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome instead causes 'lead-pipe' rigidity with hyporeflexia/bradyreflexia, evolving over days after dopamine antagonist exposure. A diffuse urticarial rash with mucosal involvement suggests a drug hypersensitivity reaction, not serotonin toxicity. Profound hyporeflexia and flaccid paralysis are inconsistent with the hyperkinetic, hyperreflexic state of serotonin syndrome. The medication history (multiple serotonergic agents) supports the diagnosis.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: Which combination in this patient's medication history most likely precipitated her presentation?
options: Sertraline combined with linezolid (an MAO inhibitor), tramadol, and dextromethorphan
Amlodipine and lisinopril
Acetaminophen and ibuprofen
Levothyroxine and metformin
stages.explanation: Serotonin syndrome arises from excess serotonergic activity, often from combining agents. Here sertraline (an SSRI) plus linezolid (a reversible MAO inhibitor antibiotic), plus tramadol and dextromethorphan (both serotonergic), create a high-risk combination—a classic and frequently tested precipitant. Calcium channel blockers and ACE inhibitors (amlodipine/lisinopril) are antihypertensives without serotonergic activity. Acetaminophen and ibuprofen are analgesics/antipyretics that do not raise serotonin. Levothyroxine and metformin treat hypothyroidism and diabetes and are not serotonergic. Recognizing occult serotonergic drugs—linezolid, tramadol, dextromethorphan, meperidine, triptans, MDMA, St. John's wort—is key to identifying the cause and stopping all offending agents.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
CK 620 U/L (mildly elevated)
WBC 11.0 ×10³/µL
Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.0 mEq/L, Cr 0.9 mg/dL
Lactate 1.8 mmol/L
CSF (lumbar puncture performed to exclude meningitis): normal cell count, normal protein/glucose
Urine toxicology: negative for cocaine/amphetamines
ECG: sinus tachycardia, normal QTc

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of initial management for this patient with moderate serotonin syndrome?
options: Dantrolene as first-line therapy
Stop all serotonergic agents, provide supportive care (IV fluids, cooling), and give benzodiazepines for agitation/clonus
Administer a dopamine antagonist such as haloperidol
Start an SSRI at a higher dose to overcome receptor tolerance
stages.explanation: Management is primarily supportive: immediately discontinue all serotonergic drugs, provide IV fluids, active cooling for hyperthermia, and benzodiazepines to control agitation, tremor, and clonus and to blunt autonomic instability. Most cases resolve within 24 hours with these measures. Dantrolene is used for malignant hyperthermia/NMS rigidity and is not first-line for serotonin syndrome. Haloperidol and other antipsychotics with anticholinergic/dopaminergic effects can impair heat dissipation and worsen hyperthermia, so they are avoided. Increasing serotonergic dosing would worsen the syndrome. For severe or refractory cases, the serotonin antagonist cyproheptadine is added; severe hyperthermia (>41.1 °C) is a medical emergency requiring sedation, paralysis, and intubation with aggressive cooling.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Her agitation and clonus persist despite benzodiazepines and supportive care. What is the appropriate next pharmacologic step?
options: Cyproheptadine (a serotonin 5-HT2A antagonist)
Bromocriptine (a dopamine agonist)
Physostigmine
Naloxone infusion
stages.explanation: When benzodiazepines and supportive measures are insufficient, cyproheptadine—a histamine-1 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptor antagonist—is the recommended antidote for serotonin syndrome; it directly counteracts excess serotonergic activity. Bromocriptine is a dopamine agonist used in neuroleptic malignant syndrome and can paradoxically worsen serotonin syndrome. Physostigmine is an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor used for anticholinergic toxicity, which presents with dry skin, absent bowel sounds, and urinary retention—opposite to this patient's diaphoresis and hyperactive bowel sounds. Naloxone reverses opioid toxicity (respiratory depression, miosis), not serotonin excess. The clinical picture of clonus, hyperreflexia, diaphoresis, and hyperthermia after serotonergic agents firmly supports serotonin syndrome and cyproheptadine as the targeted therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Serotonin syndrome
pearls: Hunter criteria: clonus (spontaneous/inducible/ocular), hyperreflexia, and lower-extremity-predominant rigidity after a serotonergic agent.
Serotonin syndrome is fast (hours) with hyperreflexia/clonus; NMS is slow (days) with lead-pipe rigidity and hyporeflexia.
Watch for hidden serotonergic drugs: tramadol, linezolid, dextromethorphan, meperidine, triptans, MDMA, St. John's wort.
Treat by stopping offending drugs + supportive care + benzodiazepines; add cyproheptadine if refractory.

cases.title: Severe Muscle Pain and Dark Urine After a Long Night on the Floor
cases.chiefComplaint: My muscles are killing me and my urine looks like cola.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man admitted to the medical ward reports severe, diffuse muscle pain, weakness, and tea-colored urine. He was found down at home by his roommate after an apparent alcohol binge and is thought to have been immobile on a hard floor for at least 12 hours overnight. He recalls leg and back muscle aches and noticed dark urine this morning. He denies chest pain, prior kidney disease, or recent strenuous exercise beyond the prolonged immobilization. PMH: alcohol use disorder; otherwise healthy. Medications: none routinely; he recently started a statin per his roommate but adherence is unclear; no other nephrotoxins. Allergies: none. Social: heavy episodic alcohol use, occasional cocaine in the past, smokes half a pack daily. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems notable for decreased urine output today and nausea. He has not had seizures. He denies crush injury beyond the prolonged pressure of lying immobile. He is mildly confused but cooperative. He has had no fevers. He took ibuprofen for the muscle pain this morning.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable young man lying still due to pain. Diffuse muscle tenderness, particularly in the thighs, calves, and lower back, with mild swelling of the calves; no compartment tense enough for immediate fasciotomy on initial assessment but compartments should be monitored. Strength limited by pain. No focal neurologic deficit. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmurs. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft. Skin shows a pressure-related erythematous area over the sacrum/hip. No rash. Mucous membranes slightly dry. Urine in the catheter bag is dark, tea/cola-colored.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His urine dipstick is strongly positive for blood, but microscopy shows few or no red blood cells. What does this discrepancy most likely indicate?
options: Glomerulonephritis with dysmorphic RBCs
Myoglobinuria from muscle breakdown (the dipstick cross-reacts with myoglobin)
A contaminated specimen
Bladder cancer with gross hematuria
stages.explanation: A urine dipstick positive for blood with few or no RBCs on microscopy is the classic clue to myoglobinuria: the heme-based dipstick detects both hemoglobin and myoglobin, so released myoglobin from damaged muscle turns it positive without actual red cells. This fits rhabdomyolysis after prolonged immobilization. Glomerulonephritis would show dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts on microscopy, not their absence. A contaminated specimen would not consistently explain the systemic picture of muscle pain and tea-colored urine. Bladder cancer causes true hematuria with RBCs present on microscopy. Recognizing this dipstick-microscopy mismatch should prompt a creatine kinase level and evaluation for rhabdomyolysis-induced acute kidney injury.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Creatine kinase (CK) 38,000 U/L (markedly elevated)
Creatinine 2.6 mg/dL (baseline ~0.9)
K 6.1 mEq/L (elevated)
Phosphate 6.4 mg/dL (elevated)
Calcium 7.6 mg/dL (low)
Bicarbonate 18 mEq/L
Urinalysis: dipstick large blood, microscopy 0-2 RBC/hpf, pigmented granular casts
Uric acid elevated; ECG: peaked T waves

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The markedly elevated CK, pigmented casts, and electrolyte derangements confirm which diagnosis and its key complication?
options: Acute interstitial nephritis from ibuprofen
Rhabdomyolysis with acute kidney injury
Hemolytic uremic syndrome
Acute glomerulonephritis
stages.explanation: A CK over 5,000 U/L (here 38,000) with myoglobinuria, pigmented granular casts, and a rising creatinine confirms rhabdomyolysis complicated by acute kidney injury—myoglobin causes tubular obstruction, direct tubular toxicity, and renal vasoconstriction. The characteristic electrolyte pattern is hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia, hyperuricemia, and metabolic acidosis, all present here. Acute interstitial nephritis (e.g., from NSAIDs) typically shows pyuria/WBC casts and sometimes eosinophiluria, not massive CK elevation. Hemolytic uremic syndrome features microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and AKI, with true RBC fragmentation. Acute glomerulonephritis presents with dysmorphic RBCs and RBC casts. The found-down, immobilized history with muscle pain and cola-colored urine points clearly to rhabdomyolysis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment to prevent and treat rhabdomyolysis-induced acute kidney injury?
options: Early aggressive IV isotonic crystalloid fluid resuscitation
Immediate loop diuretics to force diuresis
Fluid restriction to prevent volume overload
Empiric hemodialysis for all patients with elevated CK
stages.explanation: Early, aggressive IV isotonic fluid resuscitation is the cornerstone: it restores intravascular volume, increases renal perfusion, and flushes myoglobin through the tubules to limit pigment nephropathy. Large volumes titrated to urine output are typically required. Loop diuretics do not prevent AKI and may worsen volume depletion and intratubular obstruction if given before adequate hydration; they are not used to 'force' diuresis early. Fluid restriction is harmful—these patients are volume-depleted and need repletion. Hemodialysis is reserved for refractory hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, volume overload, or established renal failure unresponsive to medical therapy, not as empiric therapy for all elevated CK. The role of urinary alkalinization and mannitol remains debated and is secondary to volume repletion.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: His potassium is 6.1 mEq/L with peaked T waves on ECG. What is the immediate first step?
options: Administer IV calcium gluconate to stabilize the cardiac membrane
Give oral potassium binder and recheck in 4 hours
Start a potassium-sparing diuretic
Transfuse packed red blood cells
stages.explanation: With hyperkalemia and ECG changes (peaked T waves), the immediate priority is IV calcium gluconate (or chloride) to stabilize the myocardial membrane and prevent fatal arrhythmia; it works within minutes though it does not lower potassium. This is followed by therapies that shift potassium intracellularly (insulin with glucose, beta-agonists, and bicarbonate if acidotic) and then remove potassium (potassium binders, dialysis). An oral binder alone with delayed recheck is too slow for ECG changes. A potassium-sparing diuretic would worsen hyperkalemia and is contraindicated. Transfusion does not treat hyperkalemia and stored blood can add a potassium load. In rhabdomyolysis, ongoing muscle breakdown can cause recurrent hyperkalemia, so frequent monitoring and definitive treatment, including dialysis if refractory, are essential.

cases.diagnosis: Rhabdomyolysis with acute kidney injury
pearls: Dipstick blood-positive but microscopy showing no RBCs = myoglobinuria—check creatine kinase for rhabdomyolysis.
Classic labs: very high CK, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia, hyperuricemia, metabolic acidosis, AKI.
Early aggressive IV isotonic fluids are the mainstay to prevent pigment nephropathy; avoid early diuretics.
For hyperkalemia with ECG changes, give IV calcium first to stabilize the membrane, then shift and remove potassium.

cases.title: Worsening Kidney Numbers and a Dangerous Potassium After Treatment
cases.chiefComplaint: Transferred to the ICU after blood work turned dangerously abnormal two days into treatment.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man with newly diagnosed high-grade B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma (bulky abdominal disease, high tumor burden) was admitted for his first cycle of chemotherapy. About 48 hours after the first dose he developed nausea, muscle cramps, decreased urine output, and lethargy, prompting ICU transfer. He reports palpitations and twitching. He denies chest pain or prior renal disease. PMH: lymphoma; otherwise healthy. Medications: chemotherapy initiated; he was started on a hypouricemic agent and IV fluids at admission, though the exact prophylaxis is being reviewed. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, rare alcohol, works in construction. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems notable for new perioral tingling and carpopedal-type cramping. He has not vomited blood. He has no fevers currently. Collateral confirms a very high pretreatment LDH and a large tumor burden. He is anxious and uncomfortable but oriented. His urine output over the last 8 hours has been minimal despite fluids.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable middle-aged man, mildly lethargic but oriented. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular; no murmurs or rub. Lungs clear. Abdomen: fullness with a palpable bulky mass and mild diffuse tenderness, no rebound. Neuromuscular: positive Trousseau and Chvostek signs suggesting hypocalcemia; mild diffuse muscle twitching. No focal neurologic deficit. Extremities without edema. Skin without rash. He appears mildly volume-replete on exam but with decreased urine output noted from the catheter. No asterixis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Two days after chemotherapy for high-burden lymphoma, which laboratory tetrad would confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia, hypercalcemia, and low uric acid
Hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia, and hyperuricemia
Hypernatremia, hyperchloremia, metabolic alkalosis, and high bicarbonate
Isolated hypoglycemia with normal renal function
stages.explanation: Tumor lysis syndrome (TLS) follows the rapid breakdown of malignant cells (spontaneous or after chemotherapy), releasing intracellular contents into the blood: hyperkalemia (potassium), hyperphosphatemia (phosphate), and hyperuricemia (nucleic acid breakdown to uric acid), with consequent hypocalcemia from calcium-phosphate precipitation. This tetrad, often with acute kidney injury, defines TLS and matches his Trousseau/Chvostek signs (hypocalcemia) and oliguria. The opposite pattern (hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia, hypercalcemia, low uric acid) is nonsensical for TLS. A hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis pattern fits vomiting or diuretics, not cell lysis. Isolated hypoglycemia with normal kidneys does not describe TLS. High-grade lymphomas, leukemias, and bulky chemosensitive tumors with elevated LDH are highest risk.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
K 6.4 mEq/L (elevated)
Phosphate 7.8 mg/dL (elevated)
Calcium 6.9 mg/dL (low; corrected)
Uric acid 14 mg/dL (elevated)
Creatinine 2.8 mg/dL (baseline 0.9)
LDH markedly elevated
ECG: peaked T waves, widening QRS
Urine output: ~15 mL/hour despite fluids

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a uric acid of 14 mg/dL with established TLS and AKI, which agent is most appropriate to rapidly lower the uric acid?
options: Allopurinol
Rasburicase
Probenecid
Colchicine
stages.explanation: Rasburicase, a recombinant urate oxidase, enzymatically converts existing uric acid to the more soluble allantoin, rapidly lowering established hyperuricemia—making it the agent of choice in high-risk or established TLS with markedly elevated uric acid and AKI. Allopurinol, a xanthine oxidase inhibitor, only prevents new uric acid formation and does not reduce the existing pool, so it is used for prophylaxis or lower-risk patients, not for rapid reduction of an already high level. Probenecid is a uricosuric that increases renal uric acid excretion and is contraindicated in TLS/AKI, as it can worsen urate nephropathy. Colchicine treats acute gout inflammation, not TLS hyperuricemia. Note: rasburicase is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency due to hemolysis risk.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: His potassium is 6.4 mEq/L with peaked T waves and a widening QRS. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Oral phosphate binder
IV calcium gluconate followed by insulin with dextrose
IV calcium plus oral phosphate to correct hypocalcemia aggressively
Observation with repeat labs in 6 hours
stages.explanation: Life-threatening hyperkalemia with ECG changes requires immediate IV calcium gluconate to stabilize the cardiac membrane, followed by insulin with dextrose (and a beta-agonist) to shift potassium intracellularly, then measures to remove potassium (binders, dialysis). A phosphate binder addresses hyperphosphatemia but does not treat acute hyperkalemia or its ECG threat. Although the patient is hypocalcemic, aggressive IV calcium correction in the setting of hyperphosphatemia risks calcium-phosphate precipitation and tissue/renal damage—so calcium is given for membrane stabilization or symptomatic/severe hypocalcemia, cautiously, while prioritizing phosphate control. Observation is unsafe with QRS widening. Given oliguria refractory to fluids plus severe metabolic derangements, urgent nephrology consultation for hemodialysis is warranted.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: For future high-risk patients, which strategy best prevents tumor lysis syndrome before initiating chemotherapy?
options: Fluid restriction and potassium supplementation
Aggressive IV hydration plus risk-stratified hypouricemic prophylaxis (allopurinol or rasburicase) with close monitoring
Routine urinary alkalinization for all patients
Prophylactic calcium and phosphate supplementation
stages.explanation: Prevention centers on identifying high-risk patients (high tumor burden, high-grade lymphoma/leukemia, elevated LDH, pre-existing renal impairment) and providing aggressive IV hydration to maintain urine output, plus risk-stratified hypouricemic prophylaxis—allopurinol for intermediate risk and rasburicase for high risk—with frequent monitoring of electrolytes, uric acid, and renal function. Fluid restriction and potassium supplementation would worsen TLS. Routine urinary alkalinization is no longer recommended for all patients because it can promote calcium-phosphate precipitation and does not improve outcomes when rasburicase is used. Prophylactic calcium and phosphate supplementation is inappropriate—phosphate is already elevated in TLS and supplementing it would be harmful. Hydration plus the right hypouricemic agent and vigilant monitoring is the evidence-based approach.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Despite IV calcium, insulin/dextrose, and continued fluids, he remains oliguric with K 6.5 mEq/L and worsening acidosis. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Initiate hemodialysis
Administer a large bolus of IV potassium-free fluid and observe overnight
Give a single dose of oral sodium bicarbonate and discharge from the ICU
Start a thiazide diuretic to increase potassium excretion
stages.explanation: When TLS causes refractory hyperkalemia, severe metabolic derangement, oliguric acute kidney injury unresponsive to medical therapy, and volume the kidneys cannot clear, hemodialysis is indicated—it rapidly removes potassium, phosphate, and uric acid and corrects acidosis. Persistent hyperkalemia despite membrane stabilization and shifting therapy in an oliguric patient is a classic dialysis indication. Simply giving more fluid will not correct hyperkalemia in an oliguric patient and risks volume overload. A single oral bicarbonate dose with ICU discharge is grossly inadequate and unsafe for ongoing life-threatening electrolyte abnormalities. Thiazide diuretics are weak, ineffective in significant renal impairment, and do not reliably remove the dangerous potassium load. Early nephrology involvement for renal replacement therapy is essential in severe TLS.

cases.diagnosis: Tumor lysis syndrome
pearls: TLS tetrad: hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hyperuricemia, and hypocalcemia, often with AKI, after treating bulky chemosensitive tumors.
Rasburicase rapidly degrades existing uric acid (contraindicated in G6PD deficiency); allopurinol only prevents new formation.
Treat hyperkalemia with ECG changes immediately: IV calcium to stabilize, then insulin/dextrose to shift, then remove potassium.
Refractory hyperkalemia, severe acidosis, or oliguric AKI in TLS are indications for urgent hemodialysis.

cases.title: A Red, Painful Leg
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening redness, warmth, and pain of the right lower leg over three days.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man presents to the ED with three days of progressive redness, warmth, and tenderness over his right shin and calf. It began as a small red patch near a scratch he sustained while gardening and has since spread upward. He reports subjective fevers and chills since yesterday. He denies recent immobility, long travel, surgery, or calf swelling preceding the redness. There is no chest pain, dyspnea, or hemoptysis. He has type 2 diabetes managed with metformin 1000 mg twice daily and hypertension on lisinopril 20 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He works as a landscaper, drinks alcohol socially, and does not smoke or use IV drugs. He has had no prior episodes of leg infection or DVT. Family history is notable for coronary artery disease in his father. He has not traveled recently and has no sick contacts. He recalls minor trauma to the area but no puncture wound, animal bite, or freshwater/saltwater exposure.
cases.exam: Alert, mildly uncomfortable. Right lower leg with a confluent area of erythema, warmth, and tenderness over the anterior shin and medial calf, with indistinct borders and mild non-pitting edema. No fluctuance, crepitus, bullae, or skin necrosis. Pain is proportionate to exam findings, not out of proportion. A small healing abrasion is visible centrally. Distal pulses are intact, capillary refill brisk. Calf is soft without a palpable cord; Homans sign negative. No inguinal lymphadenopathy is appreciated. The contralateral leg is normal. No tracking lymphangitic streaks above the knee.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly favors cellulitis over deep vein thrombosis in this patient?
options: Unilateral leg swelling
Warmth and tenderness of the limb
Sharply demarcated advancing erythema with a portal of entry and fever
A history of recent minor trauma
stages.explanation: Advancing erythema with a skin portal of entry plus systemic fever points to a superficial soft-tissue infection rather than DVT. DVT classically causes swelling, warmth, and tenderness but does NOT produce spreading cutaneous erythema or a portal of entry, and fever is uncommon. Unilateral swelling (option A) and warmth/tenderness (option B) overlap heavily between the two and cannot discriminate. Recent minor trauma (option D) is nonspecific—it can precede both a DVT and a skin infection. The combination of an entry wound, erythema with systemic signs, and absence of a cord makes cellulitis far more likely, though Doppler ultrasound is warranted if DVT cannot be excluded clinically.

stages.label: RED FLAGS
stages.question: Which finding would most raise concern for necrotizing soft-tissue infection requiring urgent surgical evaluation?
options: Mild non-pitting edema of the calf
Pain out of proportion to exam, with rapid progression and skin crepitus
Low-grade fever of 38.1 °C
A small healing abrasion at the site
stages.explanation: Pain out of proportion to physical findings, fulminant progression over hours, crepitus, bullae, skin anesthesia, or systemic toxicity are hallmark red flags for necrotizing fasciitis—a surgical emergency requiring emergent debridement, not antibiotics alone. Mild edema (option A) is expected in uncomplicated cellulitis. Low-grade fever (option C) is common in simple cellulitis and not specific for necrotizing infection. A healing abrasion (option D) merely identifies the likely portal of entry. This patient's pain is proportionate and there is no crepitus or necrosis, arguing against a necrotizing process, but clinicians must reassess frequently because deterioration can be rapid.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 13.8 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
Hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL
Platelets 265 ×10³/µL
Sodium 139 mEq/L
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Glucose 168 mg/dL
CRP 64 mg/L
Lactate 1.3 mmol/L
Venous Doppler right leg: no deep vein thrombosis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The Doppler is negative for DVT and the leg shows non-purulent cellulitis. What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic strategy for this stable patient?
options: Oral or IV beta-lactam targeting beta-hemolytic streptococci (e.g., cephalexin or cefazolin)
Oral metronidazole alone
Vancomycin plus piperacillin-tazobactam plus clindamycin
No antibiotics; elevate the leg and observe
stages.explanation: Non-purulent cellulitis (no abscess or purulent drainage) is most often caused by beta-hemolytic streptococci, so a beta-lactam such as cephalexin (oral) or cefazolin (IV) is first-line. Metronidazole (option B) covers anaerobes, not the relevant streptococci/staph, and is inappropriate as monotherapy. Broad triple therapy with vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam, and clindamycin (option C) is reserved for necrotizing infection or severe sepsis—overkill and harmful here. Withholding antibiotics (option D) is unsafe given systemic signs and spreading erythema. MRSA coverage is added only if there is purulence, penetrating trauma, or risk factors; this patient lacks those features.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After a dose of IV cefazolin, the patient feels better and is afebrile. Which is the most appropriate disposition plan?
options: Admit to the ICU for vasopressor monitoring
Discharge home on oral cephalexin with skin-marker demarcation, elevation, follow-up in 48 hours, and return precautions
Discharge with no antibiotics and topical antifungal cream
Admit for IV vancomycin and surgical consultation
stages.explanation: A hemodynamically stable patient with non-purulent cellulitis who responds to initial therapy can be managed as an outpatient on oral cephalexin, with the erythema border marked to track progression, leg elevation, 48-hour follow-up, and clear return precautions (expanding redness, fever, severe pain). ICU admission (option A) is unwarranted without hemodynamic instability or sepsis. No antibiotics with antifungal cream (option C) ignores the bacterial infection. IV vancomycin plus surgery (option D) overtreats—there are no MRSA risk factors, abscess, or necrotizing features. Treating his diabetes-related hyperglycemia and counseling on glycemic control also support healing.

cases.diagnosis: Non-purulent cellulitis of the right lower leg (DVT and necrotizing infection excluded)
pearls: Non-purulent cellulitis is usually streptococcal—treat with a beta-lactam; reserve MRSA coverage for purulence, penetrating trauma, or specific risk factors.
Pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, or rapid progression should trigger immediate evaluation for necrotizing fasciitis and surgical consultation.
DVT causes swelling, warmth, and tenderness but not spreading erythema or a portal of entry; use Doppler ultrasound when DVT cannot be clinically excluded.
Mark the erythema border and arrange 48-hour follow-up; failure to improve suggests resistant organisms, abscess, or an alternative diagnosis.
