VATS (C) is for recurrent or persistent cases, not first presentation. Discharging without observation or repeat imaging (D) is unsafe because the pneumothorax could enlarge.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: After successful management, what counseling most reduces his risk of recurrence?
options: Avoid air travel permanently
Begin daily inhaled corticosteroids
Smoking cessation
Restrict all physical activity indefinitely
stages.explanation: Smoking dramatically increases the risk of primary spontaneous pneumothorax and recurrence; cessation is the single most important modifiable intervention, and he should be strongly counseled to quit. Permanent avoidance of air travel (A) is not required—patients are advised to avoid flying only until full resolution (typically 1-2 weeks after radiographic clearance). Inhaled corticosteroids (B) have no role in preventing recurrence. Indefinite activity restriction (D) is unnecessary; patients are generally advised to avoid scuba diving but can resume normal activity after recovery.

cases.diagnosis: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax
pearls: Tall, thin young male smokers are the classic demographic for primary spontaneous pneumothorax from apical bleb rupture.
Hypotension, tracheal deviation, and distended neck veins signal tension pneumothorax requiring immediate needle decompression—don't wait for imaging.
Small (<2 cm), minimally symptomatic pneumothoraces can be observed with supplemental oxygen; larger or symptomatic ones need aspiration or a chest tube.
Smoking cessation is the most important step to reduce recurrence risk.

cases.title: Worsening Breathlessness After Pneumonia
cases.chiefComplaint: Increasing shortness of breath and right-sided chest discomfort several days into treatment for pneumonia.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man was admitted four days ago with community-acquired pneumonia and started on ceftriaxone and azithromycin. His fever initially improved, but over the last 48 hours he has developed worsening dyspnea, a low-grade fever, and dull right-sided chest pain that is worse with deep breathing. PMH includes poorly controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, and the inpatient antibiotics. He has no known drug allergies. He smoked for 20 years and quit a decade ago; he drinks one or two beers weekly. He works as a warehouse supervisor. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for fatigue, decreased appetite, and a productive cough; he denies hemoptysis, leg swelling, or orthopnea. He reports feeling worse rather than better despite antibiotics, which prompted reassessment.
cases.exam: Mildly tachypneic man in no acute distress at rest. Examination of the right lower chest reveals dullness to percussion, decreased breath sounds, decreased tactile fremitus, and reduced chest expansion on that side. Egophony is noted just above the area of dullness. The left lung is clear. No tracheal deviation. Heart sounds are regular and tachycardic without murmur or rub. Abdomen soft and nontender. No peripheral edema or calf tenderness. No jugular venous distension.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The exam suggests a right pleural effusion complicating his pneumonia. What is the best initial imaging step to confirm and characterize it?
options: Chest radiograph, with a lateral decubitus film or ultrasound to assess layering
CT pulmonary angiogram
Immediate chest tube placement
Echocardiography
stages.explanation: A chest radiograph confirms the effusion (blunting of the costophrenic angle, meniscus sign), and a lateral decubitus film or, preferably, bedside ultrasound assesses whether fluid layers freely versus is loculated—guiding safe thoracentesis. CT pulmonary angiogram (B) evaluates PE, not the suspected parapneumonic effusion. Immediate chest tube placement (C) is premature before characterizing the fluid; not all parapneumonic effusions require drainage. Echocardiography (D) assesses cardiac causes of effusion, but the clinical context (pneumonia, fever, focal findings) points to a parapneumonic process, so imaging the effusion directly is the priority.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Chest X-ray: moderate right pleural effusion blunting the costophrenic angle, with associated right lower lobe consolidation
Bedside ultrasound: moderate right pleural effusion with internal septations and some echogenic material; fluid is partially loculated
No pericardial effusion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Diagnostic thoracentesis is performed. Which set of pleural fluid findings would, by Light's criteria, classify the effusion as an EXUDATE?
options: Pleural-to-serum protein ratio 0.3 and pleural-to-serum LDH ratio 0.4
Pleural fluid protein 1.5 g/dL with serum protein 7 g/dL
Pleural-to-serum protein ratio >0.5, or pleural-to-serum LDH ratio >0.6, or pleural LDH >2/3 upper limit of normal serum LDH
Pleural fluid glucose 90 mg/dL with pH 7.45
stages.explanation: Light's criteria identify an exudate if ANY one is met: pleural/serum protein ratio >0.5, pleural/serum LDH ratio >0.6, or pleural LDH >2/3 the upper limit of normal serum LDH. Parapneumonic effusions are exudative. Option A describes ratios consistent with a transudate. Option B (low pleural protein relative to serum) suggests a transudate. Option D describes normal glucose and alkaline pH typical of a simple, non-complicated effusion and does not define exudate status. Recognizing the exudate is the first step; further analysis (pH, glucose, Gram stain/culture) then determines whether the effusion is complicated.

stages.label: PLEURAL FLUID ANALYSIS
stages.content:
Appearance: turbid
Pleural protein 4.8 g/dL (serum 6.6 g/dL)
Pleural LDH 1,050 U/L (serum LDH 220 U/L; ULN 230 U/L)
Pleural fluid pH 7.08
Pleural fluid glucose 38 mg/dL
Gram stain: gram-positive cocci in chains
WBC 28,000 /µL, neutrophil predominant

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given pleural fluid pH 7.08, glucose 38 mg/dL, and a positive Gram stain, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Continue antibiotics alone and repeat imaging in one week
Tube thoracostomy (chest drain) for the complicated parapneumonic effusion/empyema, plus continued antibiotics
Therapeutic thoracentesis once and then observe
Start systemic corticosteroids
stages.explanation: A complicated parapneumonic effusion or empyema—indicated by pH <7.20, glucose <60 mg/dL, positive Gram stain/culture, or frank pus—requires drainage with a chest tube in addition to antibiotics; antibiotics alone will not resolve infected, loculated pleural fluid. Continuing antibiotics alone (A) risks progression to a trapped lung. A single therapeutic thoracentesis (C) is insufficient for a loculated, infected collection that will reaccumulate. Corticosteroids (D) have no established role. Loculated collections may also need intrapleural fibrinolytics/DNase or surgical decortication if drainage is inadequate.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Despite chest tube drainage, repeat imaging shows persistent loculated fluid and ongoing fever. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Remove the chest tube and switch antibiotics only
Instill intrapleural tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) and DNase, or consult surgery for VATS decortication
Begin broad-spectrum antifungal therapy
Repeat diagnostic thoracentesis from a new site
stages.explanation: When a chest tube fails to adequately drain a loculated empyema, intrapleural fibrinolytic therapy with tPA plus DNase can break down septations and improve drainage; if that fails, surgical intervention (VATS or decortication) is indicated. Removing the tube and only changing antibiotics (A) abandons source control. Empiric antifungals (C) are unwarranted—the Gram stain showed gram-positive cocci (likely Streptococcus). A new diagnostic tap (D) repeats data already obtained and does not address the failure of drainage. Source control of the infected pleural space is essential.

cases.diagnosis: Complicated parapneumonic effusion (empyema)
pearls: Light's criteria classify an effusion as exudative if pleural/serum protein >0.5, pleural/serum LDH >0.6, or pleural LDH >2/3 serum ULN.
Complicated parapneumonic effusion/empyema (pH <7.20, glucose <60, positive Gram stain, or pus) requires drainage plus antibiotics.
Bedside ultrasound detects loculations and guides safe thoracentesis better than plain films.
Failed chest-tube drainage of loculated empyema warrants intrapleural tPA/DNase or surgical decortication.

cases.title: Months of Cough, Night Sweats, and Weight Loss
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent cough for three months with night sweats and unintentional weight loss.
cases.history: A 44-year-old man presents to clinic with a productive cough that has persisted for about three months, recently producing blood-streaked sputum. He reports drenching night sweats, low-grade fevers, fatigue, and an unintentional 7 kg weight loss over the same period. He emigrated from a country with high tuberculosis prevalence two years ago and recalls a household contact with chronic cough. He has no significant PMH and takes no medications. He has no known drug allergies. He works in food service and lives in a shared apartment. He drinks alcohol occasionally and does not smoke or use injection drugs. He has never been tested for HIV. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for anorexia and malaise; he denies chest pain, orthopnea, or leg swelling. He has not traveled recently but is concerned because a coworker noticed his weight loss.
cases.exam: Thin, chronically ill-appearing man in no acute distress. Lung exam reveals occasional crackles over the right upper lung field with otherwise normal breath sounds; no wheezes. No clubbing or cyanosis. Lymph node exam reveals a small, mobile, nontender right cervical node. Heart sounds regular without murmur. Abdomen soft, no hepatosplenomegaly. No peripheral edema. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable, including a normal oropharynx and no oral thrush.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the clinical picture and epidemiology, what is the single most important immediate infection-control action when this patient is identified in clinic?
options: Place him in contact precautions with gown and gloves
Place him in an airborne infection isolation (negative-pressure) room with respiratory precautions
No precautions are needed until cultures return
Place him in droplet precautions with a surgical mask only
stages.explanation: Suspected pulmonary tuberculosis requires immediate airborne precautions—an airborne infection isolation (negative-pressure) room, with staff wearing fit-tested N95 respirators—because M. tuberculosis spreads via airborne droplet nuclei. Contact precautions (A) address organisms spread by direct/indirect contact, not airborne pathogens. Waiting for cultures (C) is dangerous, as cultures take weeks and the patient is potentially highly infectious now. Droplet precautions with a surgical mask (D) are insufficient for TB; airborne transmission requires a respirator and negative-pressure room. Early isolation protects other patients and staff.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic workup for suspected active pulmonary tuberculosis?
options: Tuberculin skin test alone
Chest radiograph plus three sputum specimens for acid-fast bacilli smear, culture, and nucleic acid amplification testing
CT chest and bronchoscopy as the first step
Interferon-gamma release assay alone
stages.explanation: Active pulmonary TB is evaluated with a chest radiograph and collection of respiratory specimens (typically three sputum samples) for AFB smear microscopy, mycobacterial culture (the gold standard), and a rapid nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT), which can also detect rifampin resistance. A tuberculin skin test (A) or IGRA (D) detects infection/immune response but cannot distinguish latent from active disease and can be negative in active TB. CT and bronchoscopy (C) are reserved for cases where sputum is nondiagnostic—not the first step. Sputum sampling provides diagnosis and informs isolation duration.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Chest X-ray: right upper lobe cavitary infiltrate with fibronodular changes
Sputum AFB smear: positive (3+)
Nucleic acid amplification test: positive for M. tuberculosis complex; no rifampin resistance detected
HIV antibody/antigen: pending
Hepatic panel: AST 22, ALT 26, normal bilirubin
Visual acuity and color vision: normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: AFB smear and NAAT confirm drug-susceptible pulmonary TB. What is the recommended initial (intensive-phase) treatment regimen?
options: Isoniazid monotherapy for 9 months
Rifampin plus isoniazid for 4 months
Isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol (RIPE) for 2 months, then isoniazid and rifampin to complete therapy
Pyrazinamide and ethambutol for 6 months
stages.explanation: Standard treatment for drug-susceptible active TB is the 2-month intensive phase with four drugs—isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol (RIPE)—followed by a continuation phase of isoniazid and rifampin for 4 more months (total 6 months). Isoniazid monotherapy (A) is used for latent TB, not active disease, and monotherapy for active TB breeds resistance. Two-drug regimens (B, D) are inadequate as initial therapy for active TB and risk resistance. Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) is added to prevent isoniazid-induced neuropathy, and TB is a reportable disease requiring public-health notification and directly observed therapy.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: Which medication-specific toxicity should be specifically monitored, and how?
options: Ethambutol—baseline and periodic visual acuity and color vision testing
Pyrazinamide—routine audiometry
Rifampin—monthly thyroid function tests
Isoniazid—serial renal ultrasounds
stages.explanation: Ethambutol can cause optic neuritis with decreased visual acuity and red-green color discrimination; baseline and periodic visual acuity/color vision testing are recommended, and patients should report visual changes. Pyrazinamide causes hyperuricemia and hepatotoxicity, not the auditory toxicity implied by audiometry (B). Rifampin causes hepatotoxicity, orange body fluids, and drug interactions—not thyroid dysfunction requiring monthly TFTs (C). Isoniazid causes hepatotoxicity and peripheral neuropathy (prevented with pyridoxine), not renal pathology monitored by ultrasound (D). All patients on RIPE also need baseline and symptom-driven liver function monitoring.

cases.diagnosis: Reactivation pulmonary tuberculosis
pearls: Suspected pulmonary TB requires immediate airborne isolation (negative-pressure room, N95 respirators).
Diagnose active TB with chest X-ray plus three sputum specimens for AFB smear, culture, and NAAT; skin test/IGRA cannot distinguish active from latent disease.
Treat drug-susceptible TB with 2 months of RIPE, then 4 months of isoniazid and rifampin; add pyridoxine to prevent INH neuropathy.
Ethambutol causes optic neuritis—monitor visual acuity and color vision; all RIPE patients need liver function monitoring.

cases.title: Worsening Breathlessness and Dry Cough in a Young Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of progressively worsening shortness of breath and a dry cough.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man is admitted with three to four weeks of progressive exertional dyspnea and a nonproductive cough, now breathless climbing a single flight of stairs. He has had low-grade fevers, fatigue, and a 5 kg weight loss. Over the past few months he developed white plaques in his mouth and recurrent skin infections. He has no formal PMH and takes no medications. He has no known drug allergies. He reports having sex with men and inconsistent condom use, and has never been tested for HIV. He does not smoke; he drinks socially and denies injection drug use. He works as a graphic designer. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for oral discomfort, malaise, and dyspnea that has accelerated over the last week; he denies chest pain, hemoptysis, leg swelling, or sick contacts. He delayed care hoping symptoms would improve on their own.
cases.exam: Thin, ill-appearing man, tachypneic with mild accessory muscle use. Oropharynx shows white plaques on the tongue and buccal mucosa that scrape off, consistent with thrush. Lung auscultation is notably clear or with only fine bibasilar crackles despite significant hypoxemia. No wheezing. Heart tachycardic, regular, no murmur. No lymphadenopathy appreciated. Abdomen soft, no hepatosplenomegaly. No focal neurologic deficits. Pulse oximetry drops to 80% with ambulation in the hallway. Skin shows a few healing furuncles.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given oral thrush, weight loss, and subacute hypoxemic dyspnea with a relatively clear chest, what underlying condition and pulmonary infection are most likely?
options: Bacterial pneumonia in an immunocompetent host
Undiagnosed HIV/AIDS with Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia
Asthma exacerbation
Pulmonary embolism
stages.explanation: The combination of oral candidiasis, weight loss, high-risk sexual history, and subacute progressive hypoxemic dyspnea with a paucity of chest findings strongly suggests advanced, undiagnosed HIV with Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP), an AIDS-defining opportunistic infection occurring when CD4 falls below 200. Bacterial pneumonia (A) usually presents more acutely with focal consolidation and productive cough. Asthma (C) causes wheezing and obstruction, not hypoxemic interstitial disease with thrush. PE (D) can cause hypoxemia but does not explain thrush, weight loss, or the subacute febrile course. The exertional desaturation is a hallmark of PJP.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of tests best supports the suspected diagnosis at this stage?
options: HIV antigen/antibody test, CD4 count, chest imaging, and serum LDH
Sputum culture and procalcitonin only
D-dimer and CT pulmonary angiogram only
Echocardiogram and BNP
stages.explanation: Confirming the underlying immunosuppression and the infection is key: an HIV antigen/antibody test plus CD4 count establishes AIDS-level immunosuppression, while chest imaging (often diffuse bilateral interstitial infiltrates) and an elevated serum LDH support PJP; definitive diagnosis uses induced sputum or bronchoalveolar lavage with special stains/PCR for Pneumocystis. Sputum culture and procalcitonin (B) target typical bacterial pneumonia. D-dimer/CTPA (C) evaluate PE, which does not fit. Echocardiogram and BNP (D) assess cardiac dyspnea, not this infectious/immunologic picture. LDH is sensitive but nonspecific and helps gauge severity.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
HIV antigen/antibody: positive; confirmatory testing positive
CD4 count: 78 cells/µL
Serum LDH: 540 U/L (elevated)
Chest X-ray: diffuse bilateral interstitial (ground-glass) infiltrates
ABG on room air: PaO2 58 mmHg, A-a gradient widened
Induced sputum: Pneumocystis jirovecii identified on direct fluorescent antibody stain
Beta-D-glucan: markedly elevated

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His PaO2 is 58 mmHg on room air. What is the most appropriate treatment for his Pneumocystis pneumonia?
options: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole alone
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole plus adjunctive corticosteroids
Atovaquone monotherapy
Azithromycin plus ceftriaxone
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for PJP is trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX). Adjunctive corticosteroids are indicated when hypoxemia is significant—PaO2 <70 mmHg on room air or A-a gradient ≥35 mmHg—because steroids reduce inflammation-mediated respiratory deterioration and mortality. His PaO2 of 58 meets this threshold, so steroids must be added. TMP-SMX alone (A) is insufficient given his hypoxemia. Atovaquone (C) is reserved for mild-to-moderate disease or sulfa intolerance, not severe hypoxemic PJP. Azithromycin plus ceftriaxone (D) treats community-acquired bacterial pneumonia, not Pneumocystis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After starting PJP treatment, when should antiretroviral therapy (ART) and Pneumocystis prophylaxis be addressed?
options: Never start ART; only treat the acute infection
Start ART typically within about 2 weeks of starting OI treatment, and continue secondary PJP prophylaxis until immune reconstitution
Delay ART for at least 6 months after the infection resolves
Start ART immediately on the same day as TMP-SMX in all cases regardless of risk
stages.explanation: For most opportunistic infections including PJP, ART is generally initiated early—typically within about 2 weeks of starting OI treatment—to improve outcomes, while monitoring for immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS). Secondary prophylaxis (e.g., TMP-SMX) continues until the CD4 count rises above 200 for at least 3 months on ART. Never starting ART (A) abandons the only durable treatment for the underlying HIV. Delaying ART 6 months (C) prolongs profound immunosuppression. Same-day ART regardless of context (D) overstates the rule; timing is individualized and certain OIs (e.g., TB meningitis) warrant a more cautious approach.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On day 7 of TMP-SMX his creatinine rises modestly, potassium climbs to 5.4 mEq/L, and a maculopapular rash appears. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Immediately stop TMP-SMX and switch to azithromycin
Add high-dose corticosteroids to suppress the reaction and continue TMP-SMX unchanged
Recognize these as expected TMP-SMX effects—monitor potassium and renal function and continue therapy unless the reaction becomes severe
Discontinue all antibiotics and observe off therapy
stages.explanation: TMP-SMX commonly causes a rise in creatinine (trimethoprim inhibits tubular creatinine secretion), hyperkalemia, and rash; for mild reactions the drug is continued with monitoring, as it remains the most effective PJP therapy. Switching to azithromycin (A) is wrong—azithromycin does not treat PJP. Adding steroids to suppress a mild rash (B) is not standard and steroids here are dosed for hypoxemia, not allergy suppression. Stopping all antibiotics (D) abandons treatment of a life-threatening infection. Severe reactions (Stevens-Johnson, severe cytopenias, renal failure) would warrant switching to an alternative such as primaquine-clindamycin or IV pentamidine.

cases.diagnosis: HIV/AIDS with Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia
pearls: PJP presents with subacute progressive dyspnea, dry cough, exertional desaturation, and a chest that sounds clearer than the hypoxemia suggests.
Oral thrush, weight loss, and CD4 <200 point to advanced undiagnosed HIV; check HIV serology, CD4, LDH, and imaging.
Treat PJP with TMP-SMX; add corticosteroids when PaO2 <70 mmHg or A-a gradient ≥35 mmHg.
Start ART within ~2 weeks of OI therapy and continue PJP prophylaxis until CD4 >200 for ≥3 months.

cases.title: Fever and Fatigue in a Man Who Injects Drugs
cases.chiefComplaint: One week of fevers, chills, and fatigue.
cases.history: A 42-year-old man is admitted with a week of intermittent fevers, chills, drenching sweats, and progressive fatigue. He reports active intravenous heroin use, often sharing equipment, and has noticed tender red spots on his fingers. He denies cough, dysuria, or abdominal pain but reports decreased appetite. PMH includes hepatitis C and prior skin abscesses. He takes no regular medications. He has no known drug allergies. He is unhoused and has not seen a physician in years. He smokes cigarettes and drinks alcohol heavily. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for myalgias, mild dyspnea on exertion, and a new sense of his heart 'pounding'; he denies focal weakness, vision changes, or recent dental work. He reports the fevers are escalating despite over-the-counter medications, prompting him to seek care.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, diaphoretic man. Cardiac auscultation reveals a new high-pitched holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration. Multiple needle track marks on both arms. Examination of the hands shows tender violaceous nodules on the finger pads (Osler nodes) and a few nontender erythematous macules on the palms (Janeway lesions). Fundoscopy shows a retinal hemorrhage with a pale center (Roth spot). Lungs have scattered crackles. No splenomegaly is appreciated. No focal neurologic deficits. Mild splinter hemorrhages under the nails.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the single most important initial diagnostic step before starting antibiotics in this patient with suspected endocarditis?
options: Obtain a single blood culture and start antibiotics immediately
Obtain at least three sets of blood cultures from separate sites before antibiotics
Order a transthoracic echocardiogram and defer cultures
Start empiric vancomycin without any cultures
stages.explanation: At least three sets of blood cultures drawn from separate venipuncture sites over time should be obtained before antibiotics, because identifying the organism (a major Duke criterion) guides targeted, often weeks-long therapy and detects continuous bacteremia. A single culture (A) is inadequate and risks missing the diagnosis or contaminants. Echocardiography (C) is essential but should not delay obtaining cultures, and cultures must precede antibiotics whenever the patient is stable. Starting antibiotics without cultures (D) can render cultures negative and obscure the causative organism. In a critically unstable septic patient, cultures are still drawn rapidly before the first dose.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Applying the modified Duke criteria, which of the following is considered a MAJOR criterion?
options: Fever ≥38°C
Echocardiographic evidence of a vegetation
Injection drug use (predisposing risk factor)
Vascular phenomena such as Janeway lesions
stages.explanation: Major Duke criteria are (1) typical positive blood cultures for endocarditis-causing organisms (or persistently positive cultures) and (2) evidence of endocardial involvement, such as a vegetation, abscess, or new valvular regurgitation on echocardiography. An echocardiographic vegetation is therefore a major criterion. Fever ≥38°C (A), a predisposing condition like IV drug use (C), and vascular phenomena such as Janeway lesions (D) are all MINOR criteria. Diagnosis requires 2 major, 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor criteria. Recognizing which findings are major versus minor is high-yield.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.5 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL
ESR 78 mm/hr, CRP elevated
Blood cultures (3 sets): all growing methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA)
Transthoracic echocardiogram: 1.2 cm mobile vegetation on the tricuspid valve with moderate tricuspid regurgitation
Chest X-ray: multiple peripheral nodular opacities, some cavitating (septic emboli)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Blood cultures grow MRSA on a tricuspid valve vegetation. What is the most appropriate antibiotic therapy?
options: Oral cephalexin for 2 weeks
Oral linezolid for 10 days
Intravenous vancomycin (or daptomycin), typically for about 6 weeks
Intravenous ceftriaxone for 4 weeks
stages.explanation: MRSA endocarditis requires prolonged intravenous bactericidal therapy—vancomycin or daptomycin—typically for about 6 weeks for native-valve disease. Oral cephalexin (A) and oral linezolid (B) are inappropriate for endocarditis, which requires sustained bactericidal IV levels; beta-lactams like cephalexin/ceftriaxone also do not reliably cover MRSA. IV ceftriaxone (D) treats methicillin-susceptible streptococcal endocarditis but not MRSA. Duration and route are critical to sterilize the vegetation. The chest findings represent septic pulmonary emboli, a classic complication of right-sided (tricuspid) endocarditis in people who inject drugs.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On day 4 of appropriate therapy he develops acute left arm weakness and aphasia. What complication has most likely occurred?
options: Systemic (cerebral) embolization from the vegetation
Medication side effect from vancomycin
Alcohol withdrawal seizure
Hypoglycemia
stages.explanation: New focal neurologic deficits in endocarditis most likely reflect embolization of vegetation fragments to the cerebral circulation, causing an embolic stroke; this is a feared complication and may prompt evaluation for mycotic aneurysm and reconsideration of surgical timing. Vancomycin toxicity (B) causes nephrotoxicity or infusion reactions, not acute focal deficits. Alcohol withdrawal seizures (C) are generalized, not focal, and would cause postictal rather than persistent focal deficits with aphasia. Hypoglycemia (D) can mimic stroke but is readily excluded by a glucose check and does not fit the embolic context. Urgent neuroimaging is warranted.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Despite appropriate antibiotics, he has persistent bacteremia, the vegetation enlarges to 1.8 cm, and he develops worsening heart failure from severe tricuspid regurgitation. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue antibiotics indefinitely and avoid surgery because of his injection drug use
Refer for surgical (cardiac) evaluation for valve repair/replacement given heart failure, persistent infection, and an enlarging large vegetation
Switch to oral antibiotics and arrange outpatient follow-up
Add a second antibiotic and continue medical therapy alone with no surgical consult
stages.explanation: Surgery is indicated in endocarditis for heart failure from valve dysfunction, uncontrolled or persistent infection despite appropriate antibiotics, large (>1 cm) or enlarging vegetations with recurrent emboli, and certain organisms/abscesses—this patient meets several criteria and warrants surgical evaluation. Withholding surgery solely because of injection drug use (A) is not justified; the decision is based on clinical indications, ideally with addiction support. De-escalating to oral antibiotics and outpatient care (C) is unsafe with uncontrolled infection and heart failure. Simply adding a drug and continuing medical therapy alone (D) ignores established surgical indications and risks death from progressive valve failure.

cases.diagnosis: Infective endocarditis (tricuspid valve, MRSA) in a person who injects drugs
pearls: Draw at least three sets of blood cultures from separate sites before antibiotics in suspected endocarditis.
Duke major criteria: typical/persistent positive blood cultures and echocardiographic endocardial involvement (vegetation, abscess, new regurgitation).
Right-sided (tricuspid) endocarditis in injection drug users classically causes septic pulmonary emboli.
MRSA native-valve endocarditis needs IV vancomycin or daptomycin for ~6 weeks; watch for embolic stroke and other embolic phenomena.

cases.title: Acute Confusion After Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: New, fluctuating confusion and agitation two days after hip surgery.
cases.history: An 84-year-old woman is on the orthopedic ward two days after open reduction and internal fixation of a hip fracture. Nursing reports she has become acutely confused and agitated, pulling at her IV lines, with periods of drowsiness alternating with restlessness, worse in the evenings. She was fully oriented before surgery. PMH includes hypertension, mild baseline cognitive impairment, and osteoarthritis. Medications since admission include oxycodone for pain, diphenhydramine for sleep, and a recently inserted urinary catheter. She has no known drug allergies. She lives alone and was independent in daily activities before the fall. She drinks a glass of wine nightly and does not smoke. Family history is noncontributory. Collateral from her daughter confirms she had no prior episodes like this and was conversational and oriented at baseline. She has eaten and drunk little since the operation.
cases.exam: Elderly woman who is inattentive, unable to follow a simple counting task, and disoriented to time and place; her attention waxes and wanes during the interview. She is intermittently drowsy. No focal motor weakness, facial asymmetry, or aphasia. Pupils equal and reactive. Mild suprapubic fullness is present. Surgical hip wound is clean and dry. Lungs clear; heart regular. Mucous membranes are dry. No nuchal rigidity. No asterixis or tremor noted at this time.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely explanation for her acute change in mental status?
options: Progression of underlying dementia
Delirium
Acute ischemic stroke
New-onset major depressive disorder
stages.explanation: Acute onset, a fluctuating course, inattention, and altered level of consciousness in a hospitalized elderly post-operative patient are the defining features of delirium. Dementia (A) develops insidiously over months to years and does not cause an acute fluctuating attentional deficit, though it is a risk factor. Acute stroke (C) typically produces focal deficits, which she lacks. Depression (D) does not cause acute inattention and a fluctuating level of consciousness over hours. Delirium is a clinical diagnosis (the CAM screen requires acute onset/fluctuation and inattention, plus disorganized thinking or altered consciousness) and signals an underlying precipitant.

stages.label: EVALUATION
stages.question: Which approach best identifies the reversible triggers of her delirium?
options: Immediately start an antipsychotic and reassess in the morning
Systematically search for precipitants—medications, infection, urinary retention, metabolic abnormalities, hypoxia, pain, and constipation
Order an MRI brain as the first and only step
Reassure the family it will resolve on its own and take no action
stages.explanation: Delirium management begins with finding and treating precipitants. A systematic review should target deliriogenic medications (opioids, anticholinergics like diphenhydramine, benzodiazepines), infection (including UTI), urinary retention, electrolyte and metabolic derangements, hypoxia, uncontrolled pain, dehydration, and constipation. Starting an antipsychotic first (A) treats symptoms without addressing the cause and carries risks. MRI alone (C) is low yield without focal findings and ignores common reversible causes. Doing nothing (D) is unsafe—delirium increases morbidity and mortality. In this patient, opioids, diphenhydramine, a catheter with retention, and poor intake are likely contributors.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Basic metabolic panel: Na 134 mEq/L, K 4.0, BUN 32 mg/dL, Cr 1.1 mg/dL (signs of dehydration)
CBC: WBC 8.1 ×10³/µL (normal)
Urinalysis: no nitrites, no leukocyte esterase, few epithelial cells
Bladder scan: 600 mL retained urine
Finger-stick glucose: 104 mg/dL
Medication review: scheduled diphenhydramine and oxycodone; anticholinergic burden high

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the workup, what is the most appropriate FIRST-LINE management of her delirium?
options: Scheduled haloperidol around the clock
Physical restraints to prevent line removal
A benzodiazepine to calm her at night
Non-pharmacologic measures plus correcting precipitants: relieve urinary retention, stop diphenhydramine, minimize opioids, rehydrate, reorient, and mobilize
stages.explanation: First-line delirium management is non-pharmacologic and cause-directed: relieve the urinary retention (catheterize/treat), discontinue the anticholinergic diphenhydramine, minimize opioids with multimodal analgesia, rehydrate, and apply reorientation, sleep hygiene, sensory aids (glasses/hearing aids), early mobilization, and family presence. Scheduled haloperidol (A) is not first-line and is reserved for severe agitation posing safety risks. Physical restraints (B) worsen agitation and injury risk and should be avoided. Benzodiazepines (C) typically worsen delirium (except in alcohol/benzodiazepine withdrawal). Addressing the modifiable triggers is the cornerstone of care.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which intervention is most effective at PREVENTING delirium in hospitalized older adults?
options: Prophylactic antipsychotics for all elderly admissions
Routine use of nightly sedative-hypnotics
Multicomponent non-pharmacologic protocols (reorientation, sleep, mobility, hydration, sensory aids, avoiding deliriogenic drugs)
Strict bed rest to prevent falls
stages.explanation: Multicomponent non-pharmacologic programs (such as the Hospital Elder Life Program) reduce delirium incidence by promoting reorientation, normal sleep-wake cycles, early mobilization, adequate hydration and nutrition, provision of glasses/hearing aids, and avoidance of deliriogenic medications. Prophylactic antipsychotics (A) are not recommended for prevention and carry harms. Routine sedative-hypnotics (B) increase delirium risk. Strict bed rest (D) promotes deconditioning and actually raises delirium and complication risk; early mobilization is protective. Prevention is far more effective than treatment for this high-morbidity condition.

cases.diagnosis: Delirium (hyperactive/mixed, multifactorial post-operative)
pearls: Delirium is defined by acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention, and altered level of consciousness—distinct from dementia's insidious decline.
Always hunt for precipitants: medications (opioids, anticholinergics, benzodiazepines), infection, urinary retention, metabolic derangements, hypoxia, pain, constipation.
First-line treatment is non-pharmacologic and cause-directed; reserve low-dose antipsychotics for dangerous agitation and avoid benzodiazepines except in withdrawal.
Multicomponent prevention protocols and early mobilization reduce delirium incidence in hospitalized elders.

cases.title: Tremor and Agitation Two Days Into Admission
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening tremor, sweating, and seeing things that aren't there.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man admitted two days ago for cellulitis now has worsening tremor, profuse sweating, anxiety, and reports seeing bugs crawling on the walls. Nursing notes increasing restlessness and a rising heart rate. On admission he disclosed drinking about a pint of vodka daily for years, with his last drink the morning of admission. PMH includes hypertension and a prior 'seizure' during a previous hospitalization. Medications: as-needed acetaminophen and the IV antibiotics for cellulitis; he was not started on a withdrawal protocol. He has no known drug allergies. He is unemployed and lives alone; he smokes one pack daily. Family history includes a father with alcohol use disorder. Review of systems is positive for nausea, insomnia, and headache; he denies chest pain or focal weakness. He is oriented but visibly anxious and tremulous, repeatedly asking to leave the hospital.
cases.exam: Diaphoretic, anxious man with a coarse resting and intention tremor of the outstretched hands. He is oriented to person, place, and time but distractible, with visual hallucinations (perceiving insects on the wall). Pupils mildly dilated. No nuchal rigidity. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Reflexes brisk and symmetric. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia with a regular rhythm and no murmur. The cellulitic lower leg is improving. No asterixis. No jaundice or ascites noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given his timeline and presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Sepsis from worsening cellulitis
Alcohol withdrawal
Thyroid storm
Acute psychosis unrelated to substances
stages.explanation: Tremor, diaphoresis, tachycardia, hypertension, anxiety, insomnia, and perceptual disturbances (alcoholic hallucinosis) beginning 24-72 hours after the last drink in a heavy chronic drinker are classic for alcohol withdrawal. His prior withdrawal seizure heightens risk for severe withdrawal. Worsening sepsis (A) is unlikely given the improving cellulitis and absence of a clear new source, though it can coexist and must be excluded. Thyroid storm (C) can mimic adrenergic withdrawal but lacks the clear temporal link to alcohol cessation. Primary psychosis (D) does not explain the autonomic hyperactivity or the precise timing after his last drink.

stages.label: ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Which validated tool should guide the intensity and frequency of his treatment?
options: Glasgow Coma Scale
CIWA-Ar (Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol) scoring
CHA2DS2-VASc score
Wells score
stages.explanation: The CIWA-Ar score quantifies withdrawal severity across symptoms such as tremor, sweating, anxiety, agitation, nausea, and hallucinations, allowing symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing that reduces total medication exposure and improves outcomes. The Glasgow Coma Scale (A) measures consciousness in trauma/altered patients, not withdrawal severity. CHA2DS2-VASc (C) estimates stroke risk in atrial fibrillation. Wells score (D) estimates pretest probability of venous thromboembolism. CIWA-driven, symptom-triggered therapy is the standard for guiding benzodiazepine treatment in alcohol withdrawal.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Basic metabolic panel: Na 138, K 3.3 mEq/L, Mg 1.4 mg/dL (low), glucose 96 mg/dL
Liver enzymes: AST 110 U/L, ALT 48 U/L (AST:ALT >2)
CBC: mild macrocytosis (MCV 102 fL)
Thyroid function: TSH normal
CIWA-Ar score: 22 (severe)
Ethanol level: undetectable

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His CIWA-Ar is 22. What is the most appropriate pharmacologic management?
options: Symptom-triggered benzodiazepines (e.g., lorazepam or diazepam) titrated to CIWA, plus thiamine and electrolyte repletion
Haloperidol as the primary agent to control symptoms
Beta-blocker monotherapy to control vital signs
Intravenous ethanol infusion
stages.explanation: Benzodiazepines are the cornerstone of alcohol-withdrawal treatment because they are cross-tolerant with alcohol at the GABA receptor and prevent progression to seizures and delirium tremens; symptom-triggered dosing guided by CIWA is preferred. Thiamine (given before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy) and repletion of magnesium and potassium are essential adjuncts. Haloperidol (B) lowers the seizure threshold and does not treat the underlying GABA deficit. Beta-blockers (C) mask autonomic signs without preventing seizures or DTs and can be dangerous as monotherapy. IV ethanol (D) is not standard and is unsafe. In lorazepam-preferred situations (significant liver disease), lorazepam avoids accumulation.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If undertreated, which life-threatening complication is he at greatest risk of developing, and when?
options: Wernicke encephalopathy within minutes of admission
Delirium tremens, typically 48-96 hours after the last drink
Hepatic encephalopathy within the first 6 hours
Serotonin syndrome from benzodiazepines
stages.explanation: Delirium tremens (DTs)—characterized by severe autonomic instability, profound confusion, agitation, and hallucinations—typically emerges 48-96 hours after the last drink and carries significant mortality if untreated; risk factors include prior withdrawal seizures/DTs and a high CIWA, both present here. Wernicke encephalopathy (A) results from thiamine deficiency and presents with confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia, but not 'within minutes.' Hepatic encephalopathy (C) relates to liver failure and is not the immediate withdrawal complication being prevented. Serotonin syndrome (D) is not caused by benzodiazepines. Adequate benzodiazepine therapy prevents progression to DTs.

cases.diagnosis: Alcohol withdrawal (severe, with high risk for delirium tremens)
pearls: Alcohol withdrawal escalates 24-72 hours after the last drink; tremor, autonomic hyperactivity, and hallucinosis are hallmarks.
Use CIWA-Ar to guide symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing—the cornerstone of treatment.
Give thiamine before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy, and replete magnesium and potassium.
Delirium tremens peaks 48-96 hours after the last drink and is potentially fatal; prior withdrawal seizures and high CIWA raise the risk.

cases.title: Unresponsive With Slow, Shallow Breathing
cases.chiefComplaint: Found unresponsive with slow breathing by a friend.
cases.history: A 29-year-old man is brought to the ED by EMS after a friend found him unresponsive on the couch at a party. The friend reports he had been using 'something' recreationally and may have taken pills; an empty bottle of oxycodone was nearby. EMS noted slow, shallow respirations and pinpoint pupils. PMH is notable for chronic low back pain for which he has been prescribed opioids, and a prior overdose. He takes oxycodone and occasionally uses heroin per his friend. He has no known drug allergies. He smokes and drinks alcohol; the friend is unsure whether alcohol was also involved tonight. He is unemployed and lives with roommates. Family history is noncontributory. No reliable review of systems is obtainable given his obtundation; there is no report of trauma, seizure activity, or known diabetes. The friend states the patient was 'totally normal' a few hours earlier.
cases.exam: Somnolent, minimally responsive man who does not follow commands and responds only to vigorous stimulation. Pupils are pinpoint (miotic) and minimally reactive bilaterally. Respirations are slow and shallow with intermittent apnea. No focal neurologic deficits elicited; he withdraws to pain in all extremities. No signs of head trauma. Lungs with shallow air entry but no focal crackles. Heart rate slow and regular. No track marks examined yet but skin is intact over visible areas. Gag reflex sluggish.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The triad of depressed consciousness, pinpoint pupils, and respiratory depression points to what diagnosis, and what is the immediate priority?
options: Opioid overdose; immediate priority is airway/breathing support and oxygenation
Benzodiazepine overdose; immediate priority is flumazenil
Ischemic stroke; immediate priority is thrombolysis
Hypoglycemia; immediate priority is insulin
stages.explanation: The classic opioid toxidrome is CNS depression, miosis (pinpoint pupils), and respiratory depression—the most life-threatening feature. The immediate priority is supporting airway and breathing (bag-valve-mask ventilation, oxygen) because death from opioid overdose is due to hypoventilation and hypoxia. Benzodiazepine overdose (B) causes sedation but typically preserves respiratory drive more and lacks miosis; flumazenil can precipitate seizures and is generally avoided. Stroke (C) causes focal deficits, not this toxidrome, and thrombolysis is irrelevant here. Hypoglycemia (D) can cause coma but is treated with dextrose, not insulin, and does not produce pinpoint pupils.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After beginning ventilatory support, what is the most appropriate pharmacologic intervention?
options: Administer a large single IV bolus of naloxone to ensure full reversal
Administer naloxone, titrated to restore adequate respirations rather than full alertness
Administer activated charcoal immediately
Administer IV flumazenil
stages.explanation: Naloxone, an opioid antagonist, reverses respiratory depression and should be titrated to restore adequate ventilation rather than full consciousness, minimizing the risk of precipitating acute withdrawal (agitation, vomiting, catecholamine surge) in an opioid-dependent patient. A large single bolus (A) risks abrupt, severe withdrawal and aspiration. Activated charcoal (C) is generally avoided in patients with depressed consciousness and an unprotected airway because of aspiration risk. Flumazenil (D) targets benzodiazepines and can trigger seizures; it is not indicated for opioid toxicity. Careful titration balances reversal of life-threatening hypoventilation against withdrawal.

stages.label: RESPONSE & LABS
stages.content:
After naloxone titration: RR increased to 16/min, SpO2 96% on supplemental O2, patient more arousable, pupils slightly larger
Finger-stick glucose: 92 mg/dL
Acetaminophen and salicylate levels: undetectable
Ethanol level: mildly elevated
ECG: sinus bradycardia, no QRS or QT prolongation
Venous blood gas: improving respiratory acidosis

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He improves after naloxone. Twenty minutes later he becomes drowsy again with falling respirations. What does this most likely indicate and require?
options: He is faking symptoms and can be discharged
Naloxone has a shorter duration of action than the ingested opioid; he needs repeat dosing or a naloxone infusion and continued monitoring
He has developed a new ischemic stroke requiring CT
The recurrence is due to the mildly elevated ethanol level alone
stages.explanation: Naloxone's duration of action (about 30-90 minutes) is often shorter than that of many opioids—especially long-acting agents—so re-sedation and recurrent respiratory depression are expected and require repeat naloxone doses or a continuous infusion, plus prolonged observation. Assuming he is malingering (A) is dangerous and ignores objective hypoventilation. Stroke (C) does not fit the toxidrome or the temporal pattern of naloxone wearing off. While ethanol (D) can contribute to sedation, the classic recurrence after initial reversal is due to naloxone wearing off before the opioid does. Observation duration should reflect the specific opioid ingested.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Before discharge, which intervention best reduces his risk of a future fatal overdose?
options: Advise abstinence and provide no further resources
Prescribe/dispense take-home naloxone and offer referral to medication for opioid use disorder (e.g., buprenorphine) and harm-reduction services
Recommend he simply increase his prescribed oxycodone dose
Discharge with a benzodiazepine to manage cravings
stages.explanation: Evidence-based overdose prevention includes dispensing take-home naloxone (and teaching the patient and contacts to use it), linking the patient to medication for opioid use disorder such as buprenorphine or methadone, and connecting them with harm-reduction services—interventions that substantially lower overdose mortality. Advising abstinence without resources (A) is ineffective. Increasing the opioid dose (C) raises overdose risk. Prescribing a benzodiazepine (D) is contraindicated because combined opioid-benzodiazepine use markedly increases respiratory depression and death. Discharge is an opportunity to initiate life-saving prevention.

cases.diagnosis: Opioid overdose
pearls: The opioid toxidrome is CNS depression, miosis (pinpoint pupils), and respiratory depression—support airway/breathing first.
Titrate naloxone to restore adequate respirations, not full alertness, to avoid precipitating severe withdrawal.
Naloxone often wears off before the opioid does—anticipate re-sedation and provide repeat dosing/infusion with prolonged monitoring.
Avoid flumazenil in mixed/unknown overdoses; at discharge, provide take-home naloxone and link to MOUD and harm-reduction services.

cases.title: Burning Chest Discomfort After Meals
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent burning behind the breastbone, worse after large meals and when lying down.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents to his primary care clinic with a 5-month history of burning retrosternal discomfort, typically 30-60 minutes after meals and worsened by lying flat at night. He occasionally tastes a sour, acidic regurgitation in the back of his throat and notes a mild morning cough. Symptoms are aggravated by coffee, spicy foods, and a large dinner, and partially relieved by over-the-counter antacids. He denies dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, melena, hematemesis, or early satiety. He has no chest pressure on exertion and no dyspnea. PMH includes obesity (BMI 32) and well-controlled hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily. He takes ibuprofen occasionally for knee pain. No prior endoscopy. Allergies: none. Social: drinks 3-4 beers most evenings, 10-pack-year smoking history (quit 2 years ago), works as an accountant, sedentary. Eats his largest meal late in the evening. Family history: father with peptic ulcer disease; no family history of esophageal or gastric cancer.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, obese man in no distress. Oropharynx without lesions or thrush. Neck supple without lymphadenopathy or thyromegaly. Lungs clear bilaterally. Heart regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs, no reproducible chest wall tenderness. Abdomen soft, mildly obese, non-tender, no epigastric mass, no hepatosplenomegaly; bowel sounds normal. No supraclavicular (Virchow) node. Stool guaiac negative on rectal exam. No peripheral edema. Findings are unremarkable aside from central obesity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the classic symptom pattern and absence of alarm features, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer immediately for upper endoscopy (EGD)
Make a clinical diagnosis of GERD and begin lifestyle modification with an empiric PPI trial
Order an esophageal pH study before any treatment
Obtain a barium swallow to confirm reflux
Check Helicobacter pylori stool antigen and treat if positive
stages.explanation: Classic GERD (heartburn, regurgitation, postprandial/recumbent worsening) in a patient without alarm features is diagnosed clinically; empiric lifestyle changes plus a PPI trial is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Immediate EGD (option B) is reserved for alarm features or treatment failure, not classic uncomplicated symptoms. pH study (option D) is for refractory or atypical cases or pre-surgical evaluation, not first line. Barium swallow (option E) is insensitive for reflux and mainly assesses structure/dysphagia. H. pylori testing (option F) is relevant for dyspepsia/PUD but is not the cornerstone of typical GERD management; eradication does not reliably improve reflux symptoms.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: Which lifestyle recommendation has the strongest evidence and best fits this patient's history?
options: Switch lisinopril to a calcium channel blocker
Weight loss, avoiding late/large meals, and elevating the head of the bed
Begin a strict low-residue (low-fiber) diet
Drink milk before bedtime to buffer acid
Take ibuprofen with each meal to reduce inflammation
stages.explanation: Weight loss, avoiding meals within 3 hours of lying down, smaller meals, and elevating the head of the bed are the lifestyle measures with the best supporting evidence and directly address his obesity and late large dinners. Changing the antihypertensive (option B) is unnecessary; lisinopril does not worsen reflux (calcium channel blockers can actually lower LES pressure). A low-residue diet (option D) treats other conditions, not GERD. Milk (option E) transiently buffers but stimulates rebound acid and adds calories. Ibuprofen (option F) is an NSAID that injures mucosa and would worsen symptoms and ulcer risk.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP VISIT (8 WEEKS)
stages.content:
Returns after 8 weeks of omeprazole 20 mg daily before breakfast plus lifestyle changes
Lost 4 kg; reduced alcohol; stopped late-night meals
Heartburn and regurgitation roughly 80% improved but still 1-2 episodes/week
New complaint today: progressive difficulty swallowing solids over the past 3 weeks
Unintentional 3 kg additional weight loss attributed to the swallowing difficulty
Reports food 'sticking' at the lower chest

stages.label: RED FLAGS
stages.question: Given the new dysphagia and weight loss, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure and continue PPI for another 8 weeks
Double the PPI to twice-daily dosing and reassess in 3 months
Refer for upper endoscopy (EGD)
Order an outpatient CT chest
Add an H2 blocker at bedtime and recheck in 6 weeks
stages.explanation: New dysphagia and unintentional weight loss are alarm features that mandate prompt EGD to exclude peptic stricture, esophageal adenocarcinoma, or other structural disease, and to assess for Barrett esophagus. Continued reassurance (option B) or escalating acid suppression (options C and 5) inappropriately delays diagnosis of a potentially serious lesion. CT chest (option E) does not evaluate the mucosa and would miss early mucosal disease; endoscopy with biopsy is the test of choice. Alarm features always override empiric management.

stages.label: LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT
stages.question: EGD shows a benign-appearing distal esophageal stricture with reflux esophagitis and no malignancy on biopsy. After endoscopic dilation, what is the best long-term acid-suppression strategy?
options: Stop the PPI now that the stricture is dilated
Continue a daily PPI long term to prevent recurrent stricture and heal esophagitis
Use antacids only as needed
Switch to as-needed H2 blocker monotherapy
Begin sucralfate as the sole agent
stages.explanation: A peptic stricture from reflux esophagitis indicates erosive disease requiring ongoing PPI therapy; continued daily PPI heals esophagitis and reduces stricture recurrence after dilation. Stopping the PPI (option B) or stepping down to as-needed antacids or H2 blockers (options D and 4) risks recurrent erosion and re-stenosis. Sucralfate alone (option F) provides mucosal coating but is far less effective than PPIs for healing erosive esophagitis. For erosive/complicated GERD, maintenance PPI at the lowest effective dose is standard.

cases.diagnosis: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) complicated by reflux esophagitis and a peptic esophageal stricture
pearls: Typical GERD without alarm features is a clinical diagnosis treated with lifestyle change plus an empiric PPI trial; no endoscopy needed up front.
Alarm features (dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, GI bleeding, anemia, vomiting, age >60 with new symptoms) mandate prompt EGD.
Weight loss, smaller and earlier meals, and head-of-bed elevation are the highest-yield lifestyle interventions.
Erosive esophagitis and peptic stricture require ongoing maintenance PPI therapy at the lowest effective dose.

cases.title: Months of Diarrhea, Cramping, and Weight Loss
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of non-bloody diarrhea, crampy right-sided abdominal pain, and a 7 kg weight loss.
cases.history: A 27-year-old woman is admitted from clinic with a 4-month history of 4-6 loose, non-bloody stools daily, crampy right lower quadrant abdominal pain, low-grade fevers, and fatigue. She has lost 7 kg unintentionally and reports decreased appetite. Over the past 2 weeks she developed tender red nodules on both shins and an aching, swelling sensation in her knees. She has occasional mouth ulcers. She denies recent travel, sick contacts, or antibiotic use. No prior abdominal surgery. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: only ibuprofen as needed (she has been taking it for joint aches). Allergies: none. Social: non-smoker—actually reports she recently started smoking socially; drinks rarely; works as a teacher; sexually active, monogamous. Family history: a maternal uncle with 'bowel disease' requiring surgery and an aunt with autoimmune thyroid disease. Review of systems notable for intermittent perianal soreness and one episode of perianal drainage she attributed to a hemorrhoid.
cases.exam: Thin, fatigued young woman. Conjunctivae mildly pale. Two shallow aphthous ulcers on buccal mucosa. No scleral icterus; eyes non-injected. Abdomen soft with focal tenderness and fullness in the right lower quadrant; no rebound or guarding; bowel sounds present. Perianal exam reveals a small skin tag and a healed-appearing fistulous opening with minimal drainage. Skin: several tender erythematous subcutaneous nodules on the anterior shins consistent with erythema nodosum. Both knees with small effusions, mildly warm, full range of motion. No spinal tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
WBC 13.5 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (MCV 78 fL)
Platelets 480 ×10³/µL
CRP 62 mg/L
ESR 48 mm/hr
Albumin 2.9 g/dL
Ferritin 14 ng/mL
Stool culture, ova & parasites: negative
Stool C. difficile toxin PCR: negative
Fecal calprotectin: 820 µg/g (markedly elevated)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which constellation of findings most strongly favors Crohn disease over ulcerative colitis?
options: Bloody diarrhea limited to the rectum
Continuous mucosal inflammation starting at the anus
Perianal fistula, mouth ulcers, and right lower quadrant involvement
Crypt abscesses on biopsy
P-ANCA positivity
stages.explanation: Perianal fistulizing disease, oral aphthous ulcers, and terminal-ileal/right-lower-quadrant involvement are hallmarks of Crohn disease, which is transmural and can affect any segment of the GI tract with skip lesions. Bloody diarrhea confined to the rectum and continuous inflammation from the anus upward (options B and 2) describe ulcerative colitis. Crypt abscesses (option E) occur in both and are not discriminating. P-ANCA (option F) is associated more with UC than Crohn. Non-caseating granulomas, transmural inflammation, fistulae, and skip lesions point to Crohn.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate diagnostic test to confirm the diagnosis and assess disease extent?
options: Ileocolonoscopy with biopsies
CT abdomen with oral contrast only
Empiric metronidazole without further testing
Capsule endoscopy as the initial study
Anti-tissue transglutaminase antibody testing
stages.explanation: Ileocolonoscopy with intubation of the terminal ileum and segmental biopsies is the gold standard to confirm Crohn disease, define extent, and obtain histology (skip lesions, granulomas). CT (option C) helps assess complications like abscess or stricture but is not the confirmatory diagnostic test. Empiric antibiotics (option D) treat neither the diagnosis nor luminal inflammation and would delay care. Capsule endoscopy (option E) is contraindicated before excluding stricture and is reserved for suspected small-bowel disease not seen on colonoscopy. Anti-tTG (option F) screens for celiac disease, not Crohn.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & IMAGING
stages.content:
Ileocolonoscopy: patchy deep ulcerations and cobblestoning in the terminal ileum and right colon, with normal intervening (skip) segments and a normal rectum
Biopsy: transmural-pattern chronic active inflammation with a non-caseating granuloma
MR enterography: terminal ileal wall thickening with mural enhancement, no abscess, no high-grade stricture
Vitamin B12: low-normal; iron studies confirm iron deficiency
QuantiFERON-TB and hepatitis B serologies: negative (obtained for biologic planning)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has a moderate-to-severe flare. What is the most appropriate therapy to induce remission now?
options: Long-term high-dose NSAIDs for the joint pain
Systemic corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone) for induction, with a plan for steroid-sparing maintenance
Loperamide alone to control diarrhea
Oral mesalamine as definitive monotherapy
Antibiotics (ciprofloxacin/metronidazole) as sole induction therapy
stages.explanation: Systemic corticosteroids are standard induction therapy for a moderate-to-severe Crohn flare, rapidly reducing inflammation; because steroids are not for maintenance, you simultaneously plan a steroid-sparing agent (thiopurine or, increasingly, an anti-TNF/biologic). NSAIDs (option B) trigger and worsen IBD flares and must be stopped. Loperamide alone (option D) only masks symptoms and can precipitate toxic dilation in active colitis. Mesalamine (option E) has limited efficacy in Crohn, especially ileal/transmural disease. Antibiotics (option F) are adjuncts for perianal/penetrating disease or abscess, not primary induction for luminal inflammation.

stages.label: MAINTENANCE & MONITORING
stages.question: As steroids are tapered, which is the most appropriate maintenance and monitoring plan?
options: Continue prednisone indefinitely at a low dose
No maintenance therapy; treat only future flares with steroids
Start a steroid-sparing agent (e.g., anti-TNF or thiopurine), supplement iron/B12, encourage smoking cessation, and ensure age-appropriate health maintenance
Switch to chronic opioids for pain control
Begin a gluten-free diet as primary maintenance
stages.explanation: Crohn requires steroid-sparing maintenance—an anti-TNF biologic and/or thiopurine—because repeated steroid courses cause cumulative toxicity. Correcting iron and B12 deficiency, smoking cessation (smoking worsens Crohn and increases surgery risk), and vaccination/health maintenance are essential. Indefinite steroids (option B) cause osteoporosis, infection, and metabolic harm. No maintenance (option C) leads to relapse and complications. Chronic opioids (option E) worsen motility, mask disease, and risk dependence. A gluten-free diet (option F) treats celiac disease, not Crohn, and is not maintenance therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Crohn disease (terminal ileal and right colonic, with perianal fistula and extraintestinal manifestations) — moderate-to-severe flare
pearls: Crohn is transmural with skip lesions and can affect mouth to anus; perianal fistulae, oral ulcers, and terminal-ileal disease are classic, and the rectum is often spared.
Smoking worsens Crohn (the opposite of ulcerative colitis); cessation reduces flares and surgery.
Steroids induce remission but are never maintenance—transition to steroid-sparing agents (thiopurines/biologics) and screen TB and hepatitis B before biologics.
Erythema nodosum and peripheral arthritis are extraintestinal signs that often track with intestinal disease activity.

cases.title: Bloody Diarrhea and Fever in a Young Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of worsening bloody diarrhea, now with high fever and abdominal distension.
cases.history: A 31-year-old man with a 3-year history of ulcerative colitis (left-sided, previously controlled on mesalamine) is admitted with 2 weeks of escalating bloody, mucoid diarrhea, now 12-15 stools per day with significant nocturnal stooling, crampy lower abdominal pain, and progressive fatigue. Over the last 48 hours he developed fevers to 39°C, abdominal distension, and decreased stool output despite continued urge. He stopped his mesalamine a month ago because he 'felt fine' and recently took loperamide and ibuprofen to control the diarrhea and cramps. He denies recent travel or antibiotics. PMH: ulcerative colitis. Medications: mesalamine (self-discontinued), recent OTC loperamide and ibuprofen. Allergies: none. Social: non-smoker, occasional alcohol, works in IT, lives with his partner. Family history: mother with autoimmune hypothyroidism. He appears acutely ill and reports lightheadedness on standing. No prior colectomy. Vaccinations up to date.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, pale, diaphoretic young man. Dry mucous membranes. Tachycardic, regular rhythm. Lungs clear. Abdomen distended and tympanitic with diffuse tenderness, decreased bowel sounds, and mild guarding without frank rebound at presentation. No palpable masses; no hepatosplenomegaly. Mild scleral injection (episcleritis). No focal neuro deficits. Rectal exam: gross blood and mucus, no palpable mass. Skin warm with delayed capillary refill. The combination of toxic appearance, fever, tachycardia, hypotension, and a distended tympanitic abdomen is concerning.

stages.label: ADMISSION LABS
stages.content:
WBC 19.8 ×10³/µL (left shift)
Hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL
Platelets 510 ×10³/µL
Na 133 mEq/L, K 3.1 mEq/L
HCO3 19 mEq/L
CRP 180 mg/L
ESR 70 mm/hr
Albumin 2.4 g/dL
Lactate 2.8 mmol/L
Stool C. difficile toxin PCR: pending
Blood cultures: drawn

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which immediate imaging study best evaluates the most dangerous complication suggested by his exam?
options: Abdominal MRI
Upright/supine abdominal radiograph (plain films)
Barium enema
Colonoscopy to the cecum
Right upper quadrant ultrasound
stages.explanation: A toxic-appearing UC patient with a distended, tympanitic abdomen is concerning for toxic megacolon; a plain abdominal radiograph is the fast, first-line study to measure transverse colon diameter (dilation >6 cm) and detect free air from perforation. MRI (option B) is slow and impractical in an unstable patient. Barium enema (option D) is contraindicated—it risks perforation in severe colitis. Full colonoscopy (option E) is dangerous in fulminant colitis/toxic megacolon due to perforation risk (limited flexible sigmoidoscopy may be used cautiously). RUQ ultrasound (option F) evaluates the biliary tree, not the colon.

stages.label: IMAGING & STOOL RESULTS
stages.content:
Abdominal radiograph: transverse colon dilated to 7.5 cm with loss of haustration and mucosal islands; no free air
C. difficile toxin PCR: negative
Stool culture and CMV studies: pending
Repeat exam: increasing distension, persistent fever, HR 124
CXR: no free air under the diaphragm

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The radiograph shows transverse colon dilation to 7.5 cm with systemic toxicity. What is the diagnosis, and which factor most likely precipitated it?
options: Irritable bowel syndrome; precipitated by stress
Toxic megacolon; precipitated by antimotility agents (loperamide), NSAIDs, hypokalemia, and stopping maintenance therapy
Diverticulitis; precipitated by low-fiber diet
Acute mesenteric ischemia; precipitated by an embolus
Small bowel obstruction; precipitated by adhesions
stages.explanation: Colonic dilation >6 cm plus systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, anemia) in severe colitis defines toxic megacolon. Antimotility agents (loperamide), opioids, anticholinergics, NSAIDs, hypokalemia, and abrupt cessation of maintenance therapy are classic precipitants—several present here. IBS (option B) never causes fever, bleeding, or toxicity. Diverticulitis (option D) causes focal LLQ disease, not pan-colonic toxic dilation. Mesenteric ischemia (option E) typically presents with pain out of proportion in older vasculopaths. SBO (option F) involves small bowel with different radiographic findings and no bloody diarrhea.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After resuscitation, what is the most appropriate initial medical management of toxic megacolon from severe UC?
options: Continue loperamide to slow the diarrhea
Oral mesalamine alone and observe
IV fluids and electrolyte repletion, bowel rest/NG decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, IV corticosteroids, stop offending agents, and urgent surgical consultation
Start oral vancomycin for presumed C. difficile
Begin an anti-TNF biologic as the sole therapy and discharge planning
stages.explanation: Initial management bundles aggressive IV fluids and potassium repletion, bowel rest with NG decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics (perforation/translocation risk), IV corticosteroids for the underlying severe UC, discontinuation of antimotility/NSAID agents, and—critically—early surgical consultation with serial exams and films. Loperamide (option B) caused/worsened the problem. Mesalamine alone (option C) is inadequate for fulminant disease. Empiric vancomycin (option E) is unwarranted with negative C. diff PCR. A biologic such as infliximab may be considered as medical rescue in steroid-refractory cases, but it is not sole first-line therapy and discharge is inappropriate (option F).

stages.label: TIMING SURGERY
stages.question: After 48-72 hours of maximal medical therapy he remains febrile with persistent colonic dilation and rising WBC, and develops worsening abdominal rigidity. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue IV steroids for another full week before reassessing
Proceed to urgent total/subtotal colectomy
Add a second antimotility agent
Discharge on oral steroids with close follow-up
Repeat colonoscopy to the cecum to reassess mucosa
stages.explanation: Failure to improve after 48-72 hours of maximal medical therapy, or any sign of perforation, uncontrolled hemorrhage, or worsening peritonitis (rigidity), is an indication for urgent total/subtotal colectomy. Prolonging steroids another week (option B) risks perforation and death. Antimotility agents (option D) are contraindicated and precipitate toxic megacolon. Discharge (option E) is unsafe in a deteriorating patient. Full colonoscopy (option F) carries high perforation risk in toxic megacolon and would not change the surgical indication. Timely surgery is lifesaving.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While stabilizing him before surgery, which preventive measure is appropriate despite his bloody diarrhea?
options: Withhold all VTE prophylaxis because of the GI bleeding
Provide pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (active IBD is strongly prothrombotic), reserving mechanical prophylaxis only if bleeding is life-threatening
Start full therapeutic anticoagulation empirically
Give prophylactic platelet transfusions
Begin aspirin for clot prevention
stages.explanation: Hospitalized patients with an active IBD flare have a markedly increased venous thromboembolism risk, and guidelines recommend pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis even with luminal GI bleeding, because the thrombotic risk usually outweighs the bleeding risk; mechanical prophylaxis is substituted only when bleeding is severe/life-threatening. Withholding all prophylaxis (option B) leaves a high-risk patient unprotected. Full therapeutic anticoagulation (option D) is not prophylaxis and would be dangerous without a documented clot. Prophylactic platelet transfusions (option E) are not indicated with a normal/elevated platelet count. Aspirin (option F) is not effective VTE prophylaxis and adds bleeding risk.

cases.diagnosis: Toxic megacolon complicating a severe ulcerative colitis flare
pearls: Toxic megacolon = colonic dilation >6 cm (transverse) plus systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, anemia/hypotension) in the setting of severe colitis.
Common precipitants: antimotility agents (loperamide/opioids), anticholinergics, NSAIDs, hypokalemia, barium enema/colonoscopy, and stopping maintenance therapy.
Avoid colonoscopy and barium studies in fulminant colitis—plain abdominal films track colonic diameter and detect free air.
If no improvement after 48-72 hours of maximal medical therapy, or if perforation/uncontrolled bleeding occurs, proceed to urgent colectomy.

cases.title: Watery Diarrhea After a Course of Antibiotics
cases.chiefComplaint: Profuse watery diarrhea and crampy abdominal pain that began after finishing antibiotics for a UTI.
cases.history: A 73-year-old woman, hospitalized 5 days ago for a complicated urinary tract infection, develops 8-10 watery, foul-smelling stools per day with lower abdominal cramping and low-grade fever. She completed a course of ciprofloxacin and was started on ceftriaxone during admission. She reports no blood in the stool but feels weak and has poor oral intake. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, GERD (on a daily proton pump inhibitor for years), and prior diverticulosis. Medications: lisinopril, metformin, omeprazole 40 mg daily, plus the recent antibiotics. Allergies: sulfa (rash). Social: lives in an assisted-living facility, non-smoker, no alcohol. Family history: non-contributory. She has had two prior hospitalizations this year. On review of systems she notes mild nausea but no vomiting, and reduced urine output today. No recent travel or sick contacts other than the facility. Her appetite is poor and she has not been drinking adequately.
cases.exam: Elderly woman appearing mildly dehydrated and uncomfortable. Mucous membranes dry. Tachycardic, regular. Lungs clear. Abdomen mildly distended with diffuse lower abdominal tenderness, no rebound or guarding, hyperactive bowel sounds. No palpable masses or organomegaly. No peritoneal signs at this time. Rectal exam: liquid brown stool, guaiac negative. Skin turgor reduced. No rash. Mild confusion noted by nursing compared with baseline, attributed to dehydration. Peripheral pulses intact; capillary refill slightly delayed.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which test best confirms the most likely diagnosis in this postantibiotic diarrhea?
options: Stool ova and parasite exam
Stool testing for C. difficile (toxin EIA plus GDH, or NAAT/PCR)
Colonoscopy with random biopsies
Abdominal CT as the confirmatory test
Blood cultures alone
stages.explanation: Recent broad-spectrum antibiotics (ciprofloxacin, ceftriaxone), PPI use, advanced age, and facility residence make C. difficile colitis most likely; the confirmatory test is stool C. difficile testing—either a NAAT/PCR or a multistep algorithm using GDH antigen plus toxin EIA—performed only on unformed stool. Ova/parasite testing (option B) targets parasitic causes, unlikely here. Colonoscopy (option D) is invasive and reserved for diagnostic uncertainty (pseudomembranes) or when stool testing is inconclusive. CT (option E) assesses complications, not the microbiologic diagnosis. Blood cultures (option F) are part of sepsis workup but do not diagnose C. difficile.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Stool C. difficile NAAT: positive; toxin EIA: positive
WBC 16.5 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL
Creatinine 1.7 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL)
Albumin 3.0 g/dL
Lactate 1.9 mmol/L
Na 134 mEq/L, K 3.3 mEq/L
Abdominal exam without peritoneal signs

stages.label: SEVERITY ASSESSMENT
stages.question: Using current severity criteria, how should this episode be classified?
options: Non-severe (mild) disease
Severe disease (WBC ≥15 ×10³/µL and/or creatinine ≥1.5 mg/dL or ≥1.5× baseline)
Fulminant disease
Asymptomatic carriage
Recurrent disease
stages.explanation: Severe C. difficile is defined by WBC ≥15,000/µL or serum creatinine ≥1.5 mg/dL (her creatinine rose from 0.9 to 1.7, nearly doubling, and WBC is 16,500). Non-severe disease (option B) requires values below those thresholds. Fulminant disease (option D) requires hypotension/shock, ileus, or toxic megacolon—not yet present despite tachycardia. Asymptomatic carriage (option E) is excluded by her active symptoms and positive toxin. Recurrent disease (option F) requires a prior treated episode within 8 weeks, which she does not have. Correct staging drives therapy choice.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for this episode (an initial, severe but non-fulminant case)?
options: IV metronidazole alone
Oral vancomycin or oral fidaxomicin, and discontinue the inciting antibiotics
Continue ceftriaxone and add loperamide
Oral metronidazole as the preferred first-line agent
Fecal microbiota transplant now as first-line therapy
stages.explanation: Current guidelines recommend oral vancomycin or oral fidaxomicin as first-line therapy for initial C. difficile (including severe, non-fulminant) and stopping the offending antibiotics whenever possible. IV metronidazole alone (option B) is inadequate except as an adjunct in fulminant disease (poor colonic levels). Continuing the inciting antibiotic and adding loperamide (option D) worsens disease and risks ileus/toxic megacolon. Oral metronidazole (option E) is now only an alternative for non-severe disease when preferred agents are unavailable. FMT (option F) is reserved for multiply recurrent disease, not the first episode.

stages.label: SUPPORTIVE & PREVENTION
stages.question: Besides starting the antibiotic, which additional measure is most appropriate now?
options: Add an antimotility agent to reduce stool frequency
Volume resuscitation with IV fluids/electrolytes, contact precautions with soap-and-water hand hygiene, and review the ongoing need for her PPI
Routine 'test of cure' stool PCR after symptoms resolve
