Start prophylactic probiotics as definitive therapy
Begin empiric antifungal therapy
stages.explanation: Supportive care includes IV fluids and electrolyte repletion (she is dehydrated with AKI and hypokalemia), contact precautions with soap-and-water hand hygiene (alcohol gel does not kill spores), and reassessing modifiable risks such as her chronic PPI. Antimotility agents (option B) are discouraged in active disease due to toxin retention and toxic megacolon risk. A 'test of cure' (option D) is not recommended—PCR can stay positive after clinical cure. Probiotics (option E) are not established definitive therapy. Antifungals (option F) have no role in C. difficile.

cases.diagnosis: Clostridioides difficile colitis (initial episode, severe but non-fulminant)
pearls: Risk factors: recent antibiotics (especially fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins, clindamycin), advanced age, hospitalization/facility residence, and PPI use.
Severity: severe = WBC ≥15,000/µL or creatinine ≥1.5 mg/dL; fulminant = hypotension/shock, ileus, or toxic megacolon.
First-line for initial disease is oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin; IV metronidazole is only an adjunct in fulminant disease.
Use soap-and-water hand hygiene (spores resist alcohol), avoid antimotility agents, and do not perform routine test-of-cure.

cases.title: Diabetic Man With Fatigue and Declining Kidney Numbers
cases.chiefComplaint: Routine diabetes follow-up with fatigue and worsening kidney function on recent labs.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes and longstanding hypertension presents for follow-up. He reports a few months of fatigue, mild lower-extremity swelling, and reduced exercise tolerance, but denies chest pain, dyspnea at rest, or gross hematuria. His glycemic control has been fair (recent A1c 8.1%). Prior labs show a slowly declining eGFR now at 28 mL/min/1.73 m². He has known diabetic retinopathy. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 8 years ago), rare alcohol, retired mechanic. Diet high in processed foods. Family history: father with diabetes and 'kidney failure' requiring dialysis. Review of systems notable for occasional muscle cramps and itchy skin; no nausea, no bone pain, no decreased urination. He takes occasional ibuprofen for back pain.
cases.exam: Overweight man in no acute distress. Fundoscopic exam consistent with diabetic retinopathy. No JVD; lungs clear; heart regular without murmurs or rubs. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no bruits auscultated, no palpable bladder. Mild bilateral pitting pedal edema (1+). Skin with diffuse excoriations from pruritus. No asterixis, no pericardial rub. Distal pulses intact; feet without ulcers but with reduced monofilament sensation. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Conjunctivae mildly pale.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Creatinine 2.6 mg/dL; eGFR 28 mL/min/1.73 m² (stable category G4)
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio: 780 mg/g
Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL (MCV 88 fL)
Ferritin 90 ng/mL, transferrin saturation 16%
Calcium 8.6 mg/dL, Phosphate 5.4 mg/dL
Intact PTH 220 pg/mL
25-OH vitamin D 18 ng/mL
Potassium 5.0 mEq/L, Bicarbonate 20 mEq/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How is his chronic kidney disease best categorized, and what is the most likely cause?
options: CKD stage G2 from analgesic nephropathy
Acute kidney injury from contrast
CKD stage G4, A3 most likely from diabetic nephropathy (with hypertension contributing)
Minimal change disease
Polycystic kidney disease
stages.explanation: eGFR 28 places him in CKD stage G4 (15-29), and an ACR of 780 mg/g is category A3 (severely increased albuminuria). In a long-standing diabetic with retinopathy, hypertension, and progressive albuminuric CKD, diabetic nephropathy is the most likely etiology, with hypertension contributing. Stage G2 (option B) is eGFR 60-89—incorrect. There is no contrast exposure or acute rise to suggest AKI (option C). Minimal change disease (option E) causes abrupt nephrotic syndrome, typically in children/young adults. PCKD (option F) would show enlarged cystic kidneys and a different history.

stages.label: ANEMIA MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His hemoglobin is 9.8 g/dL with TSAT 16% and ferritin 90 ng/mL. What is the most appropriate next step for the anemia of CKD?
options: Immediately start a high-dose erythropoiesis-stimulating agent targeting hemoglobin >13 g/dL
Repleting iron (often IV in CKD) first, since iron deficiency is present and limits erythropoiesis
Transfuse packed red cells now
Begin vitamin B12 and folate supplementation
No action; this hemoglobin is normal for his age
stages.explanation: Anemia of CKD is multifactorial, but absolute/functional iron deficiency must be corrected first—TSAT 16% (low) with ferritin 90 indicates iron-limited erythropoiesis, and IV iron is often preferred in CKD. ESAs are considered only after iron repletion and are targeted to avoid hemoglobin >11.5 g/dL (never >13, which increases cardiovascular and thrombotic risk—option B). Transfusion (option D) is reserved for symptomatic severe anemia and risks alloimmunization in transplant candidates. Normocytic anemia with normal indices argues against B12/folate deficiency (option E). Hgb 9.8 is not normal (option F).

stages.label: MINERAL BONE DISEASE
stages.question: His phosphate is 5.4 mg/dL, calcium 8.6 mg/dL, PTH 220 pg/mL, and vitamin D 18 ng/mL. What is the most appropriate initial management of his CKD-mineral bone disorder?
options: Start high-dose IV calcitriol immediately to suppress PTH
Dietary phosphate restriction and a phosphate binder, plus correcting vitamin D deficiency
Begin cinacalcet as first-line therapy now
Aggressively raise serum calcium with oral calcium carbonate to >11 mg/dL
No intervention until he requires dialysis
stages.explanation: Secondary hyperparathyroidism in CKD is driven by phosphate retention, low calcitriol, and vitamin D deficiency. Initial steps are dietary phosphate restriction and a phosphate binder to control hyperphosphatemia, plus repleting nutritional vitamin D (level 18 is deficient). Aggressive IV calcitriol up front (option B) risks hypercalcemia/hyperphosphatemia and vascular calcification. Cinacalcet (option D) is used for refractory secondary hyperparathyroidism, generally in dialysis patients, not as the first step. Pushing calcium to >11 (option E) promotes vascular calcification. Waiting for dialysis (option F) allows progressive bone disease and calcification.

stages.label: MEDICATION REVIEW
stages.content:
Current eGFR 28 mL/min/1.73 m²
Still taking metformin 1000 mg twice daily
Taking occasional ibuprofen for back pain
BP today 146/86 mmHg on lisinopril and amlodipine
Not on an SGLT2 inhibitor
Not yet referred to nephrology

stages.label: NEXT STEPS & REFERRAL
stages.question: Which set of changes is most appropriate now?
options: Increase metformin and continue PRN ibuprofen
Discontinue metformin at this eGFR, stop NSAIDs, add an SGLT2 inhibitor, optimize BP and RAAS blockade, and refer to nephrology
Stop lisinopril because of the albuminuria
Refer to nephrology only once he reaches eGFR <10 or needs dialysis
Start a potassium supplement for his borderline potassium
stages.explanation: At eGFR 28, metformin should be discontinued (contraindicated <30 due to lactic acidosis risk), NSAIDs stopped (nephrotoxic, worsen CKD/hyperkalemia), and an SGLT2 inhibitor added for cardiorenal protection in albuminuric diabetic CKD. Continue RAAS blockade for proteinuria and intensify BP control. Nephrology referral is indicated at G4 (eGFR <30), heavy albuminuria, or rapid progression—not deferred to near-dialysis (option E). Increasing metformin/continuing NSAIDs (option B) is harmful. Stopping lisinopril (option D) removes a key antiproteinuric agent. He has hyperkalemia tendency, so potassium supplementation (option F) is contraindicated.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic kidney disease stage G4 A3, most likely diabetic nephropathy, with anemia of CKD and CKD-mineral bone disorder
pearls: Stage CKD by eGFR (G1-G5) AND albuminuria (A1-A3); diabetic nephropathy is the leading cause and usually accompanies retinopathy.
Correct iron deficiency (often IV iron) before ESAs; target hemoglobin ~10-11.5 g/dL and avoid >13 g/dL.
Manage CKD-MBD with phosphate restriction/binders and vitamin D repletion; reserve cinacalcet and active vitamin D for refractory secondary hyperparathyroidism.
Stop metformin at eGFR <30 and avoid NSAIDs; add SGLT2 inhibitors and RAAS blockade, and refer to nephrology at G4 or with heavy albuminuria.

cases.title: Frothy Urine and Swelling All Over
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of worsening swelling in the legs, face, and around the eyes, with frothy urine.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man presents with 2-3 weeks of progressive bilateral leg swelling, periorbital puffiness worst in the mornings, and noticeably foamy urine. He has gained about 5 kg over this period and reports mild fatigue. He denies hematuria, dysuria, joint pains, rashes, fevers, or recent infections, and has no shortness of breath at rest. No chest pain or calf pain. PMH: previously healthy, no diabetes, no known hypertension. Medications: none regularly; took an NSAID a few times for a headache. Allergies: none. Social: works in finance, non-smoker, social alcohol, no IV drug use; recently returned from a long-haul flight 1 week ago. Family history: unremarkable for kidney disease. Review of systems negative for hemoptysis, sinus symptoms, oral ulcers, or photosensitivity. He has not had recent strep throat. No new medications or herbal supplements. He is otherwise active and works full-time.
cases.exam: Middle-aged man with notable periorbital and facial edema. No JVD; lungs clear without crackles. Heart regular, no murmurs or rub. Abdomen soft, mild shifting dullness suggesting modest ascites, non-tender, no organomegaly. Lower extremities with 3+ pitting edema to the thighs and mild scrotal edema. No rash, no synovitis, no palpable purpura. Calves symmetric and non-tender, no cords (assessing for DVT). No xanthelasma. Neurologic exam normal. The dominant findings are diffuse edema and anasarca-type fluid distribution without signs of heart failure or infection.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
Urinalysis: 4+ protein, no blood, oval fat bodies and fatty casts present
Urine protein-to-creatinine ratio: 9.2 g/g
Serum albumin 2.1 g/dL
Total cholesterol 320 mg/dL, LDL elevated
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL, eGFR ~90
Serum sodium 138 mEq/L, glucose 92 mg/dL
A1c 5.4%

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings defines the syndrome he has?
options: Hematuria, red cell casts, hypertension, and oliguria
Proteinuria >3.5 g/day, hypoalbuminemia, edema, and hyperlipidemia
Pyuria, dysuria, and bacteriuria
Isolated microscopic hematuria with normal protein
Elevated creatinine with hyperkalemia and metabolic acidosis only
stages.explanation: Nephrotic syndrome is defined by heavy proteinuria (>3.5 g/day; his UPCR is 9.2 g/g), hypoalbuminemia (2.1 g/dL), edema, and hyperlipidemia—all present, with bland urine showing oval fat bodies/fatty casts. Hematuria with RBC casts, hypertension, and oliguria (option B) describes a nephritic picture. Pyuria/dysuria/bacteriuria (option D) is urinary tract infection. Isolated hematuria (option E) is not nephrotic. Elevated creatinine with hyperkalemia and acidosis alone (option F) describes renal failure features, not the defining nephrotic tetrad.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: After confirming nephrotic-range proteinuria, what is the most appropriate next step to determine the underlying glomerular disease in this adult?
options: Empiric high-dose corticosteroids without further testing
Serologic/secondary workup followed by renal biopsy for definitive histologic diagnosis
Renal ultrasound alone to make the diagnosis
24-hour urine sodium only
Begin chronic anticoagulation and reassess in 6 months
stages.explanation: In adults, nephrotic syndrome warrants a workup for secondary causes (e.g., glucose/A1c, hepatitis B/C, HIV, ANA, complement, SPEP/free light chains, anti-PLA2R) followed by renal biopsy, which establishes the specific histologic diagnosis (e.g., FSGS, membranous, minimal change) and guides therapy. Empiric steroids without histology (option B) is appropriate in children (presumed minimal change) but not in adults. Ultrasound (option D) assesses size/obstruction, not glomerular pathology. A 24-hour urine sodium (option E) does not diagnose the cause. Starting anticoagulation alone (option F) ignores the need for diagnosis and treatment of the underlying disease.

stages.label: BIOPSY & SEROLOGIES
stages.content:
Hepatitis B/C, HIV: negative
ANA negative; C3/C4 normal; SPEP/free light chains normal
Anti-PLA2R antibody: positive
Renal biopsy: diffuse thickening of glomerular basement membranes with subepithelial immune deposits ('spikes' on silver stain); IF with granular IgG and C3 along the GBM
No crescents; no significant interstitial fibrosis

stages.label: THROMBOEMBOLISM RISK
stages.question: One week later he develops acute right flank pain, gross hematuria, and worsening kidney function after his long flight. Which complication of nephrotic syndrome is most likely, and why is he at risk?
options: Acute pyelonephritis from urinary stasis
Renal vein thrombosis due to a hypercoagulable state from urinary loss of antithrombin III and other anticoagulant proteins
Nephrolithiasis from hypercalciuria
Contrast-induced nephropathy
Renal artery dissection from hypertension
stages.explanation: Nephrotic syndrome—especially membranous nephropathy—causes a hypercoagulable state through urinary loss of antithrombin III, altered procoagulant/anticoagulant balance, and hemoconcentration; the classic complication is renal vein thrombosis, presenting with flank pain, hematuria, and worsening renal function, often precipitated by immobility (his long flight). Pyelonephritis (option B) would feature fever, pyuria, and bacteriuria. Stones (option D) are not typical of this presentation. He had no contrast (option E). Renal artery dissection (option F) is rare and not linked to nephrotic physiology. Membranous carries the highest VTE risk among nephrotic causes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given biopsy-proven PLA2R-positive membranous nephropathy with a confirmed thrombotic complication, which management plan is most appropriate?
options: Anticoagulation alone and no immunosuppression
Therapeutic anticoagulation for the thrombosis, RAAS blockade and a statin for the proteinuria/lipids, plus immunosuppression (e.g., rituximab) for high-risk membranous nephropathy
Diuretics only and outpatient observation
Empiric antibiotics for presumed infection
Immediate dialysis initiation
stages.explanation: This patient needs therapeutic anticoagulation for renal vein thrombosis, RAAS blockade (ACE inhibitor/ARB) to reduce proteinuria, a statin for nephrotic hyperlipidemia, and immunosuppression for primary (PLA2R-positive) membranous nephropathy with high-risk features such as heavy persistent proteinuria—rituximab is a preferred agent. Anticoagulation alone (option B) ignores the underlying disease and proteinuria. Diuretics only (option D) treats edema but not disease or clot. Antibiotics (option E) are unwarranted without infection. Dialysis (option F) is not indicated—his eGFR is preserved and there is no dialysis indication.

stages.label: PREVENTIVE CARE
stages.question: Which additional preventive issue is most important to address given his nephrotic syndrome?
options: Routine prophylactic antibiotics for life
Vaccination (including pneumococcal) and infection awareness, since urinary loss of immunoglobulins and complement increases infection risk
Strict bed rest to reduce proteinuria
A high-protein diet to replace urinary losses
Avoiding all vaccines because of immunosuppression
stages.explanation: Nephrotic patients lose immunoglobulins and complement factors in the urine, raising the risk of infections such as pneumococcal disease and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis; pneumococcal (and other indicated) vaccination plus infection vigilance is important, and timing should account for planned immunosuppression. Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics (option B) are not standard. Strict bed rest (option D) increases thrombosis risk and is harmful. A high-protein diet (option E) does not correct hypoalbuminemia and may worsen proteinuria—moderate protein intake is advised. Avoiding all vaccines (option F) is wrong; appropriate (especially inactivated) vaccines are encouraged, ideally before immunosuppression.

cases.diagnosis: Primary (PLA2R-positive) membranous nephropathy presenting as nephrotic syndrome, complicated by renal vein thrombosis
pearls: Nephrotic syndrome = proteinuria >3.5 g/day, hypoalbuminemia, edema, and hyperlipidemia, with bland urine and oval fat bodies/fatty casts.
In adults, pursue secondary workup and renal biopsy; anti-PLA2R positivity supports primary membranous nephropathy.
Nephrotic syndrome is hypercoagulable (urinary loss of antithrombin III); membranous nephropathy carries the highest risk of renal vein thrombosis/VTE.
Treat with RAAS blockade and statin plus disease-specific immunosuppression (e.g., rituximab for high-risk membranous), and anticoagulate confirmed thromboembolism.

cases.title: Incidental High Calcium With Fatigue and Stones
cases.chiefComplaint: Referred for an incidentally elevated calcium found on routine bloodwork, with fatigue and a history of kidney stones.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman is referred after routine labs showed an elevated calcium. She reports months of vague fatigue, mild constipation, increased thirst, and occasional dull bone aches, which she attributed to aging. She has had two episodes of kidney stones over the past 3 years. She denies bone fractures, recent immobilization, vitamin/calcium megadosing, or symptoms of malignancy such as weight loss or night sweats. No nausea, vomiting, or confusion. PMH: hypertension, osteopenia on a prior DXA, nephrolithiasis. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, calcium/vitamin D supplement (modest dose). Allergies: none. Social: non-smoker, rare alcohol, works as a librarian. Family history: a sister with a 'parathyroid operation'; no family history of MEN syndromes that she knows of. Review of systems otherwise negative. She drinks coffee but stays hydrated. She has not had recent immobilization or excessive supplement intake.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman in no distress. Mentation normal; no confusion. Neck supple, no palpable thyroid nodules or masses, no lymphadenopathy. Lungs clear; heart regular. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no masses or organomegaly. No focal bone tenderness; no joint effusions. Neurologic exam non-focal with intact strength and normal reflexes. No proximal muscle weakness on testing. No band keratopathy noted. Mild dry mucous membranes. Overall exam is largely unremarkable apart from mild hypertension.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Calcium 11.6 mg/dL (corrected; albumin normal)
Ionized calcium: elevated
Intact PTH 96 pg/mL (inappropriately high-normal/elevated)
Phosphate 2.4 mg/dL (low)
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL, eGFR ~70
25-OH vitamin D 22 ng/mL
24-hour urine calcium: 320 mg/24h (elevated)

stages.label: PTH INTERPRETATION
stages.question: Given the high calcium with an inappropriately non-suppressed PTH and low phosphate, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Hypercalcemia of malignancy (PTHrP-mediated)
Primary hyperparathyroidism
Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia
Vitamin D intoxication
Milk-alkali syndrome
stages.explanation: Hypercalcemia with an inappropriately normal-to-high PTH (it should be suppressed when calcium is high), accompanied by hypophosphatemia and hypercalciuria, is classic for primary hyperparathyroidism. In hypercalcemia of malignancy (option B), PTH is suppressed (low). Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (option D) shows LOW urine calcium (her 24-hour urine calcium is elevated at 320 mg), arguing against it. Vitamin D intoxication (option E) and milk-alkali syndrome (option F) cause PTH-independent hypercalcemia with a suppressed PTH. The non-suppressed PTH localizes the problem to the parathyroid glands.

stages.label: MEDICATION REVIEW
stages.question: Which of her medications could be contributing to the hypercalcemia and should be reconsidered first?
options: Hydrochlorothiazide
Lisinopril
Aspirin
Metformin
Atorvastatin
stages.explanation: Thiazide diuretics like hydrochlorothiazide reduce urinary calcium excretion and can raise serum calcium, potentially unmasking or worsening hypercalcemia; it should be reconsidered (often stopped) and calcium rechecked. Lisinopril (option B) does not raise calcium. Aspirin (option C), metformin (option D), and atorvastatin (option E) are not causes of hypercalcemia. Note that even after stopping a thiazide, her inappropriately elevated PTH still points to primary hyperparathyroidism, but removing contributing drugs is a sensible first step in interpretation.

stages.label: ADDITIONAL DATA
stages.content:
DXA: T-score -2.6 at the distal radius (osteoporosis range)
Renal ultrasound: small nonobstructing stones in the right kidney
Repeat corrected calcium after holding HCTZ: 11.5 mg/dL
Neck ultrasound/sestamibi: single enlarged right inferior parathyroid adenoma
Age 58; eGFR ~70

stages.label: SURGICAL CRITERIA
stages.question: Which findings meet criteria for parathyroidectomy in primary hyperparathyroidism?
options: Only symptomatic patients with overt hypercalcemic crisis qualify
Calcium >1 mg/dL above the upper limit of normal, osteoporosis (T-score ≤ -2.5) or fragility fracture, age <50, eGFR <60, or nephrolithiasis/marked hypercalciuria
Surgery is contraindicated once stones have formed
Only patients with multigland disease qualify
PTH must exceed 500 pg/mL before surgery is considered
stages.explanation: Parathyroidectomy is indicated for serum calcium >1 mg/dL above normal, evidence of skeletal disease (osteoporosis with T-score ≤ -2.5 or fragility fracture), age <50, renal involvement (eGFR <60, nephrolithiasis, or significant hypercalciuria). She qualifies via osteoporosis (T -2.6) and nephrolithiasis. Surgery is not limited to hypercalcemic crisis (option B). Stones are an indication for, not a contraindication to (option D), surgery. Single-adenoma disease is the most common surgical target—multigland disease is not required (option E). There is no specific PTH threshold of 500 (option F).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She meets surgical criteria and has a localized single adenoma. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Lifelong cinacalcet as the preferred definitive treatment
Parathyroidectomy (focused/minimally invasive given a localized single adenoma)
Long-term bisphosphonate alone as definitive cure
Increase calcium and vitamin D supplementation
Continue the thiazide and observe indefinitely
stages.explanation: Parathyroidectomy is the definitive, curative treatment for primary hyperparathyroidism, and a localized single adenoma allows a focused/minimally invasive approach. Cinacalcet (option B) lowers calcium but is not curative and is reserved for patients who cannot have surgery. Bisphosphonates (option D) help bone density but do not cure the disease or correct hypercalcemia/PTH excess. Increasing calcium/vitamin D (option E) would worsen hypercalcemia. Continuing a thiazide and observing (option F) is inappropriate given she meets clear operative criteria; surgery offers cure and protects bone and kidneys.

cases.diagnosis: Primary hyperparathyroidism due to a single parathyroid adenoma
pearls: Hypercalcemia with non-suppressed (inappropriately normal/high) PTH = primary hyperparathyroidism; expect low phosphate and high urine calcium.
Distinguish from familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia, which shows LOW urinary calcium (low calcium/creatinine clearance ratio).
Thiazides raise serum calcium and should be reconsidered during evaluation of hypercalcemia.
Operate for calcium >1 mg/dL above normal, age <50, osteoporosis/fracture, eGFR <60, or nephrolithiasis—parathyroidectomy is curative.

cases.title: Confused and Dehydrated With a Very High Calcium
cases.chiefComplaint: Brought in confused and lethargic, found to have a markedly elevated calcium.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man is admitted with 1 week of worsening confusion, lethargy, constipation, marked thirst, and polyuria. His family reports decreased oral intake, generalized weakness, and a 9 kg unintentional weight loss over 3 months, plus a chronic cough. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history. He denies any history of parathyroid disease, kidney stones, or excessive calcium/vitamin D intake. PMH: COPD, hypertension. Medications: tiotropium inhaler, amlodipine. Allergies: none. Social: current smoker, moderate prior alcohol use, retired construction worker with possible asbestos exposure. Family history: non-contributory. Review of systems notable for the chronic cough, weight loss, and recent intermittent hemoptysis. He has had no fever. On arrival he is somnolent but arousable, oriented only to person. The combination of constitutional symptoms, heavy smoking, and severe hypercalcemia raises concern for an underlying malignancy.
cases.exam: Somnolent but arousable man, oriented to person only, with dry mucous membranes and poor skin turgor. Neck supple, no thyromegaly; a firm right supraclavicular lymph node is palpable. Lungs with decreased breath sounds at the right base and scattered rhonchi. Heart tachycardic, regular. Abdomen soft, mildly distended, hypoactive bowel sounds, non-tender. No focal motor deficit; reflexes diminished; no asterixis. Mild generalized muscle weakness. Clubbing of the fingers noted. The exam is notable for volume depletion, altered mentation, lymphadenopathy, and pulmonary findings.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Calcium 14.2 mg/dL (corrected)
Intact PTH: 6 pg/mL (suppressed/low)
Phosphate: low-normal
Creatinine 1.9 mg/dL (likely from dehydration)
Albumin 3.4 g/dL
ECG: shortened QT interval
Chest imaging pending
Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With calcium 14.2 mg/dL and a suppressed PTH in a heavy smoker with weight loss and a lung lesion, what mechanism is most likely?
options: Primary hyperparathyroidism
Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy mediated by PTH-related protein (PTHrP)
Familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia
Vitamin D intoxication
Thiazide-induced hypercalcemia
stages.explanation: Severe hypercalcemia with a suppressed PTH in a heavy smoker with weight loss, lymphadenopathy, and a lung lesion points to hypercalcemia of malignancy—most often humoral hypercalcemia mediated by PTHrP (classically squamous cell lung cancer). The suppressed PTH excludes primary hyperparathyroidism (option B), where PTH is inappropriately high. FHH (option D) causes mild, lifelong hypercalcemia with low urine calcium, not acute severe disease with cancer features. Vitamin D intoxication (option E) and thiazide effect (option F) are PTH-independent but do not fit the malignant constitutional picture; confirmatory testing would show elevated PTHrP.

stages.label: INITIAL MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important first step in treating his severe symptomatic hypercalcemia?
options: Loop diuretic (furosemide) as the first-line agent
Aggressive isotonic IV saline volume resuscitation
Oral phosphate binders
Immediate hemodialysis
Restrict all IV fluids to avoid fluid overload
stages.explanation: The cornerstone first step is aggressive isotonic (normal) saline to restore intravascular volume and promote calciuresis; these patients are markedly volume-depleted from calcium-induced nephrogenic diabetes insipidus and poor intake. Loop diuretics (option B) are NOT first-line and are used only after the patient is volume-replete and only if needed (e.g., volume overload)—giving furosemide first worsens dehydration. Phosphate binders (option D) do not acutely lower calcium. Dialysis (option E) is reserved for severe hypercalcemia with renal failure or refractory cases. Restricting fluids (option F) is dangerous and worsens hypercalcemia.

stages.label: PHARMACOLOGIC THERAPY
stages.question: After starting IV fluids, which combination best addresses both the immediate and the sustained lowering of calcium?
options: Calcitonin alone for long-term control
Calcitonin for rapid, short-term calcium lowering plus an IV bisphosphonate (e.g., zoledronic acid) for durable control
A thiazide diuretic to increase calcium excretion
High-dose vitamin D to suppress PTHrP
Oral calcium carbonate to balance levels
stages.explanation: Calcitonin works within hours to rapidly lower calcium but tachyphylaxis limits it to a few days; an IV bisphosphonate such as zoledronic acid provides durable lowering by inhibiting osteoclastic bone resorption (onset ~2-4 days). Combining them bridges the gap between fast and sustained control. Calcitonin alone (option B) loses effect quickly. Thiazides (option D) raise calcium and are contraindicated. Vitamin D (option E) would worsen hypercalcemia and does not suppress PTHrP. Oral calcium (option F) is obviously contraindicated. Denosumab is an alternative for bisphosphonate-refractory or renal-impaired patients.

stages.label: SAFETY CHECK
stages.question: Before giving IV zoledronic acid to this patient with a creatinine of 1.9 mg/dL, which action is most important?
options: Give the full dose rapidly without adjustment to maximize effect
Ensure adequate volume repletion and assess/adjust dosing for renal function, since IV bisphosphonates can be nephrotoxic and require caution in renal impairment
Withhold IV fluids to avoid diluting the drug
Add a thiazide to enhance the bisphosphonate effect
Give oral calcium with the infusion to prevent rebound
stages.explanation: IV bisphosphonates such as zoledronic acid can be nephrotoxic and are dosed/used cautiously in renal impairment; ensuring the patient is volume-replete first (which also independently lowers calcium) and adjusting for renal function reduces risk. His creatinine of 1.9 is largely from dehydration, so hydration is doubly beneficial. Rapid full dosing without adjustment (option B) increases nephrotoxicity. Withholding fluids (option D) worsens both hypercalcemia and kidney injury. A thiazide (option E) raises calcium and is contraindicated. Oral calcium (option F) would counteract therapy. Denosumab is an alternative when renal function precludes bisphosphonates.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC WORKUP
stages.content:
CT chest: large right hilar mass with mediastinal and supraclavicular lymphadenopathy
PTHrP level: elevated
25-OH and 1,25-OH vitamin D: not elevated
Biopsy of supraclavicular node: non-small cell lung carcinoma (squamous cell)
Calcium after 48h of fluids + calcitonin + zoledronic acid: 11.0 mg/dL and improving mentation

stages.label: DEFINITIVE DIRECTION
stages.question: His calcium is improving with supportive measures. What is the most appropriate next step regarding the underlying cause?
options: Schedule parathyroidectomy
Treat the underlying malignancy (oncology referral/staging) since controlling the cancer is essential for durable calcium control
Start cinacalcet as definitive therapy for the hypercalcemia
Discharge now with no further oncologic workup
Begin lifelong calcitonin monotherapy
stages.explanation: Hypercalcemia of malignancy reflects active tumor burden; durable control ultimately depends on treating the underlying cancer, so the key next step is oncology referral, staging, and a treatment plan for the squamous cell lung carcinoma. Parathyroidectomy (option B) is wrong—the parathyroids are suppressed and not the source. Cinacalcet (option D) is for hyperparathyroidism, not PTHrP-mediated disease. Discharging without oncologic workup (option E) abandons the actual cause. Calcitonin monotherapy (option F) is short-lived and not definitive. Supportive measures buy time while definitive cancer-directed therapy is arranged.

cases.diagnosis: Hypercalcemia of malignancy (PTHrP-mediated) due to squamous cell lung carcinoma
pearls: Hypercalcemia + suppressed PTH + constitutional/cancer features = hypercalcemia of malignancy; confirm with PTHrP (humoral, classically squamous cell) and check vitamin D metabolites.
First-line treatment is aggressive isotonic saline; loop diuretics are NOT routine and only after volume repletion if overloaded.
Calcitonin lowers calcium fast but briefly (tachyphylaxis); pair it with an IV bisphosphonate (or denosumab) for durable control.
Lasting calcium control requires treating the underlying malignancy.

cases.title: Abnormal ECG in a Dialysis-Risk Patient
cases.chiefComplaint: Generalized weakness and palpitations with a strikingly abnormal ECG in the emergency department.
cases.history: A 69-year-old man with chronic kidney disease (stage G4, baseline creatinine ~3 mg/dL) presents to the ED with 2 days of generalized weakness, mild nausea, and palpitations. He missed his last nephrology visit and has been taking over-the-counter ibuprofen for back pain. He reports no chest pain or dyspnea but feels 'off.' PMH: type 2 diabetes, hypertension, CKD, heart failure with preserved EF. Medications: lisinopril 40 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, metoprolol, insulin, plus recent ibuprofen; he also started a salt substitute (potassium-based) to cut sodium. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker, no alcohol, lives alone. Family history: non-contributory. Review of systems notable for decreased urine output over the last day. He has not had recent diarrhea or vomiting. The combination of CKD, ACE inhibitor, potassium-sparing diuretic, NSAID, and a potassium-containing salt substitute is a classic recipe for dangerous potassium accumulation.
cases.exam: Older man appearing fatigued. Bradycardic, regular rhythm. Lungs with mild bibasilar crackles. Heart sounds normal, no murmurs. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no organomegaly. Mild bilateral lower-extremity edema. Neurologic exam with generalized mild weakness, intact sensation, diminished deep tendon reflexes. No focal deficits. Mucous membranes moist. No flank tenderness. The bradycardia and diffuse weakness in the context of his medications and CKD are concerning for an electrolyte emergency, prompting an immediate ECG.

stages.label: ECG & POINT-OF-CARE LABS
stages.content:
ECG: tall, peaked, narrow-based T waves; PR prolongation; QRS beginning to widen; bradycardia
Point-of-care potassium: 7.2 mEq/L
Creatinine 4.8 mg/dL (up from baseline ~3)
Bicarbonate 18 mEq/L
Glucose 150 mg/dL
Calcium 8.8 mg/dL

stages.label: IMMEDIATE PRIORITY
stages.question: With K 7.2 mEq/L and peaked T waves plus QRS widening, what is the most important immediate intervention?
options: IV insulin with glucose to shift potassium intracellularly
IV calcium (gluconate or chloride) to stabilize the cardiac membrane
Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate
Nebulized albuterol
Emergent hemodialysis as the first action
stages.explanation: When hyperkalemia produces ECG changes (peaked T waves, widening QRS), the first action is IV calcium to stabilize the myocardial membrane and prevent lethal arrhythmia; it acts within minutes but does not lower potassium. Insulin/glucose (option B) and albuterol (option E) shift potassium intracellularly and are given next, but membrane protection comes first when ECG changes are present. Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (option D) acts slowly over hours and is not for emergencies. Dialysis (option F) is the definitive removal therapy but is not the immediate first step before membrane stabilization.

stages.label: SHIFTING POTASSIUM
stages.question: After cardiac stabilization, which measures temporarily shift potassium into cells?
options: IV calcium and oral potassium binder
IV regular insulin with dextrose, and nebulized beta-2 agonist (albuterol); consider bicarbonate if acidotic
Loop diuretic alone
Normal saline bolus alone
Spironolactone and an ACE inhibitor
stages.explanation: Insulin (with dextrose to prevent hypoglycemia) and high-dose nebulized albuterol drive potassium intracellularly within minutes to an hour; sodium bicarbonate can help when metabolic acidosis is present. IV calcium stabilizes but does not shift potassium, and binders remove rather than shift it (option B). A loop diuretic (option D) removes potassium via the kidney (useful if the patient makes urine) but is a removal, not a shifting, strategy and is slower. Saline alone (option E) does not meaningfully shift potassium. Spironolactone and ACE inhibitors (option F) RAISE potassium and must be stopped.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE REMOVAL
stages.question: His potassium remains elevated, urine output is minimal, creatinine is rising, and he has volume overload. What is the most appropriate definitive potassium-removal strategy?
options: Repeat insulin/glucose every hour indefinitely
Hemodialysis
More IV calcium
Increase the ACE inhibitor dose
Add a potassium-based salt substitute to balance electrolytes
stages.explanation: Shifting agents are temporary; potassium must actually be removed from the body. In a patient with advanced/worsening kidney failure, oliguria, refractory hyperkalemia, and volume overload, hemodialysis is the definitive removal therapy. Repeating insulin/glucose (option B) only buys short-term time and risks hypoglycemia. More calcium (option D) protects the membrane but removes no potassium. Increasing the ACE inhibitor (option E) and adding a potassium-based salt substitute (option F) would dangerously worsen hyperkalemia. In patients who still make urine, loop diuretics or GI potassium binders may suffice, but this patient needs dialysis.

stages.label: ROOT CAUSE
stages.question: Which intervention will most reduce his risk of recurrent hyperkalemia?
options: Continue all current medications unchanged
Stop the NSAID, hold/adjust the ACE inhibitor and spironolactone, discontinue the potassium-based salt substitute, and provide dietary potassium counseling
Add a second potassium-sparing diuretic
Encourage a high-potassium diet to 'train' his kidneys
Start a potassium supplement at discharge
stages.explanation: Recurrence prevention means removing the offending contributors: stop the NSAID (reduces renal perfusion and impairs potassium excretion, worsening AKI on CKD), hold or adjust the RAAS agents (ACE inhibitor and spironolactone both raise potassium), discontinue the potassium-based salt substitute, and counsel on a low-potassium diet. Continuing everything (option B) repeats the crisis. Adding another potassium-sparing diuretic (option D) or a potassium supplement (option F) would raise potassium further. A high-potassium diet (option E) is dangerous in CKD. Newer oral binders (patiromer/SZC) can help if RAAS therapy must continue.

cases.diagnosis: Severe hyperkalemia (K 7.2 mEq/L) with ECG changes in the setting of CKD, RAAS blockade, NSAID use, and a potassium-based salt substitute
pearls: ECG changes (peaked T waves -> PR/QRS widening -> sine wave) signal cardiac toxicity—give IV calcium first to stabilize the membrane.
Shift potassium with insulin/glucose and albuterol (and bicarbonate if acidotic); these are temporary measures.
Definitive removal is via the kidney (loop diuretics if urine output preserved), GI binders, or hemodialysis for refractory hyperkalemia/renal failure.
Hunt for and stop precipitants: NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, potassium-sparing diuretics, and potassium-containing salt substitutes, especially in CKD.

cases.title: A Hot, Swollen Knee With Fever
cases.chiefComplaint: One day of severe pain, swelling, warmth, and redness in the right knee with inability to bear weight, plus fever.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents to the ED with 1 day of rapidly worsening right knee pain, swelling, warmth, and erythema, and is unable to bear weight or bend the knee. He has felt feverish with chills. He recalls no significant trauma but had a minor abrasion over the knee while gardening last week and notes a recent skin infection on his shin. PMH: type 2 diabetes, prior gout in the great toe, and hypertension. Medications: metformin, lisinopril; he takes no chronic urate-lowering therapy. Allergies: none. Social: drinks alcohol on weekends, non-smoker, no IV drug use, monogamous. Family history: father with gout. Review of systems negative for other joint involvement, rash, urethral discharge, or recent tick exposure. He reports the knee is far more painful than his prior gout attacks and the fever is new. He has not started any new medications. The monoarticular presentation with fever raises immediate concern for a joint-space infection.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable man guarding the right knee. The knee is swollen with a large effusion, warm, erythematous, and exquisitely tender; even minimal passive range of motion causes severe pain, and he holds it slightly flexed. No other joints are inflamed. Overlying skin shows a healing abrasion and a small area of cellulitis on the shin. Distal pulses intact; foot neurovascularly normal. No tophi visualized at the knee. No murmurs on cardiac auscultation. No lymphangitic streaking proximally. The single hot, swollen joint with fever and refusal to move it is the key finding.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most important immediate diagnostic step for this acute, hot, swollen monoarthritis with fever?
options: Start empiric antibiotics and observe the response before any procedure
Emergent arthrocentesis of the knee with synovial fluid analysis before antibiotics, if feasible
Order serum uric acid to confirm gout
Obtain an MRI of the knee first
Begin oral colchicine and reassess in 24 hours
stages.explanation: Septic arthritis is a joint-threatening emergency, and prompt arthrocentesis with synovial fluid analysis (cell count, Gram stain, culture, crystals) is the key diagnostic step—ideally before antibiotics so cultures are not falsely negative, as long as it does not delay treatment in an unstable patient. Empiric antibiotics without tapping (option B) sterilizes cultures and delays diagnosis. Serum uric acid (option D) is neither sensitive nor specific and is often normal or low during an acute attack. MRI (option E) wastes time and is not needed for diagnosis. Colchicine (option F) treats gout but would dangerously delay treatment if this is infection.

stages.label: SYNOVIAL FLUID ANALYSIS
stages.content:
Synovial fluid: opaque, yellow, low viscosity
WBC 78,000 /µL with 92% neutrophils
Gram stain: gram-positive cocci in clusters
Crystals: none seen under polarized light
Synovial fluid culture: pending
Serum WBC 14.8 ×10³/µL; CRP 160 mg/L; blood cultures drawn

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: How should this synovial fluid be interpreted?
options: Normal/non-inflammatory fluid
Highly inflammatory with a positive Gram stain—consistent with septic (bacterial) arthritis, most likely Staphylococcus aureus
Crystal-proven acute gout
Hemarthrosis from trauma
Reactive arthritis
stages.explanation: A synovial WBC of 78,000/µL with neutrophil predominance is highly inflammatory and, combined with gram-positive cocci in clusters, strongly indicates bacterial septic arthritis—most commonly Staphylococcus aureus, especially with a skin source. Non-inflammatory fluid (option B) has WBC <2,000. The absence of crystals plus a positive Gram stain argues against gout (option D), and serum uric acid is unreliable acutely. Hemarthrosis (option E) would be grossly bloody, not purulent. Reactive arthritis (option F) is sterile and follows GU/GI infection, without organisms on Gram stain. Crystals and infection can rarely coexist, so infection must still be treated.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of confirmed septic arthritis of the knee?
options: Oral antibiotics alone as an outpatient
Urgent joint drainage (arthroscopic/surgical washout or serial aspiration) PLUS empiric IV antibiotics, then tailor to cultures
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection
Colchicine and indomethacin
Immobilize the joint and give antibiotics only after cultures finalize
stages.explanation: Septic arthritis requires both urgent joint decompression/drainage (surgical/arthroscopic washout or serial aspiration) and prompt empiric IV antibiotics covering S. aureus (including MRSA, e.g., vancomycin), later narrowed by culture and sensitivities. Oral antibiotics alone as outpatient (option B) is inadequate for a closed-space joint infection. Intra-articular steroids (option D) are contraindicated in infection. Colchicine/indomethacin (option E) treat gout, not infection. Delaying antibiotics until final cultures (option F) risks rapid cartilage destruction—empiric therapy starts immediately after fluid is obtained. Drainage plus antibiotics preserves the joint.

stages.label: SOURCE & COMPLICATIONS
stages.question: Beyond treating the knee, which step is most important given the likely organism and his risk factors?
options: Start urate-lowering therapy during the acute episode
Evaluate for the source of bacteremia/seeding (e.g., skin/cellulitis, possible endocarditis) and monitor for septic spread, while controlling his diabetes
Discharge once pain improves, with no follow-up
Order a 24-hour urine uric acid
Begin chronic prophylactic antibiotics for life
stages.explanation: S. aureus septic arthritis often arises from hematogenous seeding or contiguous skin infection; identifying and treating the source (his cellulitis/abrasion), drawing blood cultures, and evaluating for endocarditis if bacteremia is documented are important, along with optimizing diabetes (a key risk factor for poor outcomes). Starting urate-lowering therapy mid-attack (option B) is not indicated and this is not gout. Discharge without follow-up (option D) is unsafe. A 24-hour urine uric acid (option E) is irrelevant here. Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics (option F) are not standard; targeted treatment of the current infection and source control are key.

cases.diagnosis: Septic (bacterial) arthritis of the knee, most likely Staphylococcus aureus
pearls: Any acute hot, swollen, painful joint with fever is septic arthritis until proven otherwise—arthrocentesis comes first (before antibiotics when feasible).
Synovial WBC >50,000/µL with neutrophil predominance and a positive Gram stain supports bacterial infection; serum uric acid is unreliable for diagnosing or excluding gout acutely.
Treatment is urgent joint drainage PLUS empiric IV antibiotics (cover S. aureus/MRSA), then narrow to culture results.
Look for the source (skin infection, bacteremia, endocarditis) and address risk factors like diabetes; crystals and infection can coexist, so treat the infection.

cases.title: Aching Hands and Morning Stiffness
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of painful, swollen hands that are stiff every morning.
cases.history: A 46-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with 4 months of progressive pain and swelling in both hands and wrists. She reports stiffness lasting about 90 minutes each morning that improves with activity. The small joints of both hands are affected symmetrically, and she has trouble opening jars and buttoning her shirt. She also reports fatigue and a low-grade subjective feeling of being unwell. There is no rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, or dry eyes. She denies recent fevers, weight loss, or preceding diarrheal or genitourinary illness. PMH is notable only for well-controlled hypothyroidism. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily and occasional ibuprofen 400 mg with partial relief. No known drug allergies. She works as an accountant, has never smoked, drinks wine socially, and is up to date on health maintenance. Family history is significant for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis and a maternal aunt with hypothyroidism. She is sexually active with her husband and uses an IUD for contraception.
cases.exam: She appears well. Hands show symmetric soft-tissue swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints bilaterally, with a positive MCP squeeze test. Both wrists are mildly swollen and tender. The distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints are spared. There are no nodules, no joint deformities, and no skin rash. Grip strength is reduced. There is no synovitis of the knees or ankles on this visit. No nail pitting, dactylitis, or enthesitis. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are unremarkable. No lymphadenopathy and no malar rash.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of her presentation most strongly suggests an inflammatory arthritis rather than osteoarthritis?
options: Involvement of the distal interphalangeal joints
Pain that worsens with use throughout the day
Morning stiffness lasting about 90 minutes that improves with activity
Bony, non-tender joint enlargement
stages.explanation: Prolonged morning stiffness (>30-60 minutes) that improves with activity is a hallmark of inflammatory arthritis such as rheumatoid arthritis, reflecting active synovitis. Her symmetric MCP/PIP synovitis with sparing of the DIP joints further supports an inflammatory process. DIP involvement (option A) and bony, non-tender enlargement (option D) are characteristic of osteoarthritis. Pain that worsens with use and is relieved by rest (option B) also points to a degenerative/mechanical process, whereas inflammatory arthritis classically improves with movement after the morning gel phenomenon. Recognizing the inflammatory pattern is the first branch point and directs serologic workup and early referral.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of laboratory tests is most appropriate and specific for confirming the suspected diagnosis?
options: ANA and anti-double-stranded DNA
Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies, plus ESR/CRP
HLA-B27 and serum uric acid
Creatine kinase and aldolase
stages.explanation: Rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-CCP antibodies are the key serologies for rheumatoid arthritis; anti-CCP is more specific (~95%) and predicts erosive disease. ESR and CRP quantify inflammation and help track activity. ANA/anti-dsDNA (option A) screen for SLE, which is possible but less consistent with isolated symmetric small-joint synovitis and no systemic/cutaneous features. HLA-B27 and uric acid (option C) target spondyloarthropathy and gout, which typically cause asymmetric or DIP/large-joint or monoarticular disease. CK and aldolase (option D) evaluate inflammatory myopathy, not arthritis. Baseline labs (CBC, LFTs, renal function) should also be obtained before starting DMARDs.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
RF: 88 IU/mL (normal <14)
Anti-CCP antibody: 120 U (strongly positive)
ESR: 48 mm/hr
CRP: 2.8 mg/dL
ANA: negative
WBC: 7.4 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin: 11.6 g/dL
Platelets: 410 ×10³/µL
Creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL
AST/ALT: normal
Hand/wrist radiographs: periarticular osteopenia and a small marginal erosion at the second MCP; joint spaces otherwise preserved

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her anti-CCP is strongly positive with an early erosion on imaging. What is the most appropriate first-line disease-modifying therapy?
options: Methotrexate, with folic acid supplementation
Long-term oral prednisone monotherapy
An anti-TNF biologic as initial monotherapy
Scheduled NSAIDs alone with reassessment in 6 months
stages.explanation: Guidelines recommend initiating a conventional synthetic DMARD as soon as RA is diagnosed; methotrexate is the anchor first-line agent and is started with folic acid to reduce toxicity. Early DMARD therapy limits joint erosion and disability. Low-dose prednisone may bridge symptoms while methotrexate takes effect but is not appropriate as long-term monotherapy (option B) given cumulative steroid toxicity. Biologics such as anti-TNF agents (option C) are generally reserved for inadequate response to methotrexate, not first-line monotherapy. NSAIDs alone (option D) control symptoms but do not modify disease, and delaying DMARDs risks irreversible joint damage in a patient already showing erosions.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.question: Before and during methotrexate therapy, which monitoring is most important to prevent serious toxicity?
options: Serial echocardiograms for cardiomyopathy
Periodic CBC and liver function tests, with baseline hepatitis serologies
Routine nerve conduction studies
Monthly bone density scans
stages.explanation: Methotrexate can cause myelosuppression and hepatotoxicity, so periodic CBC and liver function tests are essential, with baseline hepatitis B/C serologies and counseling to avoid alcohol. A baseline chest film is also reasonable given the risk of pneumonitis. Echocardiograms (option A) are not part of methotrexate monitoring. Nerve conduction studies (option C) are unnecessary; methotrexate is not neurotoxic in this way. Bone density scans (option D) matter for chronic glucocorticoid use, not for methotrexate monitoring. Patients should also receive contraception counseling because methotrexate is teratogenic and must be stopped before conception.

cases.diagnosis: Rheumatoid arthritis (seropositive, early erosive)
pearls: Symmetric MCP/PIP/wrist synovitis with morning stiffness >30-60 minutes and DIP sparing points to RA, not osteoarthritis.
Anti-CCP is more specific than RF and predicts erosive disease; obtain both with ESR/CRP.
Start a DMARD (methotrexate first-line) early to prevent irreversible joint damage; steroids only bridge.
Methotrexate is teratogenic and hepatotoxic—give folic acid, monitor CBC/LFTs, and counsel on contraception and alcohol.

cases.title: Facial Rash, Joint Pain, and Fatigue
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of fatigue, achy joints, and a rash across her cheeks that worsens in the sun.
cases.history: A 26-year-old woman presents with 3 months of fatigue, diffuse arthralgias, and a facial rash. She describes pain and morning stiffness in the small joints of her hands and her wrists and knees, without obvious swelling. She has noticed a red rash over both cheeks and the bridge of her nose that flares after time outdoors but spares the nasolabial folds. She reports painless mouth sores, mild hair thinning, and intermittent low-grade fevers. She denies chest pain, pleuritic pain, seizures, or psychosis. She has had no leg swelling or frothy urine that she has noticed. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: only an oral contraceptive pill and occasional acetaminophen. No known drug allergies. She is a graduate student, does not smoke, drinks rarely, and is sexually active with one male partner. Family history includes a maternal aunt with an unspecified autoimmune disease and a grandmother with hypothyroidism. Review of systems is otherwise notable for Raynaud-like color changes in her fingers during cold weather.
cases.exam: She appears tired but not ill. There is an erythematous, slightly raised rash over both malar eminences and the nasal bridge that spares the nasolabial folds. A few shallow, painless ulcers are present on the hard palate. Mild diffuse thinning of scalp hair is noted. Joint exam shows tenderness of the MCPs, PIPs, and wrists without frank synovitis or deformity; knees are nontender. There is no synovial swelling, no skin tightening, and no digital ulcers. Cardiac exam reveals no rub or murmur; lungs are clear. There is no peripheral edema. Neurologic exam is normal, and there is no lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given this constellation of findings, which initial screening test is most appropriate?
options: Antinuclear antibody (ANA)
Anti-CCP antibody
Serum uric acid
Skin biopsy of the malar rash
stages.explanation: A young woman with a photosensitive malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds, oral ulcers, arthralgias, and constitutional symptoms is highly suspicious for systemic lupus erythematosus. ANA is the best initial screening test—it is highly sensitive (~95-99%), so a negative result makes SLE very unlikely. Anti-CCP (option B) targets rheumatoid arthritis, which does not explain the rash or oral ulcers. Uric acid (option C) is for gout, inconsistent with this polyarticular, symmetric, non-swollen presentation. A skin biopsy (option D) can support cutaneous lupus but is not the appropriate first systemic screen; serologies are obtained first. A positive ANA then prompts specific antibody testing.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ANA: positive, titer 1:640, homogeneous pattern
Anti-dsDNA: 180 IU/mL (high)
Anti-Smith: positive
C3: 62 mg/dL (low)
C4: 10 mg/dL (low)
WBC: 3.1 ×10³/µL
Lymphocytes: 0.7 ×10³/µL (lymphopenia)
Hemoglobin: 10.4 g/dL
Platelets: 110 ×10³/µL
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL
Urinalysis: 2+ protein, RBCs present with occasional RBC casts

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which of the following autoantibodies is most specific for this diagnosis and correlates with disease activity?
options: Anti-Ro (SSA)
Anti-dsDNA
Rheumatoid factor
Anti-histone antibody
stages.explanation: Anti-dsDNA is highly specific for SLE and, along with anti-Smith, helps confirm the diagnosis; anti-dsDNA titers often correlate with disease activity, particularly lupus nephritis, and rising titers with falling complement signal flares. Anti-Smith is even more specific but does not track activity well. Anti-Ro/SSA (option A) is associated with Sjögren syndrome, subacute cutaneous lupus, and neonatal lupus but is not specific to SLE. Rheumatoid factor (option C) is nonspecific and seen in RA and other conditions. Anti-histone antibodies (option D) are characteristic of drug-induced lupus, which typically lacks renal and CNS involvement and resolves after stopping the offending drug.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Her urinalysis shows proteinuria and RBC casts. What is the most appropriate next step to evaluate possible lupus nephritis?
options: Reassure and repeat urinalysis in 6 months
Start empiric high-dose steroids without further testing
Quantify proteinuria (spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio) and refer for renal biopsy
Order renal ultrasound only
stages.explanation: Active urinary sediment with RBC casts and proteinuria strongly suggests lupus nephritis. The next steps are to quantify proteinuria with a spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio (or 24-hour collection) and to obtain a renal biopsy, which establishes the ISN/RPS class and guides immunosuppressive therapy—the biopsy class determines whether aggressive induction therapy is warranted. Reassurance and delayed recheck (option A) risks progressive, irreversible kidney damage. Empiric high-dose steroids without biopsy (option B) may be needed urgently in some cases but ideally follow tissue diagnosis to tailor therapy and avoid overtreatment. Renal ultrasound alone (option D) assesses anatomy and obstruction but cannot characterize glomerular inflammation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Regardless of nephritis, which medication should be started in essentially all SLE patients to reduce flares and organ damage?
options: Hydroxychloroquine
Chronic high-dose prednisone
Methotrexate
Allopurinol
stages.explanation: Hydroxychloroquine is foundational therapy for nearly all SLE patients: it reduces flares, lowers the risk of organ damage and thrombosis, improves survival, and helps control skin and joint disease. Baseline and periodic ophthalmologic exams are needed because of dose-dependent retinal toxicity. Chronic high-dose prednisone (option B) controls acute activity but causes substantial cumulative toxicity and is not a maintenance strategy. Methotrexate (option C) can help joint and skin disease but is not universally indicated and is teratogenic and contraindicated in significant renal disease. Allopurinol (option D) treats gout and has no role in lupus; in fact it can trigger hypersensitivity. Hydroxychloroquine is the answer that applies to virtually every patient.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Beyond medication, which counseling point is most important for this patient's long-term outcome?
options: Strict sun protection and avoidance of UV exposure
High-protein, high-purine diet
Daily aspirin for all patients regardless of antibody status
Discontinuing all contraception immediately
stages.explanation: Ultraviolet light triggers cutaneous and systemic lupus flares, so rigorous photoprotection (broad-spectrum sunscreen, protective clothing, sun avoidance) is a key, evidence-based behavioral measure. A high-purine diet (option B) is irrelevant to lupus and would worsen gout if present. Daily aspirin for everyone (option C) is not indicated; antiplatelet/anticoagulation decisions depend on antiphospholipid antibody status and thrombotic risk. Abruptly discontinuing all contraception (option D) is wrong—pregnancy should be planned during quiescent disease, estrogen-containing contraceptives may be avoided if antiphospholipid antibodies are present, and effective contraception is important while on teratogenic drugs. Smoking cessation and vaccination are also valuable counseling points.

cases.diagnosis: Systemic lupus erythematosus with probable lupus nephritis
pearls: ANA is the best screening test for SLE (very sensitive); anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith are specific, and anti-dsDNA with low complement tracks activity/nephritis.
A malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds plus photosensitivity, oral ulcers, arthralgias, and cytopenias is classic SLE.
Active urinary sediment (proteinuria, RBC casts) warrants protein quantification and renal biopsy to classify and treat lupus nephritis.
Hydroxychloroquine benefits nearly all SLE patients; counsel on photoprotection and screen for retinal toxicity.

cases.title: New Headache With Jaw Pain in an Older Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of a new right-sided headache with jaw pain when chewing and brief vision blurring.
cases.history: A 75-year-old woman presents with 2 weeks of a new, persistent right temporal headache unlike any she has had before. She reports aching in her jaw that begins after a few minutes of chewing and forces her to stop, as well as scalp tenderness when brushing her hair. This morning she had a transient episode of blurred and partially dimmed vision in the right eye lasting a few minutes, which frightened her. She also describes new shoulder and hip girdle stiffness, low-grade fevers, fatigue, and a 3 kg unintentional weight loss over a month. She denies focal weakness, slurred speech, or true loss of consciousness. PMH includes hypertension and osteoporosis. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, alendronate weekly, calcium/vitamin D, and acetaminophen as needed. No known drug allergies. She is a retired teacher, never smoked, lives alone, and is independent. Family history is noncontributory. She has no prior history of migraine or chronic headaches and is generally healthy for her age.
cases.exam: She is alert and uncomfortable. The right temporal artery is tender, thickened, and nodular with diminished pulsation; the scalp is tender to palpation. Visual acuity is mildly reduced in the right eye compared with the left; pupils are equal and reactive, and a subtle right relative afferent pupillary defect is suspected. Funduscopy is technically limited but shows no obvious pallor at this time. There is tenderness and reduced active range of motion at both shoulders and hips without true weakness. Cranial nerves are otherwise intact, and there is no facial droop or limb drift. Neck is supple. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable, and peripheral pulses are intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given her new temporal headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, and transient visual loss, what is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Order an outpatient temporal artery biopsy and have her return for results before treatment
Start high-dose corticosteroids immediately, without waiting for biopsy
Prescribe sumatriptan and arrange routine neurology follow-up
Obtain a CT head and defer treatment pending the result
stages.explanation: This is classic giant cell arteritis (GCA) with impending visual loss—a medical emergency. Because vision loss from arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy is sudden and irreversible, high-dose corticosteroids must be started immediately on clinical suspicion, before biopsy. The biopsy (option A) should be obtained soon, but treatment is never delayed for it; steroids do not negate biopsy findings for at least 1-2 weeks. Triptans (option C) treat migraine and are dangerously inappropriate here, both delaying treatment and causing vasoconstriction. CT head (option D) does not diagnose GCA and would delay sight-saving therapy. Prompt steroids can prevent blindness in the affected and contralateral eye.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR: 96 mm/hr
CRP: 8.4 mg/dL
Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (normocytic)
Platelets: 480 ×10³/µL
WBC: 9.0 ×10³/µL
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL
AST/ALT: mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase
CT head (non-contrast): no hemorrhage or mass

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test is the gold standard to confirm the diagnosis, and how does starting steroids affect it?
options: MRI brain; steroids make it falsely negative within hours
Temporal artery biopsy; it should be done within about 1-2 weeks of starting steroids before yield falls
ESR alone; a normal ESR excludes the disease
Carotid Doppler; steroids have no effect on results
stages.explanation: Temporal artery biopsy is the diagnostic gold standard, revealing granulomatous inflammation with giant cells and often skip lesions (favoring an adequate-length specimen). Biopsy should ideally be performed within about 1-2 weeks of starting corticosteroids, as histologic changes persist for a period before yield declines—so treatment is started first and biopsy follows promptly. MRI brain (option A) is not the confirmatory test for GCA. ESR (option C) is supportive and usually markedly elevated, but a normal ESR does not exclude GCA, so it cannot rule the disease out. Carotid Doppler (option D) is not used to diagnose temporal arteritis. Vascular ultrasound of the temporal artery (halo sign) is an adjunct in some centers.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Her right visual symptoms recur and she now has frank visual loss in that eye. What is the most appropriate escalation?
options: Switch to oral NSAIDs and observe
Administer high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (pulse therapy)
Stop steroids to avoid masking the diagnosis
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation with heparin
stages.explanation: Acute or progressive visual loss in GCA warrants escalation to high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (pulse therapy, typically over 3 days) to maximize the chance of preserving vision and protecting the contralateral eye, followed by high-dose oral steroids. NSAIDs (option A) are inadequate for this vasculitis and will not prevent blindness. Stopping steroids (option C) is dangerous and would accelerate visual loss; treatment must continue. Anticoagulation (option D) does not treat the underlying granulomatous arteritis and is not indicated. Adjunctive low-dose aspirin is sometimes used, but the core therapy is aggressive corticosteroids. Tocilizumab is a steroid-sparing option for relapsing or refractory disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She also has shoulder and hip girdle stiffness. Which associated condition is this, and what does it imply for management?
options: Rheumatoid arthritis, requiring methotrexate first-line
Polymyalgia rheumatica, which coexists with GCA and responds to corticosteroids
Fibromyalgia, requiring duloxetine
Osteoarthritis, requiring only physical therapy
stages.explanation: Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) frequently coexists with GCA—up to roughly half of GCA patients have PMR symptoms—and presents with bilateral shoulder and hip girdle aching and stiffness with elevated inflammatory markers. Both conditions respond to corticosteroids, though GCA requires much higher doses than PMR. Recognizing the overlap reinforces the diagnosis and the need for prolonged, carefully tapered steroids. Rheumatoid arthritis (option A) causes peripheral synovitis, not girdle stiffness, and is not treated with steroids first-line. Fibromyalgia (option C) does not raise ESR/CRP. Osteoarthritis (option D) is degenerative, lacks systemic inflammation, and would not explain her markedly elevated ESR or systemic symptoms.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: As she begins prolonged corticosteroid therapy, which preventive measure is most important?
options: Begin calcium, vitamin D, and bone-protective therapy (e.g., a bisphosphonate) for steroid-induced osteoporosis
Start a low-sodium diet as the sole intervention
Avoid all vaccinations indefinitely
Prescribe routine prophylactic antibiotics for the steroid course
stages.explanation: Prolonged high-dose corticosteroids cause bone loss, so steroid-induced osteoporosis prophylaxis—adequate calcium and vitamin D plus bone-protective therapy such as a bisphosphonate—is essential, especially in an older woman already on alendronate for osteoporosis. Glucose monitoring, blood pressure control, and PCP prophylaxis in select high-dose cases are also relevant. A low-sodium diet alone (option B) addresses only fluid retention and ignores the major bone risk. Avoiding all vaccinations (option C) is wrong; appropriate non-live vaccines are encouraged in immunosuppressed patients. Routine prophylactic antibiotics for everyone (option D) are not standard. Steroids are tapered slowly over many months while monitoring for relapse.

cases.diagnosis: Giant cell (temporal) arteritis with impending/early visual loss and associated polymyalgia rheumatica
pearls: New temporal headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, and visual symptoms in a patient over 50 = GCA until proven otherwise; start high-dose steroids immediately.
Never delay corticosteroids for biopsy—visual loss is sudden and irreversible; biopsy still has yield within ~1-2 weeks of treatment.
Markedly elevated ESR/CRP supports GCA, but a normal ESR does not exclude it; acute visual loss warrants IV pulse steroids.
GCA and polymyalgia rheumatica overlap; provide steroid-induced osteoporosis prophylaxis and a slow taper with relapse monitoring.

cases.title: Stiff Shoulders and Hips in the Morning
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of aching and stiffness in both shoulders and hips, worst in the morning.
cases.history: A 71-year-old woman presents with 6 weeks of aching and stiffness across both shoulders, the upper arms, the neck, and the hip girdles. The stiffness is dramatic in the morning, lasting well over an hour, and she has difficulty rising from a chair, lifting her arms to comb her hair, and turning over in bed. Symptoms came on relatively abruptly. She reports mild fatigue and a low-grade feeling of being unwell but denies true muscle weakness when she can finally move. Importantly, she denies headache, jaw pain when chewing, scalp tenderness, and visual changes. There is no rash, no swollen peripheral joints, and no recent illness. PMH includes hypothyroidism and hyperlipidemia. Medications: levothyroxine 100 mcg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg nightly; she stopped the statin a week ago wondering if it caused the pain, with no improvement. No known drug allergies. She is a retired librarian, never smoked, and drinks occasionally. Family history is unremarkable. She is otherwise functioning independently at home.
cases.exam: She appears well but moves stiffly. There is tenderness and markedly reduced active range of motion at both shoulders and hips due to pain, but passive range is better preserved. Muscle strength is intact when formally tested (pain limits effort), and there is no true proximal weakness. There is no muscle atrophy and no tenderness over the temporal arteries; temporal pulses are normal. Peripheral joints show no synovitis or swelling. There is no rash, no lymphadenopathy, and no thyromegaly. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are normal. Neurologic exam, including reflexes and sensation, is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial laboratory test best supports the suspected inflammatory cause of her girdle stiffness?
options: Creatine kinase (CK)
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and CRP
TSH
Serum calcium
stages.explanation: Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) is suggested by bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain and prolonged morning stiffness in a patient over 50, and it characteristically produces a markedly elevated ESR and CRP. These inflammatory markers are the most useful initial tests to support the diagnosis and to monitor response. CK (option A) is typically normal in PMR—elevation would instead suggest an inflammatory myopathy like polymyositis, which causes true weakness rather than stiffness. TSH (option C) is reasonable to exclude hypothyroid myalgia but is less specific to the inflammatory picture; her thyroid disease is treated. Calcium (option D) does not explain this presentation. Importantly, PMR also shows true weakness absent on exam, only pain-limited motion.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR: 78 mm/hr
CRP: 4.6 mg/dL
CK: 90 U/L (normal)
TSH: 2.1 µIU/mL (normal)
Hemoglobin: 11.2 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia)
WBC: 8.1 ×10³/µL
Creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Anti-CCP: negative
Calcium: 9.4 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A normal CK with markedly elevated ESR/CRP and pain-limited (not weak) proximal motion best fits which diagnosis?
options: Polymyositis
Polymyalgia rheumatica
Statin-induced myopathy
Fibromyalgia
stages.explanation: Polymyalgia rheumatica is the best fit: bilateral girdle stiffness, age >50, dramatically elevated inflammatory markers, and a normal CK with no true weakness (pain limits motion, but strength is preserved). Polymyositis (option A) causes objective proximal muscle weakness with an elevated CK, neither of which she has. Statin-induced myopathy (option C) can cause myalgias and elevated CK, but her CK is normal and stopping the statin produced no improvement, making it unlikely. Fibromyalgia (option D) produces diffuse pain and tender points but does not raise ESR/CRP and is not typically of abrupt onset with morning girdle stiffness in this age group. The clinical pattern plus a normal CK and high ESR clinches PMR.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial treatment for polymyalgia rheumatica?
options: High-dose corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone 40-60 mg daily) as for giant cell arteritis
Low-dose corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone 15 mg daily)
Methotrexate as first-line monotherapy
High-dose NSAIDs alone
stages.explanation: PMR is treated with low-dose corticosteroids, typically prednisone around 15 mg daily, which usually produces a dramatic, near-complete symptomatic response within days—a hallmark of the disease. This contrasts sharply with GCA, which requires high-dose steroids (option A) to prevent blindness; this patient has no cranial/visual symptoms. Methotrexate (option C) is reserved as a steroid-sparing agent for relapsing or steroid-dependent disease, not first-line. NSAIDs alone (option D) provide inadequate control of PMR. A rapid, robust response to low-dose prednisone strongly supports the diagnosis, whereas a poor response should prompt reconsideration. Steroids are then tapered slowly over many months.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two months into a steroid taper, she develops a new temporal headache and brief visual blurring. What is the most important immediate action?
options: Reassure her this is expected during a steroid taper
Promptly increase to high-dose corticosteroids and evaluate for giant cell arteritis
Order an MRI of the lumbar spine
Stop steroids entirely and observe
stages.explanation: PMR can coexist with or progress to giant cell arteritis. New temporal headache and visual symptoms raise immediate concern for GCA, which threatens vision. The correct action is to promptly escalate to high-dose corticosteroids and evaluate for GCA (clinical assessment, ESR/CRP, temporal artery biopsy or ultrasound)—do not wait. Reassurance (option A) is dangerous and risks irreversible blindness. Lumbar spine MRI (option C) is irrelevant to cranial symptoms. Stopping steroids (option D) would worsen any underlying arteritis and precipitate visual loss. Educating PMR patients to report cranial or visual symptoms urgently is a key part of management because of this important overlap.

cases.diagnosis: Polymyalgia rheumatica
pearls: PMR: age >50 with bilateral shoulder/hip girdle stiffness, morning stiffness >45-60 min, and high ESR/CRP with a NORMAL CK and no true weakness.
A dramatic response to low-dose prednisone (~15 mg/day) within days is characteristic and supports the diagnosis.
Elevated CK or true proximal weakness points to inflammatory myopathy, not PMR.
Always counsel PMR patients to report new headache, jaw claudication, or visual change—GCA can coexist and threatens vision.

cases.title: Recurrent Throbbing Headaches With Visual Sparkles
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent severe one-sided throbbing headaches preceded by flashing zigzag lights.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman presents to clinic with recurrent headaches occurring about 3-4 times per month for the past 2 years. Each headache is unilateral, throbbing, and moderate-to-severe, lasting roughly a day if untreated. Before some attacks she sees shimmering zigzag lines and bright spots in her vision that spread over about 20 minutes and then resolve before the headache begins. The headaches are worsened by light, sound, and movement, and she often feels nauseated and seeks a dark, quiet room. Attacks are sometimes triggered by poor sleep, stress, menses, and red wine. She denies fever, neck stiffness, weakness, numbness, difficulty speaking, or headaches that wake her from sleep. There are no positional or exertional features and no progressive worsening. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: combined oral contraceptive pill and occasional ibuprofen with partial relief. No known drug allergies. She is a teacher, does not smoke, drinks socially, and exercises regularly. Family history is notable for migraines in her mother and sister.
cases.exam: She is comfortable and in no distress at the time of the visit. Neurologic exam is completely normal: cranial nerves are intact, funduscopy shows sharp disc margins without papilledema, visual fields are full, and extraocular movements are normal. Strength, sensation, coordination, and gait are normal, and reflexes are symmetric. There is no neck stiffness and no temporal artery tenderness. There is no scalp or sinus tenderness. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable. Skin shows no rash. The remainder of the general examination is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the history and a normal exam, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Obtain MRI brain with contrast before any treatment
Diagnose migraine clinically and counsel on management; no neuroimaging needed
Perform a lumbar puncture to exclude meningitis
Refer urgently for temporal artery biopsy
stages.explanation: Her recurrent, unilateral, throbbing headaches with photophobia, phonophobia, nausea, a visual aura that spreads and resolves before the headache, and a normal neurologic exam meet clinical criteria for migraine with aura.
