Migraine is a clinical diagnosis, and with no red flags, neuroimaging is not required. Routine MRI (option A) is unnecessary and low-yield in classic migraine with a normal exam. Lumbar puncture (option C) is indicated for suspected meningitis or thunderclap headache, not episodic migraine. Temporal artery biopsy (option D) is for suspected giant cell arteritis in older patients with cranial symptoms, not a 30-year-old with classic migraine. Imaging is reserved for red-flag features.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which feature, if present, would be a 'red flag' prompting neuroimaging?
options: Photophobia and phonophobia during attacks
A family history of migraine
A sudden 'thunderclap' headache reaching maximal intensity within seconds to minutes
Nausea relieved by lying in a dark room
stages.explanation: A thunderclap headache—maximal intensity within seconds to a minute—is a major red flag for subarachnoid hemorrhage and other serious causes, mandating urgent imaging (and possibly lumbar puncture). Other red flags include focal deficits, headache worsened by Valsalva or position, onset after age 50, systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss), immunosuppression or cancer, and a clearly different or progressive 'worst headache of life.' Photophobia and phonophobia (option A) are typical migraine features, not red flags. Family history of migraine (option B) actually supports the diagnosis. Nausea relieved by a dark, quiet room (option D) is characteristic migraine behavior. Recognizing red flags distinguishes benign primary headache from dangerous secondary causes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For acute, moderate-to-severe migraine attacks, which abortive therapy is most appropriate first-line?
options: A triptan (e.g., sumatriptan), with or without an NSAID
Daily opioid analgesics
Prophylactic propranolol taken only during an attack
Butalbital-containing combination analgesics as routine therapy
stages.explanation: Triptans (serotonin 5-HT1B/1D agonists) such as sumatriptan are first-line abortive therapy for moderate-to-severe migraine, often combined with an NSAID for added efficacy; they work best taken early in the attack. Opioids (option B) should be avoided—they are ineffective for migraine pathophysiology and risk dependence and medication-overuse headache. Propranolol (option C) is a preventive (prophylactic) agent taken daily, not an abortive used during an attack. Butalbital combinations (option D) carry high risk of medication-overuse headache and dependence and are not appropriate routine therapy. Triptans are contraindicated in uncontrolled hypertension, coronary or cerebrovascular disease, and certain aura subtypes; this young, healthy patient is a good candidate.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Because she has migraine WITH aura and smokes occasionally, which counseling point about her contraception is most important?
options: Estrogen-containing contraceptives are encouraged because they reduce migraine frequency
Combined (estrogen-containing) contraceptives raise ischemic stroke risk in migraine with aura and should generally be avoided
She should switch to a higher-estrogen pill
Contraceptive choice has no bearing on migraine with aura
stages.explanation: Migraine with aura is itself associated with increased ischemic stroke risk, and estrogen-containing (combined) contraceptives further raise that risk—markedly so with concurrent smoking. Therefore combined hormonal contraceptives are generally contraindicated or avoided in women who have migraine with aura, and a non-estrogen option (e.g., progestin-only methods or an IUD) is preferred, along with smoking cessation counseling. Estrogen does not reliably reduce migraine frequency (option A), and a higher-estrogen pill (option C) would increase risk. Saying contraception is irrelevant (option D) ignores a clinically important safety interaction. This counseling can meaningfully reduce her stroke risk and is a high-yield exam point.

cases.diagnosis: Migraine with aura
pearls: Migraine is a clinical diagnosis: recurrent unilateral throbbing headache with photophobia, phonophobia, nausea, and (in aura) gradual, reversible visual symptoms; a normal exam needs no imaging.
Thunderclap onset, focal deficits, positional/exertional worsening, age >50 onset, fever, or 'worst-ever, different' headache are red flags requiring workup.
Triptans ± NSAIDs are first-line abortive therapy; avoid opioids and butalbital to prevent medication-overuse headache.
Migraine with aura plus estrogen-containing contraception (especially with smoking) raises ischemic stroke risk—prefer progestin-only methods and advise smoking cessation.

cases.title: Collapse With Shaking at Work
cases.chiefComplaint: Witnessed loss of consciousness with whole-body shaking and confusion afterward.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a witnessed episode at work. Coworkers report he suddenly cried out, stiffened, fell, and then had rhythmic jerking of all four limbs lasting about 90 seconds. He bit the side of his tongue and was incontinent of urine. Afterward he was confused and sleepy for about 20 minutes before gradually returning to baseline. He has no memory of the event. He has had no prior seizures. Over the past month he reports occasional morning headaches and feeling 'off,' but no focal weakness or numbness. He denies recent fever, head trauma, or illicit drug use, and he has not been ill. PMH includes hypertension and a 30-pack-year smoking history. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily. He drinks 2-3 beers most nights and admits he has been cutting back recently. No known drug allergies. He works in construction and drives daily. Family history is noncontributory. He has lost about 4 kg without trying over 2 months.
cases.exam: He is now alert and oriented, tired-appearing. There is a lateral tongue laceration. Neurologic exam shows no focal weakness, normal sensation, symmetric reflexes, and a normal gait when tested. Cranial nerves are intact, and there is no neck stiffness or photophobia. There is no papilledema on funduscopy. No signs of head trauma other than the tongue bite. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal with regular rhythm and no murmurs. Lungs are clear. Skin shows no rash or stigmata of chronic liver disease. He is not tremulous and is not diaphoretic; there are no other findings of acute alcohol withdrawal at this moment.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which features most strongly distinguish a generalized tonic-clonic seizure from a syncopal episode?
options: Brief lightheadedness with rapid full recovery
Lateral tongue biting, urinary incontinence, and prolonged postictal confusion
Pallor and diaphoresis just before collapse
Immediate return to baseline with no confusion
stages.explanation: Lateral tongue biting, urinary incontinence, and a prolonged postictal state of confusion/drowsiness strongly favor a generalized tonic-clonic seizure over syncope. Syncope typically features brief prodromal lightheadedness, pallor and diaphoresis (option C), rapid and complete recovery without significant confusion (options A and 3), and at most a few brief myoclonic jerks. The combination of an aura/cry, tonic stiffening then clonic jerking lasting more than a minute, lateral (not tip) tongue trauma, incontinence, and a postictal period is classic for seizure. Distinguishing these guides the workup: seizures prompt evaluation for provoking causes and structural lesions, whereas syncope prompts a cardiac and orthostatic evaluation.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After stabilization, which initial workup is most appropriate for a first unprovoked-appearing seizure?
options: Discharge with no testing since he is back to baseline
Fingerstick glucose, electrolytes/calcium/magnesium, toxicology, neuroimaging (CT then MRI), and EEG
Lumbar puncture for all first seizures regardless of presentation
Carotid ultrasound and echocardiogram only
stages.explanation: A first seizure requires a search for provoking/metabolic causes and structural lesions. Initial testing includes a fingerstick glucose and electrolytes (sodium, calcium, magnesium, glucose), toxicology screen, and assessment for alcohol withdrawal, plus neuroimaging—non-contrast CT acutely, with MRI brain for better structural detail—and an EEG to assess epileptiform activity and recurrence risk. Discharging without testing (option A) misses dangerous causes. Routine lumbar puncture for all first seizures (option C) is not indicated; LP is reserved for suspected CNS infection or subarachnoid hemorrhage (fever, meningismus, immunocompromise). Carotid ultrasound and echo alone (option D) target syncope/embolic stroke, not the seizure workup. His weight loss, smoking, and morning headaches additionally raise concern for an intracranial mass.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Fingerstick glucose: 104 mg/dL
Sodium: 139 mEq/L
Calcium: 9.2 mg/dL
Magnesium: 2.0 mg/dL
Toxicology screen: negative
Blood alcohol level: negative
CBC: within normal limits
Non-contrast CT head: no acute hemorrhage
MRI brain with contrast: 2.5 cm enhancing mass in the left frontal lobe with surrounding vasogenic edema, concerning for neoplasm

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given normal metabolic studies, negative tox/alcohol, and an enhancing brain mass, how is this seizure best classified?
options: Provoked seizure due to alcohol withdrawal
Provoked seizure due to hypoglycemia
Unprovoked seizure with a likely structural (symptomatic) cause
Psychogenic nonepileptic event
stages.explanation: His metabolic panel, glucose, toxicology, and alcohol level are normal, excluding common acute provoking factors, while MRI reveals a structural lesion. This is best classified as an unprovoked seizure with an identified structural cause (a remote/structural symptomatic seizure), which carries a high risk of recurrence and generally meets criteria to be treated as epilepsy. Alcohol withdrawal (option A) is excluded by a negative alcohol level and absent withdrawal signs, though it remains a counseling point. Hypoglycemia (option B) is excluded by a normal glucose. A psychogenic nonepileptic event (option D) is inconsistent with the stereotyped tonic-clonic semiology, tongue biting, incontinence, postictal confusion, and a causative lesion. The structural lesion drives management and recurrence risk.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which counseling point regarding driving is most appropriate before he leaves the ED?
options: He may drive immediately because he is back to baseline
He must not drive and should be advised of state-specific seizure driving restrictions and reporting requirements
He can drive as long as someone rides with him
Driving restrictions apply only after a second seizure
stages.explanation: After a seizure with loss of consciousness, patients must be counseled not to drive and informed of state-specific seizure-related driving restrictions and physician reporting requirements; most jurisdictions require a seizure-free interval before driving resumes. This is a critical safety and medicolegal issue. Allowing immediate driving (option A) endangers the patient and public. Having a passenger present (option C) does not mitigate the risk of a recurrent seizure at the wheel. Waiting for a second seizure (option D) is incorrect—restrictions apply after the first seizure with impaired consciousness. He should also be started on an appropriate antiseizure medication given the structural lesion and high recurrence risk, and referred to neurology/neurosurgery for the mass.

cases.diagnosis: New-onset (first) generalized tonic-clonic seizure due to a structural brain lesion (unprovoked, symptomatic)
pearls: Lateral tongue biting, incontinence, and prolonged postictal confusion favor seizure; brief recovery with pallor/diaphoresis favors syncope.
First-seizure workup: glucose, electrolytes (Na, Ca, Mg), toxicology/alcohol, neuroimaging (CT then MRI), and EEG; LP only if infection/SAH suspected.
A seizure with an identified structural lesion is unprovoked/symptomatic, carries high recurrence risk, and generally warrants antiseizure medication.
Always counsel against driving after a seizure with impaired consciousness and review state-specific restrictions and reporting laws.

cases.title: The Worst Headache of Her Life
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden, severe headache that peaked instantly while she was at her desk.
cases.history: A 48-year-old woman presents to the emergency department after the abrupt onset of an excruciating headache that reached maximal intensity within seconds while she was sitting at her desk. She describes it as the worst headache of her life, diffuse but worst at the back of her head, accompanied by nausea, several episodes of vomiting, and neck discomfort. She had a brief loss of consciousness at onset, then recovered. She denies fever, recent trauma, or prior similar headaches, and she is not a chronic headache sufferer. Over the past week she recalls one milder, sudden 'thunderclap' headache that resolved spontaneously, which she dismissed. PMH includes hypertension and tobacco use (20-pack-year). Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily; she takes no anticoagulants. No known drug allergies. She drinks alcohol socially and uses no illicit drugs. Family history is notable for a sister with a 'brain aneurysm' and polycystic kidney disease. She is photophobic and wants the lights off, and she appears frightened by the severity of the pain.
cases.exam: She is in obvious distress, holding her head, photophobic. She is alert and oriented. There is nuchal rigidity with discomfort on neck flexion. Funduscopy shows no papilledema but a small subhyaloid (preretinal) hemorrhage in the right eye. Cranial nerves are intact, and pupils are equal and reactive. There is no focal motor weakness, sensory deficit, or pronator drift, and reflexes are symmetric. Gait is not tested due to pain and risk. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal aside from hypertension. There is no rash. The remainder of the general exam is unremarkable, and she is moving all extremities.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For this thunderclap headache, what is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic test?
options: MRI brain without contrast
Non-contrast CT of the head
Lumbar puncture before any imaging
Catheter cerebral angiography first
stages.explanation: A thunderclap headache mandates urgent evaluation for subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH). The first test is a non-contrast head CT, which is highly sensitive for SAH when performed early (within about 6 hours of onset). MRI (option A) is less practical and not the standard first study for acute SAH in most EDs. Lumbar puncture before imaging (option C) is unsafe and unnecessary as the initial step—LP follows a negative CT to look for xanthochromia. Catheter angiography (option D) is reserved to localize an aneurysm after SAH is confirmed, not as the first diagnostic test. The subhyaloid hemorrhage and sudden maximal headache strongly suggest SAH, making rapid CT essential.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: no definite hemorrhage identified (study performed ~10 hours after symptom onset)
Sodium: 138 mEq/L
Platelets: 240 ×10³/µL
INR: 1.0
Glucose: 110 mg/dL
Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The CT is negative but was done ~10 hours after onset, and suspicion remains high. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Discharge with outpatient migraine follow-up
Perform a lumbar puncture and evaluate the CSF for xanthochromia
Repeat the non-contrast CT in 24 hours
Begin empiric IV antibiotics for meningitis
stages.explanation: CT sensitivity for SAH declines after the first several hours, so a negative CT at ~10 hours does not exclude SAH when clinical suspicion is high. The next step is a lumbar puncture to evaluate the CSF for red blood cells and xanthochromia (yellow discoloration from hemoglobin breakdown), which is detectable several hours after bleeding and persists for days—helping distinguish true SAH from a traumatic tap. Discharging her as migraine (option A) is dangerous given the classic history and high pretest probability. Repeating CT in 24 hours (option C) delays diagnosis and is less sensitive than LP for this question. Empiric antibiotics (option D) target meningitis, which is not the leading concern here. CT angiography is an alternative pathway in some protocols.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Lumbar puncture: opening pressure elevated
CSF tube 1 RBC: 18,000 /µL
CSF tube 4 RBC: 17,200 /µL (no significant clearing)
CSF appearance after centrifugation: xanthochromic (yellow)
CSF WBC: mildly elevated, proportional to RBCs
CT angiography of the head: 7 mm saccular aneurysm at the anterior communicating artery

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The CSF confirms SAH and CTA reveals an anterior communicating artery aneurysm. What is the definitive management priority?
options: Observation with serial CTs and no intervention
Early securing of the aneurysm by endovascular coiling or surgical clipping
Therapeutic anticoagulation to prevent stroke
Immediate lumbar drain placement as definitive treatment
stages.explanation: Once aneurysmal SAH is confirmed, the priority is to secure the ruptured aneurysm early—by endovascular coiling or microsurgical clipping—to prevent catastrophic rebleeding, which carries very high mortality and peaks in the first days. Observation alone (option A) leaves the patient at high rebleeding risk. Therapeutic anticoagulation (option C) is contraindicated and would worsen bleeding. A lumbar drain (option D) may help manage hydrocephalus or CSF dynamics but is not definitive treatment of the aneurysm. Supportive care includes blood pressure control to reduce rebleeding before the aneurysm is secured, close neuro monitoring in an ICU, and management of complications such as hydrocephalus, seizures, and delayed cerebral ischemia from vasospasm.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Several days after the aneurysm is secured, which medication is given to reduce neurologic injury from a common delayed complication?
options: Nimodipine to reduce poor outcomes from cerebral vasospasm/delayed ischemia
Mannitol as routine prophylaxis for all patients
Tissue plasminogen activator (tPA)
High-dose corticosteroids to prevent vasospasm
stages.explanation: Delayed cerebral ischemia from vasospasm typically peaks around days 4-14 after aneurysmal SAH and is a leading cause of morbidity. Oral nimodipine, a calcium channel blocker, improves neurologic outcomes and is given to essentially all aneurysmal SAH patients (its benefit is on outcomes rather than reliably reversing angiographic spasm). Mannitol (option B) is used for acute intracranial hypertension, not routine vasospasm prophylaxis. tPA (option C) is thrombolytic and contraindicated after hemorrhage. Corticosteroids (option D) have not been shown to prevent vasospasm and are not standard. Management also includes maintaining euvolemia, monitoring (often with transcranial Dopplers), and escalation to induced hypertension or endovascular therapy if symptomatic vasospasm develops.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She becomes progressively drowsy on day 2 with enlarging ventricles on repeat CT. Which acute complication is most likely, and what is the appropriate intervention?
options: Acute hydrocephalus, treated with CSF diversion (external ventricular drain)
Medication overdose, treated with naloxone
Hypoglycemia, treated with IV dextrose
Migraine recurrence, treated with a triptan
stages.explanation: Subarachnoid blood can obstruct CSF flow and arachnoid granulation reabsorption, causing acute hydrocephalus—a common early complication of SAH that presents with declining consciousness and enlarging ventricles on CT. The treatment is CSF diversion, typically an external ventricular drain, which is both therapeutic and allows ICP monitoring. Naloxone (option B) would be appropriate for opioid toxicity, which does not explain ventricular enlargement. Hypoglycemia (option C) is excluded by her normal glucose and would not enlarge the ventricles. A triptan for migraine (option D) is wholly inappropriate in confirmed SAH and is contraindicated. Recognizing hydrocephalus is essential because it is rapidly reversible with timely drainage, whereas delayed cerebral ischemia from vasospasm peaks later (days 4-14).

cases.diagnosis: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (anterior communicating artery aneurysm)
pearls: Thunderclap headache ('worst of life,' maximal in seconds) requires immediate non-contrast head CT; CT is most sensitive within ~6 hours.
A negative CT after several hours with persistent suspicion warrants LP for xanthochromia; nonclearing RBCs across tubes argue against a traumatic tap.
Confirmed aneurysmal SAH needs early coiling or clipping to prevent rebleeding, plus BP control and ICU neuromonitoring.
Nimodipine improves outcomes from delayed cerebral ischemia/vasospasm (peaks ~days 4-14); avoid anticoagulation and watch for hydrocephalus.

cases.title: Tearing Chest Pain Radiating to the Back
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden, severe tearing chest pain radiating between the shoulder blades.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents to the emergency department with the sudden onset of severe, sharp, tearing chest pain that began abruptly an hour ago and radiated to his back between the shoulder blades. The pain was maximal at onset rather than building gradually. He is diaphoretic and anxious. He denies fever, cough, or recent illness. He has poorly controlled hypertension and has not taken his medications consistently. PMH includes hypertension and tobacco use (40-pack-year). Medications: amlodipine and losartan, taken irregularly. No known drug allergies. He drinks alcohol occasionally and denies cocaine or other stimulant use. Family history is notable for a brother with an aortic aneurysm and a father who died suddenly in his 50s. He works as a long-haul truck driver. He reports no prior episodes of chest pain and denies leg swelling or recent immobilization. He took no aspirin before arrival. He has no history of connective tissue disease but is tall with a long arm span.
cases.exam: He is diaphoretic and in distress. There is a notable blood pressure differential between the right and left arms. A soft early diastolic decrescendo murmur is heard at the left sternal border, suggesting aortic regurgitation. Peripheral pulses are asymmetric, with a diminished left radial pulse. Heart sounds are otherwise regular without an S3 or rub. Lungs are clear with no crackles. There is no jugular venous distention and no pericardial rub. Abdomen is soft and nontender without a pulsatile mass. Neurologic exam is intact with no focal deficit. There is no leg edema, and calves are nontender.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of findings most strongly suggests acute aortic dissection over acute coronary syndrome?
options: Substernal pressure radiating to the left arm relieved by nitroglycerin
Tearing pain maximal at onset, an interarm BP differential, and a new aortic regurgitation murmur
Pleuritic pain worse with inspiration and a pericardial rub
Exertional chest tightness with diaphoresis resolving at rest
stages.explanation: Abrupt, tearing/ripping pain that is maximal at onset and radiates to the back, combined with an interarm blood pressure differential and a new aortic regurgitation murmur (from proximal extension into the aortic root), is highly suggestive of acute aortic dissection. Pulse deficits and pain migration further support it. Substernal pressure radiating to the arm and relieved by nitroglycerin (option A) and exertional tightness resolving with rest (option D) describe typical anginal/ACS patterns. Pleuritic pain with a pericardial rub (option C) suggests pericarditis. Recognizing the dissection pattern is crucial because management diverges sharply from ACS—notably, reflexive anticoagulation/antiplatelet/thrombolytic therapy can be catastrophic in dissection.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: In a hemodynamically stable patient with suspected dissection, what is the most appropriate confirmatory imaging?
options: CT angiography of the chest/abdomen (with the aorta)
Coronary angiography first
Ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scan
Abdominal ultrasound only
stages.explanation: CT angiography of the chest and abdomen is the test of choice in a stable patient with suspected aortic dissection: it is fast, widely available, and accurately delineates the intimal flap, the extent of the false lumen, and branch-vessel involvement, classifying the dissection (Stanford A vs B). Transesophageal echocardiography is an alternative for unstable patients or when CT is not feasible. Coronary angiography first (option B) is wrong and dangerous—it delays diagnosis and is not the priority; dissection can secondarily involve the coronary ostia. A V/Q scan (option C) evaluates pulmonary embolism, not the aorta. Abdominal ultrasound alone (option D) cannot assess the thoracic aorta or the dissection's full extent. A widened mediastinum on chest X-ray can be a clue beforehand.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Chest X-ray: widened mediastinum
CT angiography: intimal flap originating in the ascending aorta and extending into the descending aorta (Stanford type A)
Troponin: mildly elevated
Creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL
Hemoglobin: 13.8 g/dL
ECG: sinus tachycardia, left ventricular hypertrophy, no ST-elevation

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Before/while arranging definitive care, what is the cornerstone of initial medical therapy ('anti-impulse' therapy)?
options: Intravenous beta-blockade (e.g., esmolol/labetalol) to lower heart rate and blood pressure, then vasodilators as needed
Start a vasodilator (nitroprusside) alone first
Give aspirin and therapeutic heparin as for ACS
Aggressive IV fluid boluses to raise blood pressure
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of medical therapy in aortic dissection is impulse control: reduce the shear force (dP/dt) on the aortic wall by first lowering heart rate with IV beta-blockade (esmolol or labetalol), targeting a heart rate around 60, then adding a vasodilator such as nitroprusside to reach a target systolic blood pressure (often ~100-120 mmHg) while controlling pain. Giving a vasodilator alone first (option B) causes reflex tachycardia that increases wall stress, so beta-blockade must precede it. Aspirin and heparin (option C) are appropriate for ACS but are dangerous in dissection and can worsen bleeding. IV fluid boluses to raise BP (option D) are counterproductive—the goal is to lower BP and shear stress, not raise it.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given that imaging shows a Stanford type A (ascending aorta) dissection, what is the definitive management?
options: Medical management alone with close monitoring
Emergent cardiothoracic surgical repair
Outpatient follow-up with serial imaging
Endovascular stenting of the descending aorta only
stages.explanation: Stanford type A dissections involve the ascending aorta and are a surgical emergency: they risk life-threatening complications such as aortic rupture, pericardial tamponade, acute severe aortic regurgitation, and coronary or cerebral malperfusion. Definitive treatment is emergent open cardiothoracic surgical repair while continuing impulse-control therapy. Medical management alone (option A) is appropriate for many uncomplicated type B (descending) dissections, not type A. Outpatient follow-up (option C) is grossly inappropriate for an acute type A dissection. Endovascular stenting of the descending aorta (option D) addresses some complicated type B dissections but does not treat the ascending involvement here. Rapid surgical consultation should occur in parallel with medical stabilization.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While awaiting the OR, he becomes acutely hypotensive with distended neck veins and muffled heart sounds. What is the most likely complication?
options: Cardiac tamponade from proximal extension of the dissection into the pericardium
Tension pneumothorax
Septic shock from pneumonia
Anaphylaxis to the CT contrast
stages.explanation: Beck's triad—hypotension, distended neck veins (elevated JVP), and muffled heart sounds—indicates cardiac tamponade, a feared and often fatal complication of proximal (type A) dissection when blood ruptures into the pericardial space. This is an indication for emergent surgical intervention; aggressive pericardiocentesis is generally avoided because relieving the tamponade can increase aortic wall stress and precipitate fatal re-bleeding, so definitive operative repair is prioritized. Tension pneumothorax (option B) causes absent breath sounds and tracheal deviation, not muffled heart sounds with a normal lung exam. Septic shock (option C) presents with fever and warm shock, not Beck's triad. Contrast anaphylaxis (option D) typically causes urticaria, bronchospasm, and shock minutes after injection, not this tamponade picture. Recognizing tamponade accelerates the move to the operating room.

cases.diagnosis: Acute Stanford type A aortic dissection
pearls: Tearing pain maximal at onset radiating to the back, an interarm BP differential, pulse deficits, and a new AR murmur point to aortic dissection.
CT angiography of the chest/abdomen confirms and classifies dissection (Stanford A vs B); a widened mediastinum on CXR is a clue.
Impulse-control therapy: IV beta-blocker FIRST (target HR ~60), then a vasodilator for BP—never a vasodilator alone (reflex tachycardia).
Stanford type A (ascending) = emergent surgery; uncomplicated type B is often managed medically. Avoid antiplatelets/anticoagulation/thrombolytics.

cases.title: Sudden Abdominal and Back Pain With Collapse
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe abdominal and flank pain with near-fainting.
cases.history: A 72-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after sudden, severe abdominal and left flank/back pain that began about 45 minutes ago, followed by lightheadedness and a near-syncopal episode. The pain is constant and severe. EMS reports he was pale and clammy en route with a low blood pressure that transiently improved with fluids. He denies trauma, hematemesis, or melena. He has a history of a known abdominal aortic aneurysm being monitored, measured at 4.8 cm at his last visit several months ago, plus hypertension, COPD, and a 50-pack-year smoking history. Medications: amlodipine, atorvastatin, and tiotropium inhaler. No known drug allergies. He lives with his wife, is a former heavy smoker, and drinks rarely. Family history includes a brother with an aortic aneurysm. He is normally independent. He has had vague intermittent abdominal discomfort for a few days, which he attributed to indigestion, before this acute, severe event.
cases.exam: He is pale, diaphoretic, and in severe distress. He is tachycardic and hypotensive. Abdominal exam reveals a tender, pulsatile mass palpable in the mid-abdomen and diffuse tenderness, most marked in the periumbilical and left flank regions; there is no rebound but he guards. Peripheral pulses are weak and thready. There is mild flank ecchymosis developing. Lungs have diffuse expiratory wheezes consistent with his COPD. Heart sounds are tachycardic and regular without murmurs. He is mildly confused, consistent with hypoperfusion, but moves all extremities. Extremities are cool with delayed capillary refill.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a known AAA, sudden abdominal/back pain, hypotension, and a pulsatile mass, what is the most likely diagnosis and immediate concern?
options: Renal colic from a ureteral stone
Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm causing hemorrhagic shock
Acute pancreatitis
Perforated peptic ulcer
stages.explanation: The triad of abdominal/back or flank pain, hypotension, and a pulsatile abdominal mass in an older man with a known AAA and smoking history is a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm until proven otherwise—a life-threatening surgical emergency causing hemorrhagic shock. Renal colic (option A) can mimic the pain and is a classic misdiagnosis, but it does not cause shock or a pulsatile mass. Acute pancreatitis (option C) causes epigastric pain radiating to the back but not a pulsatile mass, and shock would be late. A perforated ulcer (option D) produces a rigid, peritonitic abdomen and free air, not a pulsatile mass with this vascular history. Recognizing rupture immediately drives emergent surgical activation rather than a prolonged workup.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is hypotensive and unstable. What is the most appropriate immediate management priority?
options: Send him for a contrast CT and resuscitate aggressively to a normal blood pressure first
Activate emergent vascular surgery, obtain large-bore IV access and type/crossmatch, and use permissive hypotension while moving toward the OR
Administer thrombolytics for presumed mesenteric ischemia
Give a large normal saline bolus to achieve a systolic BP of 140 mmHg
stages.explanation: An unstable patient with suspected ruptured AAA needs immediate vascular surgery activation, large-bore IV access, and type-and-crossmatch with activation of massive transfusion, while expediting transfer to the operating room. Resuscitation uses permissive (controlled) hypotension—targeting a lower systolic pressure (around 70-90 mmHg) with mental status as a guide—to avoid dislodging clot and worsening hemorrhage. Sending an unstable patient for CT and resuscitating to normal pressure first (option A) wastes time and can precipitate fatal bleeding; unstable patients often go straight to the OR. Thrombolytics (option C) would be catastrophic in active hemorrhage. Aggressive fluids to a systolic of 140 (option D) increases bleeding and is exactly what permissive hypotension seeks to avoid.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Bedside ultrasound (FAST/aorta): dilated abdominal aorta ~6.5 cm with surrounding retroperitoneal fluid
Hemoglobin: 8.6 g/dL
Lactate: 4.8 mmol/L
Creatinine: 1.6 mg/dL
INR: 1.1
Type and crossmatch: in progress, massive transfusion protocol activated

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Bedside ultrasound confirms a large aneurysm with retroperitoneal fluid in this unstable patient. What is the definitive treatment?
options: Emergent operative repair (open or endovascular/EVAR) without delay
Admit to the ICU for medical blood pressure control only
Discharge with urgent outpatient vascular surgery follow-up
Start a beta-blocker infusion and observe for 24 hours
stages.explanation: A ruptured AAA confirmed in an unstable patient requires emergent operative repair without delay—either open surgical repair or endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR) if anatomy and resources permit—as the only definitive, life-saving treatment. Bedside ultrasound is ideal in the unstable patient because it rapidly confirms the aneurysm and free fluid without leaving the resuscitation area. ICU admission for BP control alone (option B) ignores ongoing hemorrhage and is fatal. Discharge with outpatient follow-up (option C) is grossly inappropriate for active rupture. A beta-blocker infusion and observation (option D) is appropriate impulse control for dissection, not for a bleeding ruptured aneurysm that needs surgical hemostasis. Time to repair directly affects survival.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: After successful repair and massive transfusion, his abdomen becomes tense and his urine output falls with rising airway pressures. Which complication should be suspected?
options: Abdominal compartment syndrome
Contrast-induced urticaria
Simple postoperative ileus requiring only observation
Delirium tremens
stages.explanation: After repair of a ruptured AAA—especially with massive transfusion, large-volume resuscitation, and retroperitoneal hematoma—intra-abdominal pressure can rise dangerously, producing abdominal compartment syndrome. The classic picture is a tense, distended abdomen with oliguria (from renal vein/perfusion compromise), elevated airway/ventilator pressures (from diaphragmatic splinting), and hemodynamic compromise; it is confirmed by an elevated bladder pressure and treated with decompressive laparotomy when severe. Contrast urticaria (option B) is a cutaneous allergic reaction, not this multisystem picture. A simple ileus (option C) does not cause oliguria with rising airway pressures and hemodynamic decline, and 'observation only' would be dangerous here. Delirium tremens (option D) causes agitation, tremor, and autonomic instability days into alcohol withdrawal, not a tense abdomen with oliguria. Early recognition is limb- and life-saving.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: For context, at what size or rate of growth is elective AAA repair generally recommended to prevent rupture?
options: At any size once detected
Diameter ≥5.5 cm (men), rapid growth (>0.5 cm/6 months), or symptomatic aneurysm
Only after the aneurysm exceeds 8 cm
Elective repair is never indicated
stages.explanation: Elective AAA repair is generally recommended when the diameter reaches about 5.5 cm in men (often a lower threshold, ~5.0 cm, in women), when there is rapid expansion (>0.5 cm over 6 months or >1 cm/year), or when the aneurysm becomes symptomatic—because rupture risk rises steeply with size. Smaller asymptomatic aneurysms are monitored with surveillance ultrasound and risk-factor modification (notably smoking cessation and blood pressure control). Repairing any aneurysm at any size (option A) overtreats low-risk small aneurysms. Waiting until 8 cm (option C) is far too late given the high rupture risk well below that. Saying repair is never indicated (option D) is plainly wrong. This patient's known 4.8 cm aneurysm had grown and then ruptured.

cases.diagnosis: Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm with hemorrhagic shock
pearls: Abdominal/back pain + hypotension + pulsatile abdominal mass in an older smoker = ruptured AAA until proven otherwise; renal colic is a classic misdiagnosis.
Unstable patients go straight to the OR—activate vascular surgery, get large-bore access and crossmatch, and use permissive hypotension (avoid over-resuscitation).
Bedside ultrasound rapidly confirms the aneurysm and free fluid in unstable patients; reserve CT for stable patients.
Elective repair when ≥5.5 cm (men), rapid growth, or symptomatic; otherwise surveillance plus smoking cessation and BP control.

cases.title: One Swollen, Painful Leg
cases.chiefComplaint: Three days of swelling and aching pain in the left calf.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman presents to clinic with 3 days of progressive swelling, warmth, and aching pain in her left lower leg. The pain is worse when she walks and the calf feels tight. She returned 5 days ago from a 10-hour flight home from an overseas vacation. She denies chest pain, shortness of breath, hemoptysis, or palpitations. There is no history of recent trauma to the leg, no fever, and no skin breaks or insect bites. PMH includes obesity and hypertension. She started a combined estrogen-containing oral contraceptive a few months ago for perimenopausal symptoms. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily and the combined oral contraceptive. No known drug allergies. She is a marketing manager, does not smoke, and drinks wine occasionally. Family history is notable for a sister who had a 'blood clot in the lung' after surgery. She has had no prior episodes of leg swelling or known clotting disorders, and her last menstrual period was several weeks ago.
cases.exam: The left calf is visibly swollen compared with the right, with a measured circumference 3.5 cm greater than the contralateral calf measured 10 cm below the tibial tuberosity. The left leg is warm and mildly erythematous along the calf, with tenderness over the deep calf veins and mild pitting edema. There is no superficial cord or obvious cellulitic spreading border, and there are no skin ulcers. Distal pulses are intact, and the foot is well perfused. The right leg is normal. Lungs are clear bilaterally, heart sounds are regular without murmur, and there is no jugular venous distention. The abdomen is soft and nontender.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step to risk-stratify this patient's suspected deep vein thrombosis?
options: Immediately start therapeutic anticoagulation before any testing
Calculate a clinical pretest probability score (e.g., Wells score for DVT)
Order CT pulmonary angiography
Obtain a venogram as the first test
stages.explanation: The first step in evaluating suspected DVT is to estimate pretest probability using a validated clinical decision rule such as the Wells score for DVT, which incorporates findings like active cancer, immobilization, calf swelling >3 cm, pitting edema, and tenderness along the deep veins. The score then guides whether to use D-dimer and/or compression ultrasound. Starting anticoagulation before any testing (option A) may be reasonable only when there will be a delay in diagnostics and probability is high, but it is not the routine first step. CT pulmonary angiography (option C) evaluates pulmonary embolism, not leg DVT, and she has no pulmonary symptoms. A venogram (option D) is invasive and rarely used now that ultrasound is standard.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her Wells score places her in the 'likely' (high-probability) category. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic test?
options: D-dimer alone, and if negative, no further testing
Compression ultrasound of the leg veins
No testing; treat empirically and never image
MRI venography of the pelvis first
stages.explanation: When pretest probability is high ('DVT likely'), compression ultrasound of the proximal leg veins is the appropriate next test; loss of vein compressibility confirms thrombosis. In high-probability patients, a normal D-dimer cannot reliably exclude DVT, so relying on D-dimer alone (option A) is inappropriate—D-dimer is most useful to rule out DVT in low/intermediate-probability patients. Treating empirically and never imaging (option C) risks unnecessary long-term anticoagulation and misses alternative diagnoses. MRI venography of the pelvis (option D) is reserved for special situations (e.g., suspected iliac/pelvic vein thrombosis not seen on ultrasound) and is not the standard first imaging test for a calf/leg DVT. Compression ultrasound is noninvasive, sensitive, and readily available.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Compression ultrasound: noncompressible left popliteal vein with intraluminal thrombus extending into the femoral vein
D-dimer: elevated
CBC: normal
Creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL
Platelets: 230 ×10³/µL
INR: 1.0

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Ultrasound confirms a proximal DVT and she is hemodynamically stable with no high bleeding risk. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Outpatient anticoagulation with a direct oral anticoagulant (e.g., apixaban or rivaroxaban)
Aspirin alone
Catheter-directed thrombolysis for all uncomplicated DVTs
Inferior vena cava filter as first-line therapy
stages.explanation: For a stable patient with acute proximal DVT and no high bleeding risk, outpatient anticoagulation with a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) such as apixaban or rivaroxaban is first-line; DOACs are convenient, do not require routine monitoring, and most uncomplicated DVTs can be managed at home. Aspirin alone (option B) is inadequate treatment for acute DVT. Catheter-directed thrombolysis (option C) is reserved for selected severe cases (e.g., limb-threatening iliofemoral DVT/phlegmasia), not routine DVT. An IVC filter (option D) is indicated only when anticoagulation is contraindicated or fails, not as first-line therapy. Typical duration is at least 3 months; provoked clots (e.g., from a transient risk factor) are often treated for 3 months.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which modifiable risk factor in her history should be specifically addressed to reduce recurrence risk?
options: Discontinue the estrogen-containing oral contraceptive and discuss alternative contraception
Begin a high-dose estrogen pill
Avoid all physical activity permanently
Start daily aspirin indefinitely instead of anticoagulation
stages.explanation: Estrogen-containing oral contraceptives increase venous thromboembolism risk, and this patient developed a DVT in that setting (compounded by recent prolonged travel and obesity). The estrogen-containing contraceptive should be discontinued and non-estrogen alternatives discussed to reduce recurrence risk, with anticoagulation maintained while estrogen is being cleared. Starting a high-dose estrogen pill (option B) would increase risk and is contraindicated. Avoiding all activity permanently (option C) is wrong—immobility promotes clots, and graduated mobility/ambulation is encouraged. Substituting daily aspirin for anticoagulation in the acute treatment phase (option D) is inadequate. Other measures include weight management, periodic movement and hydration during long travel, and evaluation for whether the clot is provoked versus unprovoked to determine anticoagulation duration.

cases.diagnosis: Acute proximal deep vein thrombosis (provoked)
pearls: Risk-stratify suspected DVT with the Wells score first; it dictates whether to use D-dimer, ultrasound, or both.
D-dimer reliably excludes DVT only in low/intermediate-probability patients; high-probability patients go straight to compression ultrasound.
Stable proximal DVT without high bleeding risk is treated outpatient with a DOAC; reserve thrombolysis and IVC filters for special cases.
Address modifiable risks—stop estrogen-containing contraceptives, mobilize during long travel, manage weight; provoked clots usually need ~3 months of anticoagulation.

cases.title: High Readings at a Wellness Visit
cases.chiefComplaint: Elevated blood pressure noted at a routine physical.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man presents for a routine wellness visit. He feels well and has no headaches, chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea, or visual changes. At a pharmacy kiosk two weeks ago his blood pressure read 150/92 mmHg, and today the medical assistant records 148/94 mmHg. He has no prior diagnosis of hypertension. He takes no prescription medications, only an occasional ibuprofen for knee pain, and no over-the-counter decongestants or supplements. He has no known drug allergies. He works as an accountant, is sedentary, and adds salt liberally to meals. He drinks three to four beers most evenings and does not smoke or use illicit drugs. He sleeps about six hours nightly and snores, though his wife has not witnessed apneas. His father had hypertension and died of a myocardial infarction at age 60; his mother has type 2 diabetes. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable. He denies polyuria, flushing, episodic palpitations, or muscle weakness that might suggest a secondary cause.
cases.exam: The patient is a well-appearing, mildly overweight man in no distress (BMI 29 kg/m²). Funduscopic exam shows sharp disc margins without hemorrhages, exudates, or AV nicking. Neck is supple with no carotid bruits and no thyromegaly. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 and S2, no S3, S4, murmurs, or displaced apical impulse. Lungs are clear bilaterally. Abdomen is soft and nontender with no abdominal bruit and no palpable masses. Peripheral pulses are 2+ and symmetric in all extremities with no radio-femoral delay. There is no peripheral edema. Neurologic exam is grossly intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on today's reading and the prior kiosk measurement, what is the most appropriate next step before diagnosing hypertension?
options: Start a thiazide diuretic immediately
Obtain out-of-office measurements (home or ambulatory monitoring) or repeat readings to confirm
Order a renal artery duplex ultrasound
Reassure him that a single high reading is meaningless and recheck in one year
stages.explanation: Hypertension should be confirmed with repeated measurements, ideally with home or ambulatory monitoring, to exclude white-coat effect and establish an accurate average before committing a patient to lifelong therapy. Starting a thiazide immediately is premature without a confirmed diagnosis and a documented average. A renal artery ultrasound is a secondary-cause workup not indicated in a 48-year-old with no red flags. Dismissing readings of 148/94 mmHg and waiting a full year is inappropriate because the values are clearly elevated and warrant timely confirmation; ignoring them risks undertreating established hypertension. Documenting an accurate out-of-office average also helps tailor the eventual treatment target and avoids overtreating a patient whose office readings overstate his true burden.

stages.label: HOME BP LOG
stages.content:
Home BP average over 7 days (2 readings AM/PM): 146/93 mmHg
Fasting glucose: 104 mg/dL
Basic metabolic panel: Na 140, K 4.2, Cr 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
Lipid panel: LDL 138 mg/dL, HDL 42 mg/dL, TG 160 mg/dL
Urinalysis: no protein, no blood
EKG: normal sinus rhythm, no LVH

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the 2017 ACC/AHA thresholds and his confirmed average of 146/93 mmHg, how is his blood pressure classified?
options: Elevated blood pressure
Stage 1 hypertension
Stage 2 hypertension
Normal blood pressure
stages.explanation: Under the 2017 ACC/AHA guideline, a systolic ≥140 or diastolic ≥90 mmHg defines stage 2 hypertension; his 146/93 mmHg falls in that range. Elevated blood pressure is systolic 120–129 with diastolic <80 mmHg. Stage 1 is systolic 130–139 or diastolic 80–89 mmHg, which his readings exceed. Normal is <120/<80 mmHg, clearly not the case here. Correct staging matters because stage 2 hypertension warrants both lifestyle modification and pharmacologic therapy at diagnosis, whereas elevated or stage 1 categories without high ASCVD risk may begin with lifestyle measures alone. Accurate classification at the first visit guides whether to start one drug or two and sets the blood-pressure goal he and his clinician will work toward over time.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which intervention has the LARGEST expected systolic blood pressure reduction among lifestyle changes you should recommend?
options: Limiting alcohol to ≤2 drinks per day
Adopting the DASH dietary pattern
Taking a daily multivitamin
Switching from beer to wine
stages.explanation: The DASH diet (rich in fruits, vegetables, low-fat dairy, reduced saturated fat) produces among the largest systolic reductions of any single lifestyle change, on the order of 11 mmHg. Moderating alcohol to ≤2 drinks/day does lower BP (about 4 mmHg) and is appropriate given his intake, but the magnitude is smaller than DASH. A multivitamin has no proven effect on blood pressure. Switching beverage type without reducing total alcohol provides no benefit. Sodium restriction, weight loss, and aerobic exercise are other high-yield measures, but DASH gives the greatest single-intervention effect among the choices.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: He has no diabetes, chronic kidney disease, or evidence of heart failure. Which first-line antihypertensive is most appropriate to initiate?
options: Atenolol
Clonidine
Chlorthalidone (a thiazide-type diuretic)
Hydralazine
stages.explanation: First-line agents for uncomplicated hypertension are a thiazide-type diuretic (e.g., chlorthalidone), a calcium channel blocker, or an ACE inhibitor/ARB; chlorthalidone is an excellent, evidence-supported choice with proven cardiovascular outcome benefit and a long duration of action. Beta-blockers like atenolol are no longer first-line for uncomplicated hypertension without a compelling indication such as coronary disease, prior myocardial infarction, or heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. Clonidine, a central alpha-2 agonist, causes rebound hypertension and sedation and is reserved for resistant cases. Hydralazine, a direct vasodilator, requires multiple daily doses and causes reflex tachycardia and fluid retention, making it unsuitable as monotherapy here.

cases.diagnosis: Stage 2 essential (primary) hypertension
pearls: Confirm hypertension with out-of-office (home or ambulatory) readings before committing to lifelong therapy, to exclude white-coat effect.
2017 ACC/AHA: stage 1 is 130–139/80–89 mmHg; stage 2 is ≥140/≥90 mmHg.
First-line agents for uncomplicated hypertension are thiazide-type diuretics, CCBs, or ACEi/ARBs—beta-blockers are not first-line without a compelling indication.
The DASH diet plus sodium restriction, weight loss, alcohol moderation, and aerobic exercise can each meaningfully lower systolic pressure.

cases.title: A Worrisome Cholesterol Panel
cases.chiefComplaint: Follow-up for an abnormal cholesterol panel.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man returns to discuss a fasting lipid panel drawn at his last visit. He feels well, with no chest pain, exertional dyspnea, or claudication. He has a history of hypertension well controlled on amlodipine 5 mg daily and is a current smoker of half a pack per day for 30 years. He has never had a heart attack, stroke, or known atherosclerotic disease, and he is not diabetic. He takes no other medications and has no known drug allergies. He works as a truck driver, exercises rarely, and eats a diet heavy in fast food. He drinks alcohol socially. His father had a myocardial infarction at age 58 and his older brother had a coronary stent placed at 55. On review of systems he denies muscle aches, tendon nodules, or symptoms of hypothyroidism. He has no personal history of liver disease. He is motivated to reduce his cardiovascular risk after his brother's recent procedure and wants to discuss whether he needs to start a medication.
cases.exam: The patient is a well-appearing man in no distress (BMI 28 kg/m²). There are no xanthelasmas around the eyelids and no tendon xanthomas at the Achilles or knuckles. There is no corneal arcus. Neck is supple with no carotid bruits and no thyromegaly. Cardiac exam shows a regular rate and rhythm with normal S1 and S2 and no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs are clear. Abdomen is soft and nontender with no organomegaly. Peripheral pulses are 2+ and symmetric, and there is no femoral or abdominal bruit. There is no peripheral edema and no skin changes of chronic ischemia. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: LIPID PANEL
stages.content:
Total cholesterol: 248 mg/dL
LDL cholesterol: 172 mg/dL
HDL cholesterol: 38 mg/dL
Triglycerides: 190 mg/dL
Fasting glucose: 98 mg/dL
HbA1c: 5.5%
TSH: 2.1 mIU/L
ALT/AST: within normal limits

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: To guide statin therapy in this 52-year-old without diabetes or known ASCVD, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Calculate his 10-year ASCVD risk using the pooled cohort equations
Start a high-intensity statin without further risk assessment
Order a coronary angiogram
Repeat the lipid panel in one year and take no action now
stages.explanation: For primary prevention in adults 40–75 without diabetes and with LDL 70–189 mg/dL, the 10-year ASCVD risk (pooled cohort equations) drives the statin decision. His LDL of 172 mg/dL is below the 190 mg/dL threshold that would mandate a statin automatically, so risk calculation is needed. Starting a high-intensity statin without assessment skips the step that determines intensity and indication. A coronary angiogram is invasive and not a screening tool in an asymptomatic patient. Deferring action a year ignores a clearly atherogenic profile and his risk factors, missing an opportunity for prevention.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His calculated 10-year ASCVD risk is 14.5%. How is his risk category classified under the ACC/AHA cholesterol guideline?
options: Low risk (<5%)
Borderline risk (5–7.4%)
Intermediate risk (7.5–19.9%)
High risk (≥20%)
stages.explanation: A 10-year ASCVD risk of 7.5% to 19.9% is the intermediate-risk category, where moderate- to high-intensity statin therapy is recommended, especially in the presence of risk-enhancing factors. Low risk is <5% and borderline is 5–7.4%, both below his 14.5% value. High risk is ≥20%, which his score does not reach. Categorizing him as intermediate is important because it favors statin initiation after a clinician–patient discussion, and his risk enhancers (current smoking, family history of premature coronary disease, low HDL, metabolic features) strengthen the case for treatment. Documenting the category also frames the shared decision-making conversation and clarifies how aggressively to lower his LDL and address his other modifiable risk factors going forward.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given intermediate risk plus risk-enhancing factors, which statin regimen is most appropriate to start?
options: Low-intensity simvastatin 10 mg daily
Moderate- to high-intensity statin (e.g., atorvastatin 20–40 mg daily)
Ezetimibe monotherapy
A fibrate as first-line LDL-lowering therapy
stages.explanation: For intermediate-risk patients with risk enhancers, a moderate- to high-intensity statin such as atorvastatin 20–40 mg is the appropriate first-line therapy, targeting at least a 30–50% LDL reduction. Low-intensity simvastatin 10 mg yields insufficient LDL lowering for his risk profile. Ezetimibe is an add-on when statins alone fail to reach goal or are not tolerated, not first-line monotherapy. Fibrates primarily lower triglycerides and are not first-line for LDL-driven ASCVD prevention; they have not shown the cardiovascular outcome benefit that statins have demonstrated. Smoking cessation counseling, weight loss, and dietary change should accompany the statin, since lifestyle measures and pharmacotherapy together yield the greatest reduction in his long-term cardiovascular risk.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After starting atorvastatin, what is the most appropriate monitoring plan?
options: Recheck a fasting lipid panel in 4–12 weeks to assess adherence and LDL response
Check creatine kinase every month routinely even if asymptomatic
Stop the statin if any mild, transient myalgia occurs
Recheck liver enzymes weekly for the first month
stages.explanation: After initiating or adjusting a statin, a fasting lipid panel in 4–12 weeks confirms adherence and the expected LDL reduction, guiding any intensification. Routine monthly CK in an asymptomatic patient is not recommended; CK is checked when significant muscle symptoms develop. Mild, transient myalgia does not require immediate discontinuation; clinicians can continue, reassess, or briefly hold and rechallenge. Routine weekly liver enzyme monitoring is unnecessary; baseline transaminases are obtained, and repeat testing is reserved for symptoms or clinical indication rather than fixed weekly intervals. Reinforcing adherence and lifestyle change at the follow-up visit, and intensifying therapy if the LDL target is not met, completes a sound primary-prevention monitoring strategy for this patient.

cases.diagnosis: Hyperlipidemia warranting statin therapy for primary ASCVD prevention
pearls: LDL ≥190 mg/dL mandates high-intensity statin regardless of calculated risk; below that, use the pooled cohort equations in non-diabetic adults 40–75.
Risk enhancers (family history of premature ASCVD, smoking, low HDL, metabolic syndrome) tip intermediate-risk patients toward statin therapy.
Moderate- to high-intensity statins aim for a 30–50% (or ≥50%) LDL reduction; recheck a lipid panel in 4–12 weeks.
A coronary artery calcium score can reclassify risk when the statin decision is uncertain in intermediate-risk patients.

cases.title: Passing Out During Basketball
cases.chiefComplaint: Brief loss of consciousness while playing basketball.
cases.history: A 22-year-old college athlete presents after fainting during a pickup basketball game. He recalls feeling lightheaded and having palpitations during a fast break, then collapsing; bystanders say he was out for about 20 seconds and recovered quickly without confusion or incontinence. This is his second episode; the first occurred while sprinting last year and he attributed it to dehydration. He has noticed occasional exertional chest tightness and shortness of breath that resolve with rest. He takes no medications, uses no performance supplements or stimulants, and has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and denies cocaine or other illicit drug use. His family history is notable: a paternal uncle died suddenly at age 35 during exercise, and his father has an unspecified "thick heart." Review of systems is negative for fever, recent viral illness, or lower-extremity edema. He is anxious about whether he can keep competing on his team and asks what caused the episode.
cases.exam: The patient is a fit young man in no distress. Jugular venous pressure is normal. Carotid upstrokes are brisk. Cardiac exam reveals a harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur best heard at the left lower sternal border and apex; the murmur intensifies when he stands from squatting and during Valsalva, and softens with passive leg raise and handgrip. There is no significant radiation to the carotids. The apical impulse is sustained without displacement. S1 and S2 are normal and there is no fixed splitting. Lungs are clear bilaterally. There is no peripheral edema, and peripheral pulses are 2+ and symmetric without radio-femoral delay. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The murmur increases with Valsalva and standing and decreases with handgrip and leg raise. What does this maneuver pattern most strongly suggest?
options: A fixed valvular obstruction such as calcific aortic stenosis
A dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction
An innocent flow murmur of an athletic heart
Mitral regurgitation from a flail leaflet
stages.explanation: Murmurs that get louder with reduced preload/afterload (Valsalva, standing) and softer with increased preload/afterload (squatting, leg raise, handgrip) point to dynamic LVOT obstruction, the hallmark of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy; less ventricular volume brings the hypertrophied septum and mitral apparatus closer together, worsening obstruction. Fixed calcific aortic stenosis softens with Valsalva because flow across a fixed orifice falls with less preload. An innocent flow murmur does not intensify with Valsalva and lacks the syncope and family history here. A flail-leaflet mitral regurgitation murmur is holosystolic and increases with handgrip (afterload), the opposite pattern.

stages.label: EKG & ECHO
stages.content:
EKG: sinus rhythm, marked left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage, deep narrow Q waves in inferolateral leads, prominent precordial T-wave inversions
Transthoracic echo: asymmetric septal hypertrophy with septal thickness 19 mm (posterior wall 10 mm)
Systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve with resting LVOT gradient 45 mmHg, rising to 80 mmHg with Valsalva
LVEF 70%; left atrium mildly enlarged; no significant aortic valve calcification

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which echocardiographic finding is most specific for the mechanism of obstruction in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy?
options: A reduced left ventricular ejection fraction
Systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve with a dynamic LVOT gradient
Concentric, symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy
Heavy aortic valve calcification with restricted leaflets
stages.explanation: Systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve producing a dynamic LVOT gradient is the characteristic mechanism of obstruction in HCM, where the hypertrophied septum and Venturi forces pull the anterior mitral leaflet into the outflow tract during systole. Reduced EF is atypical—HCM usually shows preserved or hyperdynamic systolic function. Concentric symmetric hypertrophy suggests hypertension or aortic stenosis rather than the asymmetric septal pattern of HCM. Heavy aortic valve calcification indicates valvular aortic stenosis, a fixed obstruction, not the dynamic, maneuver-sensitive obstruction seen here with SAM. Recognizing this mechanism also explains why preload-reducing maneuvers worsen his murmur and symptoms, and it directs both medical therapy and counseling about avoiding dehydration and abrupt exertion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond starting medical therapy, which counseling point is most critical for this patient at his next steps?
options: He may continue competitive sprinting sports as long as he stays hydrated
He should avoid intense competitive athletics and undergo risk stratification for sudden cardiac death
He should begin a high-dose diuretic to reduce the outflow gradient
He should start digoxin to improve symptoms
stages.explanation: HCM is a leading cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes; he must avoid intense competitive athletics and undergo SCD risk stratification (prior syncope, family history of sudden death, septal thickness, nonsustained VT, and abnormal exercise BP response). Allowing continued competitive sprinting ignores his exertional syncope and high-risk family history. Diuretics reduce preload and can worsen dynamic LVOT obstruction, so high-dose diuresis is harmful. Digoxin is a positive inotrope that increases contractility and the outflow gradient, also worsening obstruction; negative inotropes such as beta-blockers are preferred instead. Family screening with echocardiography and an EKG is also warranted given the autosomal dominant inheritance and his uncle's sudden death, since first-degree relatives may harbor the same condition.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which medication is the most appropriate first-line therapy to relieve his obstructive symptoms?
options: A non-vasodilating beta-blocker such as metoprolol
A long-acting nitrate
Lisinopril
Amlodipine
stages.explanation: A non-vasodilating beta-blocker (e.g., metoprolol) is first-line in obstructive HCM; its negative inotropic and chronotropic effects reduce contractility, prolong diastolic filling, and lower the dynamic outflow gradient. Nitrates reduce preload and afterload, increasing the gradient and potentially provoking syncope, so they are contraindicated. ACE inhibitors like lisinopril are afterload reducers that can worsen dynamic obstruction. Dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers such as amlodipine cause vasodilation and reflex tachycardia, worsening the gradient; if a calcium channel blocker is needed, the non-dihydropyridine verapamil is preferred for its negative inotropy. If symptoms persist despite an adequately dosed beta-blocker, disopyramide or, in refractory obstructive cases, septal reduction therapy (myectomy or alcohol septal ablation) can be considered next.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: He is found to have several high-risk features on stratification. Which intervention most directly addresses his risk of sudden cardiac death?
options: Placement of an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD)
Starting aspirin and a statin
Initiating warfarin for stroke prevention
Coronary artery bypass grafting
stages.explanation: An implantable cardioverter-defibrillator is the definitive intervention to prevent sudden cardiac death in HCM patients with high-risk features (unexplained syncope, family history of SCD, massive hypertrophy, nonsustained VT, abnormal exercise BP). It treats the lethal ventricular arrhythmias that cause sudden death. Aspirin and a statin address atherosclerotic risk, not the arrhythmic mechanism in HCM. Warfarin is for thromboembolism in atrial fibrillation, which is not his current issue. CABG treats obstructive coronary disease, irrelevant to a young patient whose risk stems from the cardiomyopathy and its arrhythmias. The device is chosen after weighing his young age, lead longevity, and the cumulative weight of his risk markers in a shared decision with the patient and family.

cases.diagnosis: Obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
pearls: An LVOT murmur that increases with Valsalva/standing and decreases with squatting/handgrip is classic for HCM (opposite of aortic stenosis and mitral regurgitation).
Systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve with a dynamic gradient is the characteristic obstructive mechanism on echo.
Avoid preload/afterload reducers (nitrates, diuretics, ACEi, dihydropyridine CCBs) and inotropes (digoxin); use negative inotropes such as beta-blockers or verapamil.
HCM is a top cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes—stratify risk and place an ICD for high-risk patients; restrict intense competitive sports.

cases.title: Sharp Chest Pain That Eases Leaning Forward
cases.chiefComplaint: Sharp chest pain worse with breathing and lying flat.
cases.history: A 35-year-old man comes to the emergency department with three days of sharp, stabbing left-sided chest pain. The pain is worse when he lies flat and when he takes a deep breath, and it improves noticeably when he sits up and leans forward. He reports a low-grade fever and an upper respiratory illness with cough and rhinorrhea about a week ago. He denies exertional component, diaphoresis, radiation to the arm or jaw, or syncope. He has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications; he has used acetaminophen with minimal relief. He has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and denies illicit drug use including cocaine. There is no personal or family history of premature coronary disease, autoimmune disease, or malignancy. Review of systems is negative for leg swelling, recent immobilization or travel, hemoptysis, and night sweats. He is anxious that he may be having a heart attack given the location of the pain, prompting his visit tonight.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and mildly uncomfortable, sitting forward on the gurney. Jugular venous pressure is normal without distension. Cardiac auscultation at the left lower sternal border reveals a scratchy, high-pitched three-component friction rub best heard with the patient leaning forward at end-expiration; heart sounds are not muffled. There is no pulsus paradoxus. Lungs are clear with good air entry bilaterally and no consolidation. The chest wall is not tender to palpation. The abdomen is soft and nontender. Extremities show no edema, no calf tenderness, and symmetric 2+ pulses. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given positional pleuritic chest pain and a friction rub after a viral illness, which test is most important to obtain first?
options: CT pulmonary angiogram
12-lead electrocardiogram
Exercise stress test
D-dimer
stages.explanation: A 12-lead EKG is the essential first test; in acute pericarditis it classically shows diffuse, concave-upward ST elevation with PR-segment depression, helping distinguish it from STEMI and confirming the clinical picture. A CT pulmonary angiogram targets pulmonary embolism, which is less likely given a positional pleuritic pain with a rub and no PE risk factors. An exercise stress test is contraindicated in acute chest pain of uncertain cause and would not diagnose pericarditis. D-dimer is nonspecific and would not establish the diagnosis here; the EKG is rapid, available, and directly supports pericarditis while excluding an acute coronary occlusion pattern.

stages.label: EKG & LABS
stages.content:
EKG: diffuse concave-upward ST-segment elevation in leads I, II, aVL, aVF, V2–V6
PR-segment depression in multiple leads with PR elevation in aVR
No reciprocal ST depression, no Q waves
Troponin I: 0.04 ng/mL (mildly elevated, ref <0.04)
CRP: 48 mg/L (elevated)
WBC: 11.5 ×10³/µL
Chest X-ray: normal cardiac silhouette, clear lungs
Bedside echo: trivial pericardial effusion, no tamponade physiology

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which EKG feature best distinguishes acute pericarditis from an acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction?
options: Convex ("tombstone") ST elevation localized to one coronary territory
Diffuse concave ST elevation with PR depression and no reciprocal changes
New left bundle branch block
Deep symmetric T-wave inversions in the anterior leads
stages.explanation: Diffuse, concave-upward ST elevation spanning multiple territories with PR-segment depression and the absence of reciprocal ST depression is characteristic of pericarditis, reflecting widespread epicardial inflammation rather than a single occluded vessel. Convex, territorial ("tombstone") ST elevation with reciprocal changes points to STEMI. A new left bundle branch block in the right context suggests acute ischemia, not pericarditis. Deep symmetric anterior T-wave inversions suggest ischemia (e.g., Wellens pattern) rather than the acute pericarditis pattern. The combination of diffuse ST elevation plus PR depression is the most reliable discriminator here. Serial tracings may also show the characteristic evolution of pericarditis, with ST normalization followed by T-wave inversion, helping confirm the diagnosis when the initial picture is ambiguous.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this otherwise healthy patient with idiopathic/viral pericarditis, which regimen is the recommended first-line treatment?
options: A systemic corticosteroid taper alone
A high-dose NSAID plus colchicine
Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics
Therapeutic anticoagulation with heparin
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for acute idiopathic/viral pericarditis is a high-dose NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen or aspirin) combined with colchicine; colchicine reduces symptoms and substantially lowers the recurrence rate. Corticosteroids are reserved for contraindications to NSAIDs, specific etiologies (e.g., autoimmune), or refractory cases because they increase recurrence when used as initial therapy. Antibiotics are unnecessary without evidence of bacterial (purulent) pericarditis, which is uncommon and clinically distinct. Anticoagulation is actually relatively contraindicated in acute pericarditis because it can promote hemorrhagic effusion and tamponade; it has no therapeutic role here. Activity restriction until symptoms and inflammatory markers normalize, along with a tapering NSAID course and a longer colchicine course, rounds out the standard outpatient regimen for this patient.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He is hemodynamically stable with only a trivial effusion and a barely elevated troponin. Which feature would be a high-risk marker prompting admission rather than outpatient management?
options: Pain that improves with leaning forward
A pericardial friction rub on exam
A large pericardial effusion or tamponade physiology
A CRP of 48 mg/L
stages.explanation: High-risk features that warrant admission include a large pericardial effusion or tamponade, high fever (>38°C), subacute onset, immunosuppression, anticoagulant use, trauma, and failure to respond to NSAIDs after a week; a large effusion or tamponade physiology is the clearest of these. Pain relieved by leaning forward is a typical, low-risk symptom of pericarditis. A friction rub is a classic diagnostic sign, not a risk marker. An elevated CRP supports the diagnosis and helps monitor response but is not itself a high-risk admission criterion; many patients with idiopathic pericarditis are managed safely as outpatients.

cases.diagnosis: Acute idiopathic (presumed viral) pericarditis
pearls: Diagnosis needs ≥2 of: pleuritic chest pain relieved by sitting forward, friction rub, diffuse ST elevation with PR depression, and a new/worsening effusion.
Diffuse concave ST elevation with PR depression and no reciprocal changes distinguishes pericarditis from STEMI.
First-line treatment is a high-dose NSAID plus colchicine; colchicine reduces recurrence. Avoid steroids initially and avoid anticoagulation.
Admit for high-risk features: large effusion/tamponade, fever >38°C, subacute course, immunosuppression, anticoagulation, trauma, or NSAID failure.

cases.title: Crashing Blood Pressure in the Unit
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening hypotension and shortness of breath in a hospitalized patient.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman in the ICU is being treated for metastatic breast cancer complicated by a malignant pericardial effusion noted on a recent echo. Over the past few hours the nurse reports progressive shortness of breath, restlessness, and falling blood pressure despite intravenous fluids. She describes a sensation of chest fullness and feeling as though she cannot catch her breath even at rest. Her chemotherapy regimen includes an anthracycline. She has a history of hypertension previously treated with lisinopril, which was held this morning for hypotension. She has no known drug allergies. She is a former smoker with a 20-pack-year history, quit ten years ago, and does not drink alcohol. There is no recent chest trauma or cardiac procedure. Family history is noncontributory. The covering team is called urgently because her systolic pressure has dropped into the 80s and she has become increasingly anxious and tachycardic, with cool extremities and decreased urine output over the last hour.
cases.exam: The patient is anxious, tachypneic, and diaphoretic, sitting upright. The jugular venous pressure is markedly elevated with distended neck veins. Cardiac auscultation reveals distant, muffled heart sounds without an obvious murmur; the apical impulse is difficult to palpate. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally without crackles. There is a measured pulsus paradoxus of 18 mmHg, with a palpable drop in pulse amplitude on inspiration. Extremities are cool with delayed capillary refill and faint, thready peripheral pulses. There is no peripheral edema or calf tenderness. The abdomen is soft. She is alert but increasingly restless and able to speak only in short phrases.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Hypotension, jugular venous distension, and muffled heart sounds (Beck's triad) with pulsus paradoxus most strongly suggest which diagnosis?
options: Tension pneumothorax
Cardiac tamponade
Acute pulmonary embolism
Acute decompensated left heart failure
stages.explanation: Beck's triad (hypotension, jugular venous distension, muffled heart sounds) plus pulsus paradoxus in a patient with a known malignant effusion is classic for cardiac tamponade, where accumulating pericardial fluid impairs diastolic filling. Tension pneumothorax also causes hypotension and JVD but produces absent breath sounds and tracheal deviation, not muffled heart sounds with clear lungs. Pulmonary embolism can cause hypotension and JVD from RV strain but typically lacks muffled heart sounds and a pre-existing effusion. Acute left heart failure usually presents with pulmonary crackles and an S3, whereas her lungs are clear—pointing away from a left-sided congestion mechanism.

stages.label: BEDSIDE ECHO & EKG
stages.content:
Bedside echo: large circumferential pericardial effusion
Diastolic collapse of the right atrium and right ventricle
Exaggerated respiratory variation in mitral and tricuspid inflow velocities
Dilated IVC with minimal respiratory collapse
EKG: sinus tachycardia with low QRS voltage and electrical alternans
Chest X-ray: enlarged, globular ("water-bottle") cardiac silhouette

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which echocardiographic finding is the most specific sign confirming hemodynamically significant tamponade?
options: A small anterior pericardial effusion
Diastolic collapse of the right atrium and right ventricle
