cases.chiefComplaint: Three days of worsening left lower abdominal pain with fever and altered bowel habits.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents with three days of steadily worsening left lower quadrant abdominal pain, now constant and aching, accompanied by subjective fevers and chills. He reports a low-grade appetite, some loose stools alternating with constipation, and mild nausea without vomiting. He has had occasional similar but milder left-sided pains over the past two years that resolved on their own. His diet is low in fiber. He has hypertension treated with lisinopril 20 mg daily and takes no NSAIDs or anticoagulants. He has no known drug allergies. He is a former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago) and drinks alcohol socially. Family history is notable for colon cancer in his father at age 68. He has never had a colonoscopy. Review of systems is positive for dysuria-like urinary urgency but negative for hematochezia, melena, vomiting, syncope, and prior abdominal surgery. He denies recent travel or sick contacts.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable but nontoxic. The abdomen is soft with focal tenderness and mild voluntary guarding in the left lower quadrant; a vague fullness is palpable there. There is no rebound tenderness or rigidity to suggest diffuse peritonitis, and the remainder of the abdomen is nontender. Bowel sounds are present and slightly hypoactive. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Rectal exam reveals no mass and stool is brown and guaiac-negative. The patient is not jaundiced. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. There is no inguinal hernia or lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the clinical picture of left lower quadrant pain, fever, and localized tenderness, which imaging study is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis and assess for complications?
options: CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast
Colonoscopy now during the acute presentation
Plain abdominal radiographs only
Right upper quadrant ultrasound
MRI of the lumbar spine
stages.explanation: The classic triad of left lower quadrant pain, fever, and localized tenderness in this demographic strongly suggests acute diverticulitis, and contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis is the imaging study of choice—it confirms the diagnosis (bowel wall thickening, pericolic fat stranding) and, critically, detects complications such as abscess, perforation, obstruction, or fistula that change management. Colonoscopy is contraindicated during acute diverticulitis because of perforation risk; it is performed weeks later to exclude malignancy. Plain films can show free air but are insensitive and do not characterize the disease. RUQ ultrasound evaluates the gallbladder/biliary tree, not the left colon. Lumbar MRI is unrelated to this presentation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 14.2 ×10³/µL with left shift
CRP 88 mg/L
Lactate 1.4 mmol/L
Urinalysis: trace WBC, no bacteria
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: sigmoid diverticulosis with focal wall thickening and pericolic fat stranding; no abscess, no free air, no obstruction

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT confirms uncomplicated acute sigmoid diverticulitis without abscess or perforation. How is this best classified, and what does it imply?
options: Complicated diverticulitis requiring emergent surgery
Uncomplicated diverticulitis, which can often be managed without surgery
Diverticulosis without inflammation, requiring no treatment
Perforated diverticulitis with peritonitis
Diverticular abscess requiring percutaneous drainage
stages.explanation: Wall thickening and pericolic fat stranding without abscess, free air, obstruction, or fistula define uncomplicated acute diverticulitis. This category can frequently be managed conservatively—with bowel rest/diet modification and, in selected patients, antibiotics—rather than surgery. It is not complicated disease, which by definition includes abscess, perforation, obstruction, or fistula and may require drainage or operation. It is more than asymptomatic diverticulosis because there is active inflammation (fever, leukocytosis, CT findings). There is no free air or peritonitis to indicate perforation, and no fluid collection to drain. Classifying severity (often via the modified Hinchey system) directs whether outpatient management, IV antibiotics, drainage, or surgery is appropriate.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: This nontoxic patient is admitted for observation with a fever and leukocytosis but no abscess or perforation. Which management plan is most appropriate?
options: Bowel rest, IV fluids, and antibiotics covering gram-negative and anaerobic organisms
Immediate sigmoid colectomy
Oral fiber supplementation alone with no antibiotics or monitoring
Therapeutic colonoscopy with polypectomy now
High-dose corticosteroids
stages.explanation: For acute diverticulitis warranting admission—fever, leukocytosis, inability to tolerate oral intake, or significant symptoms—appropriate management is bowel rest, IV fluids, and antibiotics directed at enteric gram-negative rods and anaerobes (e.g., a regimen covering organisms such as E. coli and Bacteroides). Surgery is reserved for complicated disease (free perforation, obstruction, failure of medical therapy) or recurrent/refractory cases, not first-line uncomplicated disease. Fiber alone is appropriate for long-term prevention, not acute inflammation with systemic signs. Colonoscopy is contraindicated acutely. Corticosteroids have no role and could worsen the infectious process. Note that selected mild, uncomplicated outpatients can be managed without antibiotics, but his fever and leukocytosis support antimicrobial therapy here.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His symptoms resolve and he is discharged. What is the most appropriate follow-up recommendation?
options: No further evaluation is ever needed
Colonoscopy in approximately 6-8 weeks after the acute episode resolves
Immediate repeat CT in one week regardless of symptoms
Lifelong daily antibiotics to prevent recurrence
Routine surgical resection for every first episode
stages.explanation: After resolution of an episode of acute diverticulitis, a colonoscopy roughly 6-8 weeks later is recommended (when not recently done) to exclude an underlying colorectal malignancy that can mimic or coexist with diverticulitis—particularly relevant here given he has never been screened and has a family history of colon cancer. Saying no evaluation is needed misses this. Routine immediate repeat CT in an improving patient is unnecessary and adds radiation. Chronic prophylactic antibiotics are not standard and promote resistance. Elective resection is considered for recurrent, complicated, or smoldering disease and in select patients, but is not indicated after a single uncomplicated episode. Dietary fiber is advised for prevention.

cases.diagnosis: Acute uncomplicated sigmoid diverticulitis
pearls: Left lower quadrant pain, fever, and localized tenderness in an older adult should prompt suspicion for diverticulitis; CT with contrast confirms it and grades complications.
Avoid colonoscopy during the acute episode (perforation risk); perform it 6-8 weeks later to exclude malignancy.
Uncomplicated disease is often managed with bowel rest, fluids, and antibiotics—surgery is reserved for complications or refractory/recurrent disease.
Complicated diverticulitis means abscess, perforation, obstruction, or fistula and may need drainage or operative intervention.

cases.title: Cramping Pain and Vomiting After an Old Operation
cases.chiefComplaint: One day of crampy abdominal pain, distension, and repeated vomiting with no bowel movement.
cases.history: A 65-year-old woman presents with about 24 hours of intermittent, crampy, periumbilical abdominal pain that comes in waves, accompanied by progressive abdominal distension and several episodes of bilious vomiting. She has not passed stool or flatus since yesterday afternoon. She reports the pain initially eased between cramps but is now more persistent. Her surgical history is significant for an open hysterectomy 15 years ago and an appendectomy in her twenties. She has hypertension and takes amlodipine 5 mg daily; she uses no opioids or anticholinergics. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is unremarkable. Review of systems is positive for nausea, anorexia, and reduced urine output today and negative for hematemesis, hematochezia, fevers earlier in the course, chest pain, and prior similar episodes. She has not had a recent colonoscopy and reports no weight loss or change in stool caliber before this event.
cases.exam: The patient appears uncomfortable during cramping episodes. The abdomen is distended and tympanitic with diffuse mild tenderness but no localized peritoneal signs at presentation—no rebound or rigidity. High-pitched, hyperactive 'tinkling' bowel sounds with rushes are auscultated. A well-healed lower midline surgical scar is present. There is no incarcerated hernia palpable at the inguinal or umbilical regions. Rectal exam reveals an empty vault with no mass and guaiac-negative stool. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is reduced, consistent with volume depletion. There is no jaundice or flank tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this patient with crampy pain, bilious vomiting, obstipation, and prior abdominal surgery, what is the single most likely underlying cause of the obstruction?
options: Colon cancer
Postoperative adhesions
Gallstone ileus
Crohn disease stricture
Incarcerated inguinal hernia
stages.explanation: Adhesions from prior abdominal/pelvic surgery are the most common cause of small bowel obstruction in adults, and this patient has two prior open operations with a midline scar—classic for adhesive SBO. Her clinical picture (colicky pain, bilious emesis, obstipation, distension, hyperactive 'tinkling' bowel sounds) fits high small bowel obstruction. Colon cancer more often causes large bowel obstruction with progressive change in stool caliber and is less likely given no such history. Gallstone ileus is rare and typically occurs in older patients with pneumobilia and an ectopic gallstone. A Crohn stricture would usually have a known inflammatory bowel history. An incarcerated hernia is a common cause too, but none is palpable on her exam, making adhesions the best answer.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 11.8 ×10³/µL
Lactate 1.6 mmol/L
Na 138, K 3.4 mEq/L, Cl 96, HCO3 30 mEq/L
BUN 32 mg/dL, Cr 1.3 mg/dL
Upright abdominal X-ray: dilated loops of small bowel with multiple air-fluid levels and paucity of colonic gas
CT abdomen/pelvis: dilated small bowel to a transition point in the pelvis with decompressed distal bowel; no free air, normal bowel wall enhancement

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT shows a transition point with proximal dilation and distal decompression, normal bowel-wall enhancement, and no free air. What does this indicate?
options: Complete obstruction with established bowel ischemia
Mechanical small bowel obstruction without current signs of strangulation
Paralytic ileus rather than mechanical obstruction
Large bowel obstruction from a colonic mass
Free perforation requiring emergent laparotomy
stages.explanation: A discrete transition point with dilated proximal and collapsed distal bowel is the hallmark of a mechanical small bowel obstruction. Critically, normal bowel-wall enhancement, absence of free air, and a normal lactate indicate that the bowel remains viable—there are no current signs of strangulation/ischemia. This favors an initial trial of nonoperative management. Established ischemia would show wall thickening, poor/absent enhancement, mesenteric edema, pneumatosis, or a rising lactate—none present. Ileus shows diffuse dilation of small and large bowel without a transition point. The colon is decompressed with a small-bowel transition point, so this is not a large bowel obstruction. There is no free air, so no perforation needing emergent surgery.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a mechanical SBO without signs of strangulation, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate exploratory laparotomy
Bowel rest, nasogastric tube decompression, IV fluids, electrolyte correction, and serial reassessment
Oral laxatives and discharge with outpatient follow-up
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics and a clear liquid diet
Colonoscopic decompression
stages.explanation: An adhesive SBO without strangulation is managed initially with nonoperative 'tincture of time': bowel rest (NPO), nasogastric tube decompression to relieve proximal distension and reduce vomiting, IV fluid resuscitation, correction of electrolytes (she is hypokalemic and volume-depleted), and serial abdominal exams plus trending labs. Many adhesive obstructions resolve with this approach. Immediate laparotomy is reserved for strangulation, perforation, or failure of nonoperative management. Oral laxatives and discharge are unsafe and ineffective in obstruction. Antibiotics are not routinely indicated absent perforation or ischemia, and oral feeding is inappropriate while obstructed. Colonoscopic decompression treats certain large bowel problems (e.g., sigmoid volvulus, colonic pseudo-obstruction), not small bowel adhesive obstruction.

stages.label: REASSESSMENT (12 HOURS)
stages.content:
Now reports constant, severe abdominal pain (no longer colicky)
Temp 38.6 °C, HR 122 bpm, BP 104/64 mmHg
Abdomen with focal peritoneal signs (localized rebound)
WBC 18.4 ×10³/µL
Lactate 4.2 mmol/L (rising)
Repeat CT: segmental small bowel wall thickening with reduced enhancement and mesenteric edema

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Twelve hours into nonoperative management she develops constant pain, fever, tachycardia, peritoneal signs, leukocytosis, and a rising lactate, with CT now showing compromised bowel enhancement. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue nasogastric decompression and reassess in the morning
Add an antiemetic and increase IV fluids only
Urgent surgical exploration for strangulated/ischemic bowel
Trial of oral contrast challenge (e.g., gastrografin)
Discharge once pain is controlled with analgesia
stages.explanation: The shift from colicky to constant pain, with fever, tachycardia, hypotension, focal peritonitis, rising leukocytosis and lactate, and CT evidence of poor bowel-wall enhancement and mesenteric edema, signals strangulation with bowel ischemia—a surgical emergency requiring urgent operative exploration (and resection of nonviable bowel). Continued observation or merely adjusting fluids and antiemetics would allow progression to infarction, perforation, and sepsis. A gastrografin challenge is a tool to predict resolution in stable, nonstrangulated SBO and is contraindicated once ischemia/peritonitis is suspected. Discharge is clearly unsafe. Loss of the benign colicky pattern, peritoneal signs, and a climbing lactate are the red flags that mandate moving from nonoperative care to the operating room.

cases.diagnosis: Adhesive small bowel obstruction progressing to strangulation
pearls: Adhesions are the leading cause of small bowel obstruction; prior abdominal surgery is the key historical clue.
A CT transition point with proximal dilation, distal decompression, and preserved wall enhancement supports a nonoperative trial.
Manage uncomplicated SBO with NPO, NG decompression, IV fluids, and electrolyte correction with serial reassessment.
Constant pain, fever, peritoneal signs, leukocytosis, rising lactate, or poor bowel enhancement signal strangulation and mandate urgent surgery.

cases.title: Severe Belly Pain That Outstrips a Soft Abdomen
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe, unrelenting abdominal pain for several hours that seems worse than the exam suggests.
cases.history: A 77-year-old man presents with roughly four hours of sudden-onset, severe, diffuse abdominal pain that he describes as the worst of his life and constant. He has vomited once and had one loose bowel movement. He reports the pain is unrelenting and not relieved by position. His history is notable for atrial fibrillation for which he was prescribed apixaban but admits he frequently misses doses. He also has hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and prior tobacco use (40 pack-years). Medications include apixaban 5 mg twice daily (nonadherent), metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, and lisinopril 20 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He lives alone and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history includes coronary disease. Review of systems is positive for nausea and a vague history of post-meal abdominal pain and unintentional weight loss over the past few months ('food fear'), and negative for hematemesis, chest pain, fever at onset, and prior abdominal surgery. He appears anxious and restless from pain.
cases.exam: The patient is in obvious distress and restless, repeatedly shifting in the bed. Strikingly, the abdomen is soft with only mild diffuse tenderness and no rebound or guarding—pain is markedly out of proportion to the unremarkable abdominal examination. Bowel sounds are present but diminished. The cardiac rhythm is irregularly irregular without murmurs. There is no abdominal distension early on. Peripheral pulses are present but the patient has cool distal extremities. Rectal exam reveals brown stool that is guaiac-positive. There is no jaundice, no palpable pulsatile mass, and no flank ecchymosis. Lungs are clear.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An elderly man with atrial fibrillation presents with severe pain out of proportion to a benign abdominal exam. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute pancreatitis
Acute mesenteric ischemia (likely arterial embolism)
Perforated peptic ulcer
Acute diverticulitis
Small bowel obstruction
stages.explanation: Pain out of proportion to a relatively benign exam in an older patient with atrial fibrillation (a source of arterial emboli, compounded by missed anticoagulation) is the textbook presentation of acute mesenteric ischemia, most commonly from embolic occlusion of the superior mesenteric artery. His chronic postprandial pain and weight loss ('intestinal angina'/food fear) suggest underlying mesenteric atherosclerosis predisposing to acute-on-chronic ischemia. Pancreatitis usually presents with epigastric pain radiating to the back and elevated lipase, not this embolic picture. A perforated ulcer typically causes a rigid, peritonitic abdomen, opposite to his soft exam. Diverticulitis causes focal LLQ findings with fever. SBO produces colicky pain with distension and a surgical history, which he lacks. AF plus disproportionate pain = mesenteric ischemia until proven otherwise.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 19.6 ×10³/µL with left shift
Lactate 5.8 mmol/L (elevated)
Arterial pH 7.28, HCO3 16 mEq/L (metabolic acidosis)
Lipase 40 U/L (normal)
Cr 1.4 mg/dL
Lactate dehydrogenase elevated
ECG: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Labs show marked leukocytosis, an elevated lactate, and metabolic acidosis. Which is the most appropriate confirmatory test?
options: Right upper quadrant ultrasound
CT angiography of the abdomen and pelvis
Plain abdominal radiograph series
Colonoscopy
Upper GI endoscopy
stages.explanation: CT angiography of the abdomen/pelvis is the test of choice for suspected acute mesenteric ischemia: it can directly demonstrate arterial occlusion/embolus in the SMA, evaluate bowel viability (wall enhancement, pneumatosis, portal venous gas), and exclude alternatives—all rapidly. Given the time-critical nature, it should not be delayed. Elevated lactate and metabolic acidosis support the diagnosis but are nonspecific and often appear late once injury is advanced. RUQ ultrasound assesses the biliary tree, not mesenteric vessels. Plain films are insensitive early and only show late signs (e.g., pneumatosis, 'thumbprinting'). Colonoscopy and upper endoscopy are inappropriate for an acute arterial ischemic emergency and could delay revascularization or risk perforation in ischemic bowel.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT angiography abdomen/pelvis: abrupt filling defect in the proximal superior mesenteric artery consistent with embolus
Segment of jejunum and ileum with diminished mural enhancement
No pneumatosis or portal venous gas
No free air; aorta normal caliber without aneurysm

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CTA confirms an SMA embolus with threatened but not yet infarcted bowel. Beyond resuscitation, what is the most appropriate immediate management priority?
options: Outpatient anticoagulation and follow-up CTA in 48 hours
Immediate systemic thrombolysis as definitive therapy and discharge planning
Emergent revascularization (surgical embolectomy or endovascular) with broad-spectrum antibiotics, anticoagulation, and surgical evaluation for bowel resection
Bowel rest and observation since lactate may normalize
Proceed directly to colonoscopy for decompression
stages.explanation: Acute mesenteric ischemia is a time-critical emergency: the goal is to restore mesenteric perfusion as fast as possible via emergent revascularization—surgical embolectomy or endovascular thrombectomy/aspiration—paired with aggressive resuscitation, systemic anticoagulation (heparin), broad-spectrum antibiotics, and urgent surgical evaluation for resection of any nonviable bowel (and a possible second-look laparotomy). Every hour of delay increases transmural infarction and mortality. Outpatient management or a 48-hour repeat scan would be fatal. Systemic thrombolysis alone is not definitive here, and discharge is absurd. Observation forfeits the narrow window before infarction. Colonoscopy has no role and is dangerous in ischemic bowel. Recognize, resuscitate, anticoagulate, give antibiotics, and revascularize emergently.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful revascularization and recovery, which long-term measure most directly addresses the underlying cause of his embolic event?
options: Lifelong proton pump inhibitor therapy
Therapeutic anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation with adherence counseling
Daily aspirin alone with no anticoagulation
Routine surveillance colonoscopy every year
Long-term antibiotic prophylaxis
stages.explanation: His embolus arose from atrial fibrillation, and the most direct preventive measure is reliable therapeutic anticoagulation (e.g., a direct oral anticoagulant or warfarin) with explicit adherence counseling, since his nonadherence to apixaban precipitated the event. Stroke/systemic-embolism risk in AF mandates this. A proton pump inhibitor does not address embolic risk. Aspirin alone is inferior to anticoagulation for cardioembolic prevention in AF and would leave him underprotected. Annual surveillance colonoscopy is unrelated to embolism prevention. Long-term antibiotic prophylaxis has no role. Optimizing rate/rhythm control and cardiovascular risk factors (statin, blood pressure) is supportive, but adherent anticoagulation is the key intervention to prevent recurrent mesenteric or systemic emboli.

cases.diagnosis: Acute mesenteric ischemia from a superior mesenteric artery embolus (cardioembolic, atrial fibrillation)
pearls: Pain out of proportion to exam in an older patient with atrial fibrillation is acute mesenteric ischemia until proven otherwise.
CT angiography is the confirmatory test; lactate and acidosis are supportive but often late findings.
Treatment is time-critical: resuscitate, anticoagulate, give antibiotics, and revascularize emergently, resecting nonviable bowel.
After a cardioembolic event, adherent therapeutic anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation is the key to preventing recurrence.

cases.title: Right Upper Belly Pain After a Fatty Meal
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe right upper abdominal pain for the past eight hours, worse after eating.
cases.history: A 48-year-old woman presents with eight hours of constant, severe right upper quadrant pain that began after a fatty dinner and radiates to the right scapula. She has nausea and vomited twice. She reports several prior episodes of shorter, self-limited right upper quadrant pain after fatty meals over the past year, but none lasting this long. She has not had fevers until today. Her history includes obesity and two prior pregnancies. She takes no regular medications other than an oral multivitamin and has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is notable for gallstones in her mother. Review of systems is positive for nausea, anorexia, and a subjective fever today and negative for jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, chest pain, dysuria, and vaginal symptoms. Her last menstrual period was three weeks ago and a pregnancy is not suspected. She denies prior abdominal surgery and recent travel.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable and lying still. The abdomen is soft but markedly tender in the right upper quadrant with voluntary guarding. There is a positive Murphy sign—inspiratory arrest with deep right subcostal palpation. There is no rebound or rigidity elsewhere and no palpable mass other than guarded tenderness. There is no scleral icterus or jaundice. Bowel sounds are normal. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. Skin is warm without rash, and there is no peripheral edema. The remainder of the abdomen is nontender.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given postprandial RUQ pain, fever, and a positive Murphy sign, which is the most appropriate first imaging study?
options: Right upper quadrant abdominal ultrasound
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast
MRCP
HIDA (cholescintigraphy) scan first-line
Upright chest radiograph
stages.explanation: Right upper quadrant ultrasound is the best initial imaging test for suspected acute cholecystitis: it is sensitive for gallstones, fast, radiation-free, and can demonstrate cholecystitis findings (gallbladder wall thickening >3 mm, pericholecystic fluid, gallstones, and a sonographic Murphy sign). CT is less sensitive for stones and is reserved for evaluating complications or alternative diagnoses. MRCP excels at characterizing the bile ducts/choledocholithiasis but is not the first step for uncomplicated cholecystitis. A HIDA scan is the most specific test for acute cholecystitis (nonvisualization of the gallbladder indicates cystic duct obstruction) and is used when the ultrasound is equivocal—not first-line. An upright chest film screens for free air/pneumonia but does not diagnose cholecystitis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.6 ×10³/µL with left shift
Total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 96 U/L
AST 30 U/L, ALT 28 U/L
Lipase 35 U/L (normal)
RUQ ultrasound: gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening 5 mm, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy sign; common bile duct 4 mm (normal caliber)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With gallstones, a thickened gallbladder wall, pericholecystic fluid, and a normal common bile duct with normal bilirubin and transaminases, what is the diagnosis?
options: Choledocholithiasis with biliary obstruction
Acute calculous cholecystitis without ductal obstruction
Ascending cholangitis
Acute pancreatitis
Symptomatic cholelithiasis (biliary colic) only
stages.explanation: The combination of gallstones, a thickened gallbladder wall, pericholecystic fluid, and a sonographic Murphy sign—plus fever and leukocytosis—defines acute calculous cholecystitis. A normal-caliber common bile duct with normal bilirubin and transaminases argues against ductal obstruction. Choledocholithiasis would typically show a dilated CBD and elevated bilirubin/alkaline phosphatase. Ascending cholangitis requires evidence of biliary obstruction with infection (e.g., Charcot triad: fever, jaundice, RUQ pain), and she has no jaundice or ductal dilation. Acute pancreatitis would feature epigastric/back pain with elevated lipase, which is normal here. Simple biliary colic is transient pain without inflammation, fever, leukocytosis, or wall thickening—her sustained pain and inflammatory findings exceed that.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is diagnosed with acute calculous cholecystitis. What is the most appropriate definitive management for this otherwise healthy patient?
options: Discharge on oral antibiotics with elective surgery in 3 months
IV fluids, analgesia, antibiotics, and early laparoscopic cholecystectomy during this admission
Percutaneous cholecystostomy tube as first-line therapy
Ursodeoxycholic acid to dissolve the stones
ERCP with sphincterotomy
stages.explanation: For acute calculous cholecystitis in a good surgical candidate, the standard of care is supportive care (IV fluids, analgesia, antiemetics, antibiotics) followed by early laparoscopic cholecystectomy during the same admission—ideally within about 72 hours of symptom onset—which lowers complications and shortens overall recovery compared with delayed surgery. Discharging for elective surgery months later risks recurrence and complications. Percutaneous cholecystostomy is reserved for poor surgical candidates or the critically ill who cannot tolerate the operation, not a healthy patient. Ursodeoxycholic acid is slow, often ineffective, and not appropriate for acute inflammation. ERCP addresses ductal stones (choledocholithiasis/cholangitis), which she does not have given a normal duct and labs.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which feature, if it developed, would most suggest a complication requiring escalation beyond routine cholecystectomy?
options: Resolution of pain after analgesia
Crepitus on imaging indicating gas in the gallbladder wall (emphysematous cholecystitis)
A single episode of vomiting
Transient mild tachycardia from pain
A normal common bile duct on repeat imaging
stages.explanation: Gas within the gallbladder wall on imaging indicates emphysematous cholecystitis, a gangrenous, gas-forming infection (often by clostridial or other gas-producing organisms, classically in diabetics) that carries a high risk of perforation and sepsis and demands urgent intervention and broad antimicrobial coverage. This is a true complication requiring escalation. Pain relief with analgesia, a single emesis, and pain-related tachycardia are expected, nonalarming findings. A normal common bile duct is reassuring and argues against ductal obstruction rather than indicating a complication. Other escalation triggers include gangrenous/perforated cholecystitis and gallbladder empyema—each shifting management toward more urgent surgery and potentially broader resuscitation.

cases.diagnosis: Acute calculous cholecystitis
pearls: A positive Murphy sign with postprandial RUQ pain and fever suggests acute cholecystitis; RUQ ultrasound is the first-line test.
Diagnostic ultrasound findings include gallstones, wall thickening >3 mm, pericholecystic fluid, and a sonographic Murphy sign; a normal CBD/bilirubin argues against ductal obstruction.
Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (ideally within 72 hours) plus antibiotics is the treatment of choice in good surgical candidates.
Percutaneous cholecystostomy is reserved for poor surgical candidates; gas in the gallbladder wall signals emphysematous cholecystitis needing urgent care.

cases.title: Fever, Jaundice, and Belly Pain Turning to Low Blood Pressure
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of fever, yellowing skin, and right upper abdominal pain, now with new confusion and falling blood pressure.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man admitted to the medical ward for evaluation of jaundice develops worsening fever, rigors, and right upper quadrant pain over two days. Today the nurse notes he is intermittently confused and his blood pressure has dropped. He has a history of gallstones and a prior episode of biliary colic; he was scheduled for outpatient evaluation of intermittent jaundice. He has type 2 diabetes (metformin 1,000 mg twice daily) and hypertension (amlodipine 5 mg daily). He has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke and rarely drinks alcohol. Family history is unremarkable. Review of systems, when he is lucid, is positive for dark urine, pale stools, anorexia, and rigors and negative for prior biliary surgery, hematemesis, and chest pain. Collateral from his wife notes he has seemed increasingly drowsy through the day. He has not had recent ERCP or biliary instrumentation.
cases.exam: The patient is ill-appearing, drowsy, and intermittently disoriented to time and place. He has obvious scleral icterus and jaundiced skin. The abdomen is soft with marked right upper quadrant tenderness and voluntary guarding but no rigidity. There is no rebound elsewhere and no palpable mass. He is warm and flushed peripherally with delayed capillary refill and mottled knees. Mucous membranes are dry. Cardiopulmonary exam reveals tachycardia with clear lungs. There is no asterixis attributable to liver failure on brief exam. Skin shows jaundice without petechiae. He is oliguric per nursing report.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: This patient has fever, jaundice, and RUQ pain (Charcot triad) now with hypotension and confusion (Reynolds pentad). What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute cholecystitis
Ascending (acute) cholangitis with septic shock
Acute viral hepatitis
Hepatic abscess
Acute pancreatitis
stages.explanation: Charcot triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) indicates ascending cholangitis—infection of an obstructed biliary tree—and the addition of hypotension and altered mental status completes Reynolds pentad, signaling suppurative cholangitis with septic shock. His gallstone history and obstructive jaundice (dark urine, pale stools) fit a stone obstructing the common bile duct with superimposed infection. Acute cholecystitis causes fever and RUQ pain but not the prominent jaundice/ductal obstruction and shock seen here. Acute viral hepatitis causes hepatocellular injury without this septic, obstructive picture. A hepatic abscess can cause fever and RUQ pain but is less consistent with the classic obstructive jaundice and rapid septic deterioration. Pancreatitis presents with epigastric/back pain and elevated lipase, not this pentad.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 19.8 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Total bilirubin 7.6 mg/dL (direct 6.0)
Alkaline phosphatase 410 U/L
AST 120, ALT 140 U/L
Lactate 4.5 mmol/L
Cr 2.0 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
Lipase 50 U/L (normal)
RUQ ultrasound: dilated common bile duct (12 mm) with a distal stone; intrahepatic ductal dilation

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Labs confirm an obstructed, infected biliary system with septic shock. What is the most appropriate immediate management before definitive biliary drainage?
options: Immediate ERCP without resuscitation or antibiotics
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation and prompt broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, with blood cultures
Oral antibiotics and observation overnight
Laparoscopic cholecystectomy as the first procedure
Antibiotics alone without any plan for biliary drainage
stages.explanation: Cholangitis with septic shock requires immediate sepsis resuscitation: IV crystalloid fluids for hypotension, prompt broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering enteric gram-negatives and anaerobes, and blood cultures—initiated without delay while arranging urgent biliary decompression. ERCP is the definitive decompressive step, but the patient must first be resuscitated and started on antibiotics (and proceeding to ERCP in a hypotensive, unstabilized patient without antimicrobials is unsafe). Oral antibiotics and overnight observation are grossly inadequate for septic shock. Cholecystectomy does not relieve the obstructing common bile duct stone and is not the first procedure in acute cholangitis. Antibiotics alone, without drainage, will fail because the infected obstruction must be relieved—source control is essential.

stages.label: RESPONSE TO RESUSCITATION
stages.content:
After 2 L crystalloid and IV antibiotics: BP 96/58 mmHg, HR 108 bpm
Lactate improving to 3.1 mmol/L
Still jaundiced; bilirubin unchanged
Ultrasound confirms persistent CBD obstruction by stone
Vasopressor requirement minimal; patient more arousable

stages.label: DEFINITIVE THERAPY
stages.question: After initial resuscitation and antibiotics, what is the most appropriate definitive intervention for the biliary obstruction?
options: Continue antibiotics alone and reassess in 72 hours
Urgent biliary decompression by ERCP (with stone extraction/sphincterotomy or stent)
Elective ERCP in 2 weeks
Immediate open common bile duct exploration as first-line
Percutaneous liver biopsy
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment of acute cholangitis is urgent biliary decompression, and ERCP—with sphincterotomy and stone extraction, and/or stent placement—is the preferred method because it both relieves the obstruction and provides source control. In severe/suppurative cholangitis, drainage should be performed urgently (typically within ~24 hours) once the patient is sufficiently resuscitated. Continuing antibiotics alone fails to remove the obstructing stone and risks ongoing sepsis. Waiting two weeks is unacceptable for an infected, obstructed system. Open CBD exploration or percutaneous transhepatic drainage are reserved for when ERCP is unavailable or unsuccessful, not first-line. Percutaneous liver biopsy has no role and is dangerous in this setting. Resuscitate, give antibiotics, then decompress promptly with ERCP.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful ERCP with stone extraction and clinical recovery, which is the most appropriate plan to prevent recurrence?
options: No further intervention since the duct is now clear
Interval (often same-admission or near-term) laparoscopic cholecystectomy once recovered
Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics
Repeat ERCP every month indefinitely
Ursodeoxycholic acid as definitive prevention
stages.explanation: After ERCP clears the duct in gallstone-related cholangitis, the gallbladder remains the source of stones, so cholecystectomy (typically laparoscopic) is recommended once the patient recovers—often during the same admission or shortly thereafter—to prevent recurrent biliary obstruction, cholangitis, and cholecystitis. Doing nothing leaves the stone reservoir in place with a high recurrence risk. Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics do not address the mechanical source and promote resistance. Routine monthly ERCP is unnecessary, invasive, and carries its own risks. Ursodeoxycholic acid is not reliable definitive prevention for a patient who has already developed obstructing stones and cholangitis. Definitive prevention is removal of the gallbladder.

cases.diagnosis: Ascending (acute) cholangitis with septic shock due to choledocholithiasis
pearls: Charcot triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) suggests cholangitis; adding hypotension and confusion (Reynolds pentad) indicates suppurative cholangitis with shock.
Resuscitate and start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics with blood cultures immediately—then arrange urgent biliary drainage.
ERCP with sphincterotomy/stone extraction or stenting is the definitive decompressive therapy; antibiotics alone are insufficient without source control.
After recovery from gallstone cholangitis, perform interval cholecystectomy to prevent recurrence.

cases.title: Sudden Flank Pain Shooting to the Groin
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe right flank pain radiating to the groin with blood in the urine.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man presents with three hours of sudden-onset, severe, colicky right flank pain that radiates to the right groin and testicle. The pain comes in waves and he is unable to find a comfortable position, pacing and writhing. He has associated nausea and vomited once. He noticed pink-tinged urine this morning. He has had one similar but milder episode two years ago that resolved spontaneously. He drinks little water through his workday and consumes several caffeinated sodas daily. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is notable for kidney stones in his father. Review of systems is positive for urinary urgency and hematuria and negative for fever, dysuria with discharge, prior urinary tract infections, recent trauma, and prior abdominal surgery. He works outdoors in a warm climate and reports significant sweating without consistent rehydration.
cases.exam: The patient is restless and writhing, unable to lie still, in contrast to the still posture of peritonitis. There is marked right costovertebral angle tenderness. The abdomen is soft and nondistended with no rebound, guarding, or palpable mass; bowel sounds are normal. The right testicle is normal in lie and size, nontender, with a normal cremasteric reflex and no swelling, arguing against testicular torsion. There is no inguinal hernia. The patient is afebrile and well-perfused. Skin is warm and dry without rash. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. Urethral meatus is normal without discharge.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given colicky flank-to-groin pain with hematuria and restlessness, which is the most appropriate imaging test to confirm a stone and define its size and location?
options: Non-contrast helical CT of the abdomen and pelvis (CT KUB)
Plain abdominal radiograph (KUB) alone
Contrast-enhanced CT urogram first-line
MRI of the abdomen
Voiding cystourethrogram
stages.explanation: Non-contrast helical CT (CT KUB) is the gold-standard test for suspected nephrolithiasis: it detects virtually all stones regardless of composition, precisely localizes them, measures size, and assesses for hydronephrosis—all without contrast. These features directly guide management thresholds. A plain KUB radiograph misses radiolucent (e.g., uric acid) stones and small calculi and underestimates obstruction. A contrast CT urogram is reserved for specific situations (e.g., evaluating the collecting system or suspected tumor) and is not first-line for acute colic. MRI is poor at imaging calcified stones. A voiding cystourethrogram evaluates vesicoureteral reflux, not acute stones. (Point-of-care ultrasound is a reasonable radiation-sparing alternative, especially in young patients or pregnancy.)

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: moderate blood, pH 6.0, no nitrites, no leukocyte esterase, no bacteria
WBC 8.9 ×10³/µL
Cr 1.0 mg/dL
Non-contrast CT: 5 mm obstructing calculus in the right distal ureter (at the ureterovesical junction)
Mild right hydronephrosis; no abscess; left kidney normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: CT shows a 5 mm distal ureteral stone with mild hydronephrosis, no fever, and normal renal function. What does the stone size predict about likely management?
options: Stones this size virtually never pass and require immediate surgery
A 5 mm distal stone has a good chance of passing spontaneously, so a trial of conservative management is appropriate
The stone must be lithotripsied today regardless of symptoms
Mild hydronephrosis with a small stone mandates emergent percutaneous nephrostomy
A 5 mm stone always requires immediate ureteral stenting
stages.explanation: Stone size strongly predicts spontaneous passage: most stones smaller than ~5 mm pass on their own, and many distal stones up to about 6 mm will pass with conservative management. A 5 mm distal ureteral stone in an afebrile patient with preserved renal function and controllable pain is therefore appropriate for a trial of conservative therapy. The claim that 5 mm stones 'never pass' is false. Immediate lithotripsy or mandatory stenting is not required for a stone of this size that can be managed expectantly. Emergent nephrostomy is reserved for obstruction with infection (obstructed infected system) or refractory cases, not mild hydronephrosis without infection. Larger stones (>~10 mm) are far less likely to pass and more often need urologic intervention.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this 5 mm distal stone managed expectantly, which outpatient plan is most appropriate?
options: Strict fluid restriction and bed rest
Analgesia (NSAIDs first-line if no contraindication), antiemetics, oral hydration, an alpha-blocker for medical expulsive therapy, urine straining, and urology follow-up
Empiric antibiotics for all stone patients
Immediate surgical stone removal as the default
Opioids only, with no follow-up plan
stages.explanation: Conservative management of a passable stone centers on pain control (NSAIDs are first-line and effective for renal colic when not contraindicated, with opioids as needed), antiemetics, adequate hydration, urine straining to capture the stone for analysis, and a return-precaution plus urology follow-up if it fails to pass. An alpha-blocker (medical expulsive therapy) can facilitate passage of distal ureteral stones. Fluid restriction is counterproductive—patients should be hydrated. Routine antibiotics are not indicated without evidence of infection. Default immediate surgery is unnecessary for a stone likely to pass. Opioids alone without antiemetics, expulsive therapy, straining, or follow-up is inadequate care. Patients should also receive clear return precautions for fever or intractable symptoms.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which development during the conservative trial would most clearly warrant urgent urologic intervention rather than continued expectant management?
options: Intermittent mild pain controlled with oral NSAIDs
Passage of the stone with resolution of pain
Fever with signs of infection plus the obstructing stone (obstructed, infected system)
Trace hematuria persisting for a day
Mild nausea that responds to antiemetics
stages.explanation: An obstructing stone with superimposed infection (fever, leukocytosis, pyuria/positive urine cultures) constitutes an obstructed, infected collecting system—a urologic emergency requiring urgent decompression (ureteral stent or percutaneous nephrostomy) plus antibiotics, because pus under pressure can rapidly progress to urosepsis. Other indications for intervention include acute kidney injury (especially with a solitary or transplant kidney or bilateral obstruction) and intractable pain/vomiting or failure to pass. Well-controlled intermittent pain, stone passage with relief, transient trace hematuria, and antiemetic-responsive nausea are all expected and do not require escalation. The key red flag is fever/infection in the setting of obstruction—do not delay decompression in that scenario.

cases.diagnosis: Nephrolithiasis (obstructing 5 mm distal ureteral stone)
pearls: Colicky flank-to-groin pain with hematuria and a writhing, restless patient is classic for ureteral stone; non-contrast CT is the gold-standard test.
Stone size predicts passage: most stones <5 mm pass spontaneously; larger stones (>~10 mm) often need intervention.
Manage passable stones with NSAID analgesia, hydration, an alpha-blocker (medical expulsive therapy), urine straining, and urology follow-up.
An obstructed, infected system (fever plus obstruction) is an emergency requiring urgent decompression and antibiotics.
